topics.title: Overview of Special Purpose Machines - Definition, Scope, and Role in Automation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT601 - SPECIAL PURPOSE MACHINES FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Special Purpose Machines - Definition, Scope, and Role in Automation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Purpose Machine (SPM):** A Special Purpose Machine is a *custom-engineered, multi-station, automated production system* designed and built to perform a **specific manufacturing operation** (or a limited set of related operations) on a **predefined component or part family** with extremely high efficiency, repeatability, and minimal human intervention.

The term *Special Purpose* implies that the machine is **not a general-purpose machine tool** (like a conventional lathe, milling machine, or drill). Instead, it is **purpose-built** to execute one specific task — such as drilling, tapping, milling, boring, or honing — on a particular workpiece geometry, often at cycle times measured in **seconds**.

**Standard KTU Definition (Board-Accepted Form):**
> *“A Special Purpose Machine (SPM) is a high-production, semi or fully automated machine tool designed and dedicated to manufacture a specific part or to perform a specific operation at a high rate of production with high accuracy and consistent quality.”*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The “Cookie Cutter” of Manufacturing

Imagine a bakery. A *general-purpose kitchen knife* can cut bread, vegetables, fruits, and meat — it is **versatile but slow** for any single job. Now imagine a **factory press that stamps out 10,000 identical cookies per hour** from dough poured into a conveyor belt. The press:
- Does **only one thing** (stamp cookies),
- Does it **extremely fast**,
- Needs **no human decision-making** per cycle,
- Cannot be re-tasked easily to make pasta.

That stamping press is the **SPM** of the bakery world. In manufacturing, the SPM is the *mechanized equivalent* of that cookie press — engineered for **maximum throughput on one specific part**.

| Feature | General Purpose Machine (GPM) | Special Purpose Machine (SPM) |
|---|---|---|
| Flexibility | High — many operations | Low — one specific operation |
| Setup Time | Long | Minimal / pre-set |
| Cycle Time | Slower | Extremely fast (seconds) |
| Cost | Lower (mass produced) | Higher (custom engineered) |
| Operator Skill | High | Low / semi-skilled |
| Production Volume | Low to medium | High to very high |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics used in SPM Design (Bolded Standard KTU Parameters):**
> - **Cycle Time (T_c):** Time to complete one full machining sequence (typically **5–60 seconds**).
> - **Productivity (P):** Parts produced per shift (often **> 1000 parts/shift**).
> - **Tool Life (T):** Number of parts produced per tool change.
> - **Machine Availability (A):** Uptime percentage, target **> 90%**.
> - **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE):** Combined metric of availability × performance × quality (target **> 85%**).

### 1.3 Why SPMs Exist — The Manufacturing Need

In mass production industries such as **Automobile, White Goods (washing machines, refrigerators), Pumps, Valves, and Aerospace sub-assemblies**, the same component is manufactured in **lakhs of units per year**. A general-purpose machine, even with a skilled operator, cannot achieve the required:
- **Dimensional accuracy** (tolerances of **±0.01 mm** or tighter),
- **Production rate** (e.g., one engine block per **45 seconds**),
- **Cost per piece** (labor cost must be near zero),
- **Consistent quality** (zero-defect philosophy).

The SPM bridges this gap by **mechanizing, automating, and integrating** the operation into a single machine frame.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Special Purpose Machines (SPM)

KTU 2024 Scheme recognises the following structural classification:

### A. Based on Degree of Automation
1. **Semi-Automatic SPMs:** Operator loads/unloads; machine performs the cycle automatically. Common in *low to medium volume* production.
2. **Fully Automatic SPMs:** Loading, machining, unloading — all automated. Used in *high volume* production lines.
3. **CNC-Based SPMs:** Re-programmable for part variants within a family. A *hybrid* of flexibility and purpose-built structure.

### B. Based on Construction / Configuration
1. **Rotary Table SPM (Indexing Type):** A horizontal or vertical rotary table indexes the workpiece through multiple stations (typically **4, 6, 8, 12, or 16 stations**). Each station performs a different operation simultaneously → maximum throughput.
2. **Inline / Trunnion SPM (Linear Transfer Type):** Workpieces move in a straight line through workstations via a chain conveyor or power-and-free system.
3. **Dial Indexing SPM:** Smaller version with 3–6 stations, used for smaller parts like bearings, rings.
4. **Plunger / Reciprocating SPM:** A linear plunger advances tooling into a stationary workpiece. Used for simple drilling/tapping on a single face.
5. **Multi-Spindle SPM:** Several spindles act in parallel on the same workpiece (e.g., drilling all holes of a flange in one stroke).

### C. Based on Operation Performed
- Drilling SPM, Tapping SPM, Milling SPM, Boring SPM, Honing SPM, Grinding SPM, Assembly SPM, Welding SPM, Leak Testing SPM, etc.

## 2.2 Scope of SPMs in Modern Industry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scope of SPMs (KTU Board Favourite Answer):** SPMs find application wherever a **dedicated, high-volume, repeatable operation** must be performed with **consistent quality and minimum human intervention**. The scope extends from traditional metal cutting to non-traditional machining and modern Industry 4.0-integrated cells.

**Industries where SPMs are indispensable:**
- **Automotive:** Engine block machining, cylinder head drilling, gearbox housing.
- **White Goods:** Washing machine tub flanges, refrigerator compressor housings.
- **Fluid Power:** Pump bodies, valve bodies, hydraulic manifold blocks.
- **Aerospace:** Structural components, landing gear sub-assemblies.
- **Electronics & Electrical:** Motor laminations, switchgear components.
- **Medical Devices:** Surgical instrument bodies, implants (high precision).

## 2.3 Role of SPMs in Automation

The role of SPMs in **Factory Automation** can be understood through the **Automation Pyramid (ISA-95 / KTU Hierarchy)**:

```
       ┌──────────────────────┐
       │   Level 4: ERP       │  (Enterprise Resource Planning)
       ├──────────────────────┤
       │   Level 3: MES       │  (Manufacturing Execution System)
       ├──────────────────────┤
       │   Level 2: SCADA/HMI │  ← SPMs are controlled at this level
       ├──────────────────────┤
       │   Level 1: PLC/CNC   │  ← SPMs operate at this level
       ├──────────────────────┤
       │   Level 0: Field     │  (Sensors, Actuators, Motors) ← SPM hardware
       └──────────────────────┘
```

**Specific Roles:**
1. **Cyclic Automation (Mechanization):** Replace manual repetitive operations.
2. **Quality Assurance:** Consistent quality through rigid mechanical repeatability.
3. **Productivity Enhancement:** Reduce cycle time, increase output.
4. **Cost Reduction:** Lower labor cost, lower rejection rate.
5. **Integration with CIM (Computer Integrated Manufacturing):** SPMs are the *physical layer* of CIM systems.
6. **Enabling Just-In-Time (JIT) Production:** Predictable cycle times support lean manufacturing.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cycle Time (T_c)** | $T_c = T_{setup} + n \cdot T_{op}$ | Setup time + (operations × operation time) | seconds |
| 2 | **Production Rate (R_p)** | $R_p = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | Parts produced per hour | parts/hour |
| 3 | **Number of Stations (n)** | $n = \dfrac{T_{longest}}{T_{index}}$ | Determined by longest operation vs index time | stations |
| 4 | **Machine Utilization (U)** | $U = \dfrac{T_{machining}}{T_c}$ | Machining time to cycle time ratio | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)** | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | Availability × Performance × Quality | % (target ≥ 85%) |
| 6 | **Return on Investment (Simple)** | $ROI = \dfrac{Savings}{Cost} \times 100$ | Justifies SPM purchase | % |
| 7 | **Takt Time** | $T_{takt} = \dfrac{Available\ Time}{Customer\ Demand}$ | Production pacing time | seconds/part |
| 8 | **Number of Spindles for Parallelism** | $N_{sp} = \dfrac{Number\ of\ Holes}{Stations}$ | Distributing holes across spindles | integer |
| 9 | **Index Time (Rotary SPM)** | $T_{index} = \dfrac{60}{\omega \cdot Z}$ | Depends on angular velocity and stations | seconds |
| 10 | **Power per Spindle (P_s)** | $P_s = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \times \eta}$ | Force × cutting speed / efficiency | kW |

> [!WARNING]
> **Absolute Value Convention:** In the formulas above, vertical bars like $|x|$ are written as $\vert x \vert$ in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` inside tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The **economic threshold** for choosing an SPM over a GPM can be calculated as the **Break-Even Quantity (BEO)**:

$$
BEO = \dfrac{C_{SPM} - C_{GPM}}{C_{GPM/unit} - C_{SPM/unit}}
$$

Where:
- $C_{SPM}$ = Total cost of the special purpose machine,
- $C_{GPM}$ = Total cost of general purpose machine(s),
- $C_{GPM/unit}$ = Per-unit cost using GPM (high labor),
- $C_{SPM/unit}$ = Per-unit cost using SPM (low labor).

> Below the break-even quantity, a GPM is more economical; above it, the SPM is justified. This is a standard interview and KTU viva question.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Production Rate of a Rotary Indexing SPM

**Problem Setup:**
A rotary indexing SPM has **6 stations**. The longest operation among all stations takes **8 seconds**, and the index time (time taken for the table to rotate one station) is **2 seconds**. Calculate the actual production rate and identify the bottleneck station.

**Step 1 — Identify the Governing Time**

The cycle time of a rotary SPM is governed by the **slowest station**, because the table can only index after the slowest operation finishes. Therefore:

$$
T_c = \max(T_{op,1}, T_{op,2}, \ldots, T_{op,n}) + T_{index}
$$

This is the fundamental rule for any **multi-station parallel system**.

**Step 2 — Substitute Known Values**

The longest operation time is given as **8 seconds**, and the index time is **2 seconds**.

$$
T_c = 8 + 2 = 10 \text{ seconds/part}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Production Rate (Parts per Hour)**

We know that 1 hour = 3600 seconds. The production rate is the reciprocal of cycle time scaled to one hour:

$$
R_p = \dfrac{3600}{T_c} = \dfrac{3600}{10} = 360 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Production Rate per Shift (8 hours)**

$$
R_{shift} = R_p \times \text{Shift hours} = 360 \times 8 = 2880 \text{ parts/shift}
$$

**Step 5 — Daily Production (2 shifts/day)**

$$
R_{day} = 2880 \times 2 = 5760 \text{ parts/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify the Bottleneck**

The operation with the longest time (8 seconds) is the **bottleneck**. To balance the line, KTU recommends either:
- Splitting the bottleneck operation into 2 stations, OR
- Using 2 parallel spindles on the same station.

If split into 2 equal stations, new op time = $8 / 2 = 4$ seconds. The new cycle time becomes:

$$
T_c^{new} = 4 + 2 = 6 \text{ seconds} \Rightarrow R_p^{new} = 600 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

This is a **40% productivity gain** purely through line balancing.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) Calculation

**Problem Setup:**
A SPM operates for **2 shifts of 8 hours each** (16 hours/day). The shift is **available for production for 14 hours** (1 hr planned maintenance, 1 hr changeover). The machine is designed to produce **500 parts/hour**, but actually produces **420 parts/hour**. Out of 6000 parts produced in a week, **60 are rejected**.

**Step 1 — Compute Availability (A)**

$$
A = \dfrac{Operating\ Time}{Planned\ Production\ Time} = \dfrac{14}{16} = 0.875 = 87.5\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Performance (P)**

$$
P = \dfrac{Actual\ Output}{Theoretical\ Output} = \dfrac{420}{500} = 0.840 = 84.0\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Quality (Q)**

$$
Q = \dfrac{Good\ Parts}{Total\ Parts} = \dfrac{6000 - 60}{6000} = \dfrac{5940}{6000} = 0.990 = 99.0\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute OEE**

$$
OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.875 \times 0.840 \times 0.990
$$

$$
OEE = 0.72765 = 72.77\%
$$

**Step 5 — World-Class Benchmark Comparison**

> [!NOTE]
> **World-Class OEE = 85% (Lean Manufacturing Standard).**
> This SPM is **below world-class**, primarily because of low *Performance* (84%) — indicating small stoppages, idling, or reduced running speed. KTU expects students to identify this and suggest corrective actions such as SMED (Single Minute Exchange of Die), TPM (Total Productive Maintenance), or root cause analysis of micro-stops.

**Step 6 — Six Big Losses Categorisation**

| Loss Category | Affected Factor | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Breakdown | Availability | Motor failure |
| Setup/Adjustment | Availability | Tool change |
| Small Stops | Performance | Sensor glitch |
| Reduced Speed | Performance | Coolant starvation |
| Startup Rejects | Quality | First-piece defects |
| Production Rejects | Quality | Tool wear |

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Break-Even Analysis for SPM Justification

**Problem Setup:**
A company needs to drill **3 holes** in a flange. With a GPM, each hole takes **45 seconds** plus **20 seconds handling** → **155 seconds/part**. Operator wage is ₹**50/hour**. With a proposed SPM, cycle time will be **30 seconds/part**, no operator needed (but the machine consumes **5 kW** at ₹**8/kWh**, used 80% of cycle time). SPM cost is ₹**8,00,000**, GPM cost is ₹**1,50,000**. Find the break-even quantity.

**Step 1 — Per-Unit Cost with GPM**

Labour cost per part:

$$
C_{labour/part} = \dfrac{50}{3600} \times 155 = 2.15 \text{ ₹/part}
$$

Maintenance + depreciation (assume 10% of GPM cost spread over **50,000 parts**):

$$
C_{GPM/unit} = 2.15 + \dfrac{1,50,000 \times 0.10}{50,000} = 2.15 + 0.30 = 2.45 \text{ ₹/part}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-Unit Cost with SPM**

Energy cost per part (cycle = 30 s, machine ON for 80% = 24 s):

$$
C_{energy/part} = \dfrac{5 \times 8}{3600} \times 24 = 0.267 \text{ ₹/part}
$$

Depreciation (assume 10% of SPM cost over **2,00,000 parts**):

$$
C_{deprn/part} = \dfrac{8,00,000 \times 0.10}{2,00,000} = 0.40 \text{ ₹/part}
$$

Total:

$$
C_{SPM/unit} = 0.267 + 0.40 = 0.667 \text{ ₹/part}
$$

**Step 3 — Break-Even Quantity**

$$
BEO = \dfrac{C_{SPM} - C_{GPM}}{C_{GPM/unit} - C_{SPM/unit}} = \dfrac{8,00,000 - 1,50,000}{2.45 - 0.667}
$$

$$
BEO = \dfrac{6,50,000}{1.783} \approx 3,64,532 \text{ parts}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation**

> If annual demand exceeds ~3.65 lakh parts, the SPM is economically justified. If the part is used in a mass-produced car (e.g., 5 lakh cars/year × 4 flanges = 20 lakh flanges), the SPM gives a **massive per-part saving** of ₹**1.78**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of a Rotary Indexing SPM (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[Station 1: Load and Clamp]
    L2[Station 2: Drill Hole 1 and 2]
    L3[Station 3: Drill Hole 3 and Tapping]
    L4[Station 4: Milling Slot]
    L5[Station 5: Boring and Reaming]
    L6[Station 6: Unload and Inspect]
    IDX[Rotary Indexing Table]
    PLC[Central PLC Controller]
    HMI[Operator HMI Panel]
    HYD[Hydraulic Power Pack]
    LUB[Auto Lubrication Unit]
    CHIP[Chip Conveyor]

    PLC --- HMI
    PLC --- HYD
    PLC --- LUB
    PLC --- CHIP
    IDX --- L1
    IDX --- L2
    IDX --- L3
    IDX --- L4
    IDX --- L5
    IDX --- L6
    L2 --> IDX
    L3 --> IDX
    L4 --> IDX
    L5 --> IDX
```

**Explanation:** The above diagram represents a **6-station rotary indexing SPM** for a typical automotive flange. The indexing table rotates in discrete steps (90° × 4 = full cycle, but with 6 stations, each step = 60°). Each station performs one operation in parallel. The PLC synchronises hydraulic clamping, spindle start/stop, and indexing motion.

## 4.2 Automation Hierarchy of SPM in CIM (Mermaid Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L4[Level 4: Enterprise Planning]
        ERP[ERP System - SAP or Oracle]
    end
    subgraph L3[Level 3: Manufacturing Operations]
        MES[MES - Production Scheduling]
    end
    subgraph L2[Level 2: Supervisory Control]
        SCADA[SCADA / HMI Dashboard]
    end
    subgraph L1[Level 1: Machine Control]
        PLC1[PLC of SPM Line 1]
        PLC2[PLC of SPM Line 2]
        CNC1[CNC of Machining Cell]
    end
    subgraph L0[Level 0: Field Devices]
        SEN1[Proximity Sensors]
        SEN2[Pressure Transducers]
        ACT1[Hydraulic Cylinders]
        ACT2[AC Servo Motors]
        VFD[Variable Frequency Drives]
    end

    ERP --> MES
    MES --> SCADA
    SCADA --> PLC1
    SCADA --> PLC2
    SCADA --> CNC1
    PLC1 --> SEN1
    PLC1 --> ACT1
    PLC2 --> SEN2
    PLC2 --> ACT2
    CNC1 --> VFD
```

**Explanation:** The SPM sits at **Level 1** of the CIM pyramid. It receives high-level production orders from the ERP (Level 4), executes the machining cycle autonomously, and reports status (parts produced, alarms, tool life) up to SCADA/MES. This is the exact role of an SPM in **Industry 4.0 / Smart Factory** environments.

## 4.3 Functional Flow of a Single Operation in SPM

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> PartLoad: Operator places blank
    PartLoad --> Clamp: Foot switch pressed
    Clamp --> SpindleStart: Pressure switch confirms clamp
    SpindleStart --> CoolantOn: Coolant flow switch confirms
    CoolantOn --> RapidApproach: Z-axis rapid to clearance
    RapidApproach --> FeedDrill: Z-axis feed at cutting speed
    FeedDrill --> DrillAtDepth: Reach target depth
    DrillAtDepth --> RapidRetract: Z-axis rapid up
    RapidRetract --> CoolantOff: Cycle complete signal
    CoolantOff --> SpindleStop: Spindle halt
    SpindleStop --> Unclamp: Limit switch confirms
    Unclamp --> Idle: Cycle restart ready
    Idle --> [*]: End of shift
```

**Explanation:** This state diagram shows the **interlocking logic sequence** of a typical drilling operation in a SPM. Notice that **pressure switch, flow switch, and limit switch** are all interlocked — this is the hallmark of *fail-safe automation* expected from a KTU-quality SPM design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a Special Purpose Machine (SPM). How does it differ from a general purpose machine tool?*

**Model Answer:**
A Special Purpose Machine (SPM) is a custom-built, high-production machine designed to perform a specific manufacturing operation (or a limited set of operations) on a predefined part with high accuracy, repeatability, and minimal human intervention.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | General Purpose Machine (GPM) | Special Purpose Machine (SPM) |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Multipurpose | Single dedicated operation |
| Flexibility | High | Low (specific to one part) |
| Cycle Time | Longer (operator dependent) | Very short (5–60 s) |
| Production Volume | Low to medium | High to very high |
| Operator Skill | High (skilled machinist) | Low (semi-skilled or none) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher (one-time investment) |

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*List any six industries where Special Purpose Machines are extensively used.*

**Model Answer:**
SPMs are extensively used in:
1. Automotive industry (engine blocks, gearboxes, cylinder heads).
2. White goods / consumer durables (washing machine tubs, compressor housings).
3. Fluid power industry (pump bodies, valve bodies, hydraulic manifolds).
4. Aerospace industry (structural components, landing gear sub-assemblies).
5. Electrical and electronics industry (motor laminations, switchgear parts).
6. Medical device industry (surgical instruments, implants, orthopaedic parts).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Explain in detail the **classification of Special Purpose Machines** based on degree of automation, construction, and operation performed. (7 Marks)

*(b)* With a neat sketch, describe the **working principle of a Rotary Indexing SPM** and explain how the cycle time is determined. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Classification of SPMs:**

**1. Based on Degree of Automation:**
- *Semi-Automatic SPMs:* Operator loads/unloads the part; the machine automatically performs the machining cycle. Cycle includes operator intervention time.
- *Fully Automatic SPMs:* Loading, clamping, machining, unloading — all automated through conveyors, robots, or dedicated feeding mechanisms. Suited for mass production.
- *CNC-Based SPMs:* Hybrid machines where the structural rigidity is purpose-built, but the control is CNC-based, allowing part family variants to be machined with minor reprogramming.

**2. Based on Construction / Configuration:**
- *Rotary Table / Indexing SPM:* A horizontal or vertical table carries the workpiece and indexes through 4 to 16 stations, each performing a different operation in parallel. Highest throughput for small to medium parts.
- *Inline / Trunnion SPM:* Workpieces travel in a straight line through workstations on a power-and-free conveyor. Used for larger parts like engine blocks.
- *Dial Indexing SPM:* A compact version with 3–6 stations, used for bearing rings, small cylindrical parts.
- *Plunger / Reciprocating SPM:* A linear plunger advances tooling into a stationary workpiece. Best for single-face operations like drilling/tapping.
- *Multi-Spindle SPM:* Several spindles operate simultaneously on the same workpiece (e.g., drilling 4 holes in one stroke). Cycle time drastically reduced.

**3. Based on Operation Performed:**
- Drilling SPM, Tapping SPM, Milling SPM, Boring SPM, Honing SPM, Grinding SPM, Assembly SPM, Welding SPM, Leak Testing SPM, Marking SPM.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Working Principle of Rotary Indexing SPM:**

A rotary indexing SPM consists of a **circular indexing table** mounted on a heavy cast base. The table is divided into a fixed number of equally spaced workstations (commonly 4, 6, 8, 12, or 16). The workpiece is clamped in a fixture on the table. A **central PLC** controls the sequence:

1. The table indexes (rotates) by one station (e.g., 60° for 6 stations).
2. The workpiece stops precisely under the corresponding tool.
3. The hydraulic/pneumatic clamp engages.
4. The tool spindle starts and performs the assigned operation.
5. The cycle ends, the clamp releases, and the table indexes again.

All stations operate **simultaneously** — while station 1 is being loaded, station 2 is drilling, station 3 is tapping, etc. This *parallelism* is the key advantage of rotary SPMs.

**Cycle Time Determination:**

The cycle time is governed by the **slowest operation** (bottleneck station) plus the **index time**:

$$
T_c = T_{max\ operation} + T_{index}
$$

**Example:** If the longest operation = 12 s, and index time = 3 s:

$$
T_c = 12 + 3 = 15 \text{ s/part}
$$

**Production Rate:**

$$
R_p = \dfrac{3600}{T_c} = \dfrac{3600}{15} = 240 \text{ parts/hour}
$$

For higher productivity, the bottleneck station is split into 2 parallel stations, or 2 spindles are mounted on the same station.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*(a)* Discuss the **role of Special Purpose Machines in Factory Automation** with reference to the Automation Pyramid. (7 Marks)

*(b)* A company is evaluating a SPM to drill **4 holes in an automotive bracket**. With a general purpose drill press, each hole takes **30 seconds**, handling time is **15 seconds/part**, and an operator at ₹**60/hour** is required. The proposed SPM has a **cycle time of 12 seconds/part**, runs on **4 kW power** (used 70% of cycle), and electricity costs ₹**9/kWh**. The SPM costs ₹**6,00,000**, the GPM costs ₹**1,00,000**. Assume 10% depreciation over **2,00,000 parts** for SPM and **50,000 parts** for GPM. **Determine the break-even quantity** and advise the company. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Role of SPMs in Factory Automation:**

SPMs are the *physical execution layer* of any factory automation system. Their role can be understood through the **5-level Automation Pyramid (ISA-95 / KTU Reference Model):**

**Level 4 – Enterprise Level (ERP):** The SPM is identified in the ERP by its part number, capacity, and routing. Production orders flow down from here.

**Level 3 – MES (Manufacturing Execution System):** The SPM reports real-time production data (parts produced, downtime, OEE) up to MES, which schedules and dispatches jobs.

**Level 2 – SCADA / HMI:** Operators monitor SPM status on HMI screens. Trends, alarms, and KPIs are visualised at this level.

**Level 1 – PLC / CNC Control:** This is where the SPM actually operates. A PLC executes the ladder logic, interlocks, and sequence. Sensors and actuators are wired here.

**Level 0 – Field Devices:** The actual mechanical hardware — proximity sensors, pressure switches, hydraulic cylinders, servo motors, VFDs, solenoids, encoders.

**Specific Roles Played by SPMs:**

1. **Cyclic Mechanization:** Replace manual labour for repetitive, hazardous, or ergonomically difficult operations.
2. **Throughput Maximisation:** Achieve production rates unattainable by manual methods (e.g., 1 engine block per 45 s).
3. **Quality Consistency:** Rigid mechanical design + automated gauging = zero-defect philosophy.
4. **Cost Reduction:** Eliminates per-part labour cost, reduces rejection cost.
5. **Lean / JIT Enabler:** Predictable cycle time supports Just-In-Time production with minimal WIP.
6. **Industry 4.0 Integration:** Modern SPMs have IIoT gateways for predictive maintenance, OEE dashboards, and digital twins.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Per-Unit Cost with GPM**

Total cycle time per part with GPM:

$$
T_{GPM} = (4 \times 30) + 15 = 135 \text{ seconds/part}
$$

Labour cost per part:

$$
C_{labour/part} = \dfrac{60}{3600} \times 135 = 2.25 \text{ ₹/part}
$$

Depreciation per part:

$$
C_{deprn, GPM} = \dfrac{1,00,000 \times 0.10}{50,000} = 0.20 \text{ ₹/part}
$$

Total per-unit cost with GPM:

$$
C_{GPM/unit} = 2.25 + 0.20 = 2.45 \text{ ₹/part}
$$

**Step 2 — Per-Unit Cost with SPM**

Cycle time = 12 seconds. Machine ON duration = 70% × 12 = 8.4 s.

Energy cost per part:

$$
C_{energy/part} = \dfrac{4 \times 9}{3600} \times 8.4 = 0.084 \text{ ₹/part}
$$

Depreciation per part:

$$
C_{deprn, SPM} = \dfrac{6,00,000 \times 0.10}{2,00,000} = 0.30 \text{ ₹/part}
$$

Total per-unit cost with SPM:

$$
C_{SPM/unit} = 0.084 + 0.30 = 0.384 \text{ ₹/part}
$$

**Step 3 — Break-Even Quantity**

$$
BEO = \dfrac{C_{SPM} - C_{GPM}}{C_{GPM/unit} - C_{SPM/unit}} = \dfrac{6,00,000 - 1,00,000}{2.45 - 0.384}
$$

$$
BEO = \dfrac{5,00,000}{2.066} \approx 2,42,012 \text{ parts}
$$

**Step 4 — Company Advisory**

> If annual demand exceeds **~2.42 lakh brackets**, the SPM is economically justified. Given the typical automotive production volumes (lakhs of vehicles per year), the SPM should be recommended. Additionally, the SPM offers **consistent quality, lower rejection rate, and operator independence**, which are intangible but significant benefits. The SPM also opens the door to **CIM/Industry 4.0 integration**, enabling future scalability.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Conflating SPM with CNC:** Many students write "SPM is a CNC machine" — this is **wrong**. CNC is a *control type*; SPM is a *machine category*. A SPM *can be* CNC-based, but most are PLC-based with mechanical/electromechanical sequencing.
> 2. **Skipping the Formula for Cycle Time:** When asked about rotary SPM cycle time, students often forget to add the **index time** to the longest operation time. Always write $T_c = T_{op,max} + T_{index}$.
> 3. **OEE Calculation Errors:** Confusing *Availability* (time-based) with *Performance* (output-based). Performance = actual output / theoretical output, **not** operating time.
> 4. **Break-Even Formula Reversal:** Some students write $\frac{C_{GPM} - C_{SPM}}{C_{SPM} - C_{GPM}}$ — the correct form has **capital cost difference in the numerator** and **per-unit cost difference in the denominator**.
> 5. **Not Mentioning Cycle Time Units:** Always specify **seconds** for cycle time and **parts/hour** for production rate. KTU examiners award half-mark units for correct unit notation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 — Quick Recall Sheet:**
> - **SPM** = Special Purpose Machine; **custom-built** for one operation on one part.
> - **Cycle Time Formula:** $T_c = T_{op,max} + T_{index}$ (rotary) or $T_c = \sum T_{op} + T_{aux}$ (inline).
> - **Production Rate:** $R_p = 3600 / T_c$ (parts per hour).
> - **5 Types of SPM by construction:** Rotary, Inline, Dial, Plunger, Multi-Spindle.
> - **3 Types by automation:** Semi-automatic, Fully automatic, CNC-based.
> - **OEE** = A × P × Q; **World-class benchmark ≥ 85%**.
> - **Break-even formula:** $BEO = (C_{SPM} - C_{GPM}) / (C_{GPM/unit} - C_{SPM/unit})$.
> - **SPMs sit at Level 1 (PLC/CNC) of the Automation Pyramid.**
> - **Industries:** Automotive, White Goods, Fluid Power, Aerospace, Electrical, Medical.
> - **Key advantage:** High throughput, consistent quality, low per-part cost, low skill dependency.
> - **Key limitation:** Low flexibility; not suitable for low-volume or frequent design-change jobs.
> - **Six Big Losses of OEE:** Breakdown, Setup, Small Stops, Reduced Speed, Startup Rejects, Production Rejects.
> - **Takt Time vs Cycle Time:** Takt = market demand pace; Cycle = machine's actual pace. SPM cycle time should be ≤ Takt time for line balancing.
> - **Examples to remember:** *Engine block boring SPM* (automotive), *Compressor housing drilling SPM* (white goods), *Manifold block multi-spindle SPM* (fluid power).
> - **SI Units:** Cycle Time [s], Production Rate [parts/h], Power [kW], OEE [%], Cost [₹].

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Special Purpose Machines - Classification Based on Applications (e.g., Assembly Machines, Inspection Machines, Testing Machines)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT601 - SPECIAL PURPOSE MACHINES FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Special Purpose Machines - Classification Based on Applications (e.g., Assembly Machines, Inspection Machines, Testing Machines)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Purpose Machines (SPMs)** are purpose-built, custom-engineered machines designed to perform a specific manufacturing or production operation on a dedicated part or a family of similar parts. When classified **based on applications**, SPMs are grouped by the *type of industrial task* they perform rather than by their kinematic structure or power source.

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups application-based SPMs into three principal categories:

1. **Assembly Machines** — used to join multiple components into a finished sub-assembly or product.
2. **Inspection Machines** — used to verify dimensional, geometric, or visual conformance of components.
3. **Testing Machines** — used to evaluate functional, mechanical, or endurance performance of components.

Supplementary application-based categories frequently examined include **Material Handling Machines**, **Processing Machines** (welding, heat treatment), and **Packaging Machines**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Hospital Model:** Imagine a multi-specialty hospital. The general physician (a *General Purpose Machine* like a CNC lathe) can handle a wide range of patients (parts) with moderate efficiency. An **SPM**, by contrast, is like a specialist surgeon (cardiac surgeon, eye surgeon) — it does *one* job, but does it at extreme speed, precision, and repeatability. **Application-based classification** is essentially asking: *"What hospital department does this specialist belong to?"* — Surgery (Assembly), Diagnostics (Inspection), or Pathology (Testing).

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics in SPMs

The following performance metrics are standard across all application-based SPM classifications and are repeatedly tested in KTU examinations:

- **Cycle Time (T_c):** The total time to complete one operational sequence, measured in **seconds (s)**.
- **Production Rate (P_r):** Number of parts produced per unit time, given by $P_r = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ in **parts/hour**.
- **Machine Efficiency (η):** Ratio of *actual* output to *theoretical* output, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE):** A composite metric combining *Availability × Performance × Quality*, expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF):** Average operational time between consecutive failures, measured in **hours (h)**.
- **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR):** Average time required to restore the machine after a failure, measured in **hours (h)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the relationship $P_r = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ when the cycle time is given in seconds. Always convert to consistent units before substitution.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification Logic — Application-Based Taxonomy

Application-based classification of SPMs follows a **functional decision tree**:

- **Does the machine JOIN components? → Assembly Machine**
- **Does the machine MEASURE / VERIFY components? → Inspection Machine**
- **Does the machine EVALUATE PERFORMANCE / ENDURANCE? → Testing Machine**
- **Does the machine MOVE / TRANSFER components? → Material Handling Machine**
- **Does the machine ALTER material properties? → Processing Machine**
- **Does the machine WRAP / CONTAIN the product? → Packaging Machine**

### 2.2 Category 1 — Assembly Machines

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An Assembly Machine is an SPM engineered to perform one or more *assembly operations* — joining two or more components to form a sub-assembly or finished product, typically using fixtures, indexing tables, and automated fastening tools.

**Sub-classifications of Assembly Machines:**

- **Single-Station Assembly Machines:** All operations occur at one workstation.
- **Multi-Station Assembly Machines (Rotary or In-line):** The part indexes through 4–24 stations; each station performs a single dedicated operation.
- **Carousel / Dial Indexing Assembly Machines:** Components rotate on a circular indexing table.
- **In-line Transfer Assembly Machines:** Components travel in a straight line on a conveyor or pallet transfer system.

**Typical assembly operations performed:**

- Press-fitting (force: **kN** range)
- Riveting (axial/radial)
- Threaded fastening (torque control: **N·m**)
- Snap-fit joining
- Adhesive bonding / welding (spot, MIG, TIG)
- Insert molding

### 2.3 Category 2 — Inspection Machines

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An Inspection Machine is an SPM designed to perform *quality verification* — measuring dimensions, detecting surface defects, and validating conformance of components against engineering specifications without altering the part.

**Sub-classifications of Inspection Machines:**

- **Dimensional Inspection Machines:** Use contact probes (CMM-based) or non-contact optics (laser triangulation, vision systems).
- **Surface Inspection Machines:** Detect scratches, dents, cracks, or coating defects using vision or eddy-current sensors.
- **Leak / Pressure Decay Inspection Machines:** Verify sealing integrity by monitoring pressure drop over a fixed time interval.
- **Vision-Based Inspection Machines:** Use machine vision algorithms for 100% inline inspection.

**Key inspection parameters:**

- **Accuracy:** Closeness of measured value to true value, in **µm**.
- **Repeatability (R):** Variation when the same operator measures the same feature multiple times, in **µm**.
- **Reproducibility:** Variation when different operators/conditions are used, in **µm**.
- **Gauge R&R:** Combined Repeatability and Reproducibility — must be **< 10%** of tolerance for an acceptable measurement system.

### 2.4 Category 3 — Testing Machines

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A Testing Machine is an SPM engineered to evaluate the *functional performance* of a component or assembly under controlled, often destructive or accelerated, conditions — measuring strength, fatigue, endurance, or operational life.

**Sub-classifications of Testing Machines:**

- **Static Testing Machines:** Apply a constant or slowly increasing load (Universal Testing Machines, Hardness Testers).
- **Dynamic / Fatigue Testing Machines:** Apply cyclic loads to determine fatigue life (S-N curve generation).
- **Endurance Testing Machines:** Run components (e.g., switches, valves, bearings) through millions of operational cycles.
- **Environmental Testing Machines:** Subject components to temperature, humidity, vibration, or salt-spray conditions.
- **Functional Testing Machines:** Verify product performance under simulated end-use conditions (e.g., pump flow testing, motor no-load/full-load testing).

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates all KTU-examinable formulas for application-based SPM classification. Use `\vert` (not `|` pipe) inside any math expressions to avoid markdown table breakage.

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application Context** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Production Rate | $P_r = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ | parts/hour | All SPMs (cycle time in seconds) |
| Production Rate (min) | $P_r = \dfrac{60}{T_c}$ | parts/minute | Fast assembly machines |
| Machine Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | % | All SPMs |
| OEE | $OEE = A \times P \times Q$ | decimal or % | Inspection & Assembly |
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | decimal | All SPMs |
| Pressure Decay | $\Delta P = \dfrac{V \times \Delta t}{C}$ | bar/min | Leak Inspection |
| Fatigue Stress | $\sigma_a = \dfrac{F_{max} - F_{min}}{2A}$ | MPa | Testing Machines |
| Fatigue Life (Basquin) | $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$ | MPa | Testing Machines |
| Press-fit Force | $F_p = \mu \times p \times \pi \times d \times L$ | N | Assembly Machines |
| Torque-Power | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | Testing/Assembly |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Assembly machines join engine blocks, transmissions; inspection machines check dimensional tolerances; testing machines validate crankshaft fatigue life.
- **Electronics Industry:** Vision-based inspection machines perform 100% PCB defect detection; testing machines burn-in electronic components.
- **Medical Devices:** Testing machines perform accelerated life tests on implants and catheters; assembly machines join sterile components in cleanroom environments.
- **Aerospace:** Multi-station assembly machines rivet fuselage panels; ultrasonic inspection machines detect subsurface defects in turbine blades.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Production Rate from Cycle Time

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most frequently asked derivation in KTU Module 1. The examiner expects you to start from the definition of cycle time and arrive at the parts-per-hour expression.

**Step 1 — Define Cycle Time**

The cycle time $T_c$ is the total time elapsed between the *start* of one operational sequence and the *start* of the next sequence on the same machine.

$$T_c \;=\; \text{Time for one complete sequence (seconds)}$$

**Step 2 — Express Output in Seconds**

The number of parts produced in **3600 seconds (1 hour)** is given by the total available time divided by the time per part.

$$N_{hour} = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$$

**Step 3 — Account for Machine Efficiency**

In real practice, the machine does not operate at 100% efficiency due to tool changes, indexing delays, and minor stoppages. We multiply by the efficiency factor $\eta$ expressed as a decimal.

$$P_r = \eta \times \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$$

**Step 4 — Final Compact Form**

$$P_r = \dfrac{3600 \eta}{T_c} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{parts}}{\text{hour}}\right]$$

**Interpretation of every symbol:**

- $P_r$ = Production rate, in **parts/hour**.
- $T_c$ = Cycle time, in **seconds**.
- $\eta$ = Machine efficiency as a **decimal** (e.g., 0.85 for 85%).
- 3600 = Number of seconds in **one hour**.

### 3.2 Derivation: OEE from Component Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE) is the gold standard for measuring *true* productivity of an SPM. KTU examiners often present the three components and ask for the combined formula.

**Step 1 — Availability (A)**

Availability measures the fraction of *scheduled* time the machine is actually running.

$$A = \dfrac{\text{Run Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$$

**Step 2 — Performance (P)**

Performance measures how fast the machine runs *relative to its designed maximum speed*.

$$P = \dfrac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Count}}{\text{Run Time}}$$

**Step 3 — Quality (Q)**

Quality measures the fraction of *good* parts produced versus all parts produced.

$$Q = \dfrac{\text{Good Count}}{\text{Total Count}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into OEE**

Multiplying the three dimensionless factors yields the OEE.

$$OEE = A \times P \times Q$$

**Step 5 — World-Class Benchmark**

$$\text{World-class OEE} = 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.99 = 0.8465 \approx 85\%$$

Any OEE below **60%** is considered poor; **85%** is world-class for SPMs.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Multi-Station Assembly Machine

> [!NOTE]
> This is a classic KTU 14-mark style numerical. Master the logic of conversion.

**Given:** A 6-station rotary indexing assembly machine has a station operation time of 12 seconds. The indexing time is 3 seconds per index. The machine efficiency is 85%. The rejection rate at the final station is 2%.

**Step 1 — Determine Total Cycle Time**

The cycle time equals the sum of (number of stations × station time) + (number of index moves × index time). For 6 stations, there are **5 index moves** between stations (the part does not index after the last station before being unloaded).

$$T_c = (6 \times 12) + (5 \times 3)$$

$$T_c = 72 + 15 = 87 \; \text{seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Theoretical Production Rate**

$$P_{theoretical} = \dfrac{3600}{87} = 41.38 \; \text{parts/hour}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Machine Efficiency**

$$P_{actual} = 41.38 \times 0.85 = 35.17 \; \text{parts/hour}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Quality Factor**

$$P_{good} = 35.17 \times (1 - 0.02) = 34.47 \; \text{good parts/hour}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Parts per 8-Hour Shift**

$$P_{shift} = 34.47 \times 8 = 275.76 \approx 276 \; \text{good parts per shift}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Application-Based SPM Types

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following matrix is a high-yield KTU comparison question. Examiners often ask: *"Compare Assembly, Inspection, and Testing machines in terms of contact vs. non-contact, output, and tolerance class."*

| **Parameter** | **Assembly Machine** | **Inspection Machine** | **Testing Machine** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Action** | Joins components | Measures / verifies | Evaluates performance |
| **Affects Part Geometry?** | Yes (adds material/force) | No (non-invasive) | Sometimes (destructive) |
| **Typical Contact Type** | Direct contact (tool) | Contact + Non-contact | Contact (load application) |
| **Output Type** | Sub-assembly / product | Pass / Fail + data | Performance curve / life |
| **Tolerance Class** | ±0.05 mm typical | ±0.001 mm (µm level) | Variable (load: ±0.5%) |
| **Cycle Time** | 5–60 s typical | 1–10 s typical | 60 s – days |
| **Cost Category** | High capital | Medium-High | High capital |
| **Examples** | Engine block assembly | CMM, Vision systems | UTM, Fatigue tester |
| **In-line / Off-line** | In-line | Both | Mostly off-line lab |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

> *Differentiate between an Assembly Machine and an Inspection Machine based on their functional objective and effect on the workpiece.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Basis** | **Assembly Machine** | **Inspection Machine** |
|---|---|---|
| Functional Objective | Joins components to form a sub-assembly | Verifies conformance to specifications |
| Effect on Workpiece | **Alters** the workpiece (adds components/force) | **Does NOT alter** the workpiece |
| Output | A joined sub-assembly | Pass/Fail decision + measured data |
| Example | Engine block assembly machine | CMM / Vision inspection station |

*(Award 1.5 marks for functional objective distinction, 1 mark for effect on workpiece, 0.5 mark for one valid example.)*

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> *List any three sub-classifications of Testing Machines and state one engineering parameter each one measures.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Universal Testing Machine (UTM):** Measures ultimate tensile strength, yield strength, and elongation — *(1 mark)*.
2. **Fatigue Testing Machine:** Measures endurance limit and number of cycles to failure under cyclic loading — *(1 mark)*.
3. **Hardness Testing Machine:** Measures material hardness on Rockwell, Brinell, or Vickers scales — *(1 mark)*.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

> **(a)** Classify Special Purpose Machines based on applications. Explain in detail the working principle and applications of **multi-station rotary indexing assembly machines** with a suitable sketch. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** An SPM has a cycle time of 45 seconds. The machine efficiency is 80% and the rejection rate is 3%. Calculate: (i) Production rate in parts/hour, (ii) Number of good parts produced per 8-hour shift, (iii) OEE if Availability = 0.90, Performance = 0.92, and Quality = 0.97. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Classification of SPMs by Application (2 marks):**

SPMs are classified into Assembly Machines, Inspection Machines, Testing Machines, Material Handling Machines, Processing Machines, and Packaging Machines.

**Multi-Station Rotary Indexing Assembly Machine (5 marks):**

- **Construction:** A circular indexing table with 4–24 equally-spaced workstations, each carrying its own fixture, tool, and actuator.
- **Working Principle:**
  1. Raw components are loaded at the *first station* (manual or via bowl feeder).
  2. The indexing table rotates by a fixed angular pitch (e.g., 360°/8 = 45° for 8 stations).
  3. At each station, a dedicated operation is performed (press-fit, rivet, screw, weld).
  4. After the final station, the assembled part is unloaded automatically.
- **Indexing Mechanism:** Geneva drive, servo motor with cam, or pneumatic ratchet.
- **Applications:** Automotive sub-assemblies, electrical switch assembly, bearing assembly, fuel injector assembly.
- **Advantages:** Compact footprint, high throughput, simultaneous parallel operations.

*(Examiner allocates: 2 marks classification, 2 marks working principle, 1 mark sketch/description, 1 mark advantages + application, 1 mark indexing mechanism.)*

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $T_c = 45 \; s$, $\eta = 0.80$, rejection rate $= 3\% = 0.03$, $A = 0.90$, $P = 0.92$, $Q = 0.97$.

**Step (i) — Production Rate (2 marks):**

$$P_r = \dfrac{3600 \times \eta}{T_c} = \dfrac{3600 \times 0.80}{45}$$

$$P_r = \dfrac{2880}{45} = 64 \; \text{parts/hour}$$

**Step (ii) — Good Parts per 8-Hour Shift (2 marks):**

$$P_{good/hour} = 64 \times (1 - 0.03) = 64 \times 0.97 = 62.08$$

$$P_{shift} = 62.08 \times 8 = 496.64 \approx 497 \; \text{good parts per shift}$$

**Step (iii) — OEE Calculation (3 marks):**

$$OEE = A \times P \times Q = 0.90 \times 0.92 \times 0.97$$

$$OEE = 0.7668 \times 0.97 = 0.8032 \approx 80.32\%$$

**Interpretation:** An OEE of ~80% is **good** (world-class is 85%), indicating the machine has minor scope for improvement in either performance or quality.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

> **(a)** Explain with examples the classification of **Inspection Machines** and **Testing Machines** based on the type of measurement / evaluation performed. State at least two sub-classifications under each. *(7 marks)*

> **(b)** A vision-based inspection system has a cycle time of 4 seconds per part. The system uses a 5-megapixel camera with a field of view of 50 mm × 50 mm. The pixel resolution is 10 µm. Calculate: (i) Theoretical pixel count per inspection, (ii) Production rate in parts/minute assuming 90% efficiency, (iii) State whether the system can resolve a feature of size 0.05 mm. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]:**

**Inspection Machines (3.5 marks):**

Inspection machines verify part conformance *without altering* the part. Sub-classifications:

1. **Dimensional Inspection Machines:** Measure linear/angular dimensions.
   - *Example:* Coordinate Measuring Machine (CMM) with ±2 µm accuracy.
   - *Example:* Laser triangulation gauge for 100% inline measurement.

2. **Surface Inspection Machines:** Detect visual defects.
   - *Example:* Machine vision system detecting paint scratches on automotive bodies.
   - *Example:* Eddy-current system detecting surface cracks on machined parts.

3. **Leak / Pressure Decay Inspection Machines:** Verify sealing.
   - *Example:* Helium leak detector for HVAC compressor testing.

**Testing Machines (3.5 marks):**

Testing machines evaluate *functional performance*, often under stress.

1. **Static Testing Machines:** Apply constant or slowly increasing load.
   - *Example:* Universal Testing Machine (UTM) for tensile testing of steel specimens.

2. **Dynamic / Fatigue Testing Machines:** Apply cyclic loads.
   - *Example:* Rotating beam fatigue tester for S-N curve generation.
   - *Example:* Servo-hydraulic fatigue machine for aircraft wing component endurance.

3. **Functional Testing Machines:** Validate end-use performance.
   - *Example:* Pump flow testing rig measuring head vs. discharge.
   - *Example:* Motor no-load / full-load test bench measuring efficiency, slip, and temperature rise.

*(Examiner allocates: 1.5 marks for Inspection, 1.5 marks for Testing, 1 mark per sub-classification with example, 0.5 mark for distinction.)*

**Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]:**

**Given:** $T_c = 4 \; s$, FOV = 50 mm × 50 mm, pixel resolution = 10 µm = 0.01 mm, Camera = 5 MP, $\eta = 90\%$.

**Step (i) — Theoretical Pixel Count (2 marks):**

Number of pixels along one axis:

$$N_x = \dfrac{FOV_x}{r} = \dfrac{50 \; mm}{0.01 \; mm} = 5000 \; \text{pixels}$$

Similarly:

$$N_y = \dfrac{50}{0.01} = 5000 \; \text{pixels}$$

Total pixels:

$$N_{total} = 5000 \times 5000 = 25,000,000 = 25 \; \text{MP}$$

**Note:** The 5 MP rating is a nominal specification; the *effective* resolution over the FOV is 25 MP — the system likely uses sub-pixel interpolation or a higher-resolution sensor.

**Step (ii) — Production Rate (2 marks):**

$$P_r = \eta \times \dfrac{60}{T_c} = 0.90 \times \dfrac{60}{4}$$

$$P_r = 0.90 \times 15 = 13.5 \; \text{parts/minute}$$

**Step (iii) — Resolution Check (3 marks):**

To resolve a feature of size $L = 0.05 \; mm$, the Nyquist criterion requires at least **2 pixels** to span the feature.

$$\text{Pixels per feature} = \dfrac{L}{r} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.01} = 5 \; \text{pixels}$$

Since 5 pixels span the feature (well above the Nyquist minimum of 2), the system **CAN resolve** the 0.05 mm feature.

**Rule of thumb applied:** A feature is *resolvable* if it is covered by at least 2 pixels in the smallest dimension.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (1–2 marks lost):** Students often use $P_r = \dfrac{60}{T_c}$ when the answer should be in **parts/hour**. Always read the unit asked in the question and convert $T_c$ accordingly.
> 2. **Forgetting to Apply Efficiency (2 marks lost):** The formula $P_r = \dfrac{3600}{T_c}$ gives the *theoretical* rate. The question will often explicitly state "machine efficiency is X%" — failing to multiply by $\eta$ is a guaranteed mark-deduction.
> 3. **Confusing Indexing Stations vs. Total Stations (1 mark lost):** For an *N-station* rotary machine, the number of *index moves* per cycle is $(N - 1)$, not $N$. The last unload is typically not counted as an index.
> 4. **OEE Confusion (2 marks lost):** Many students write OEE = A + P + Q (additive). It is **multiplicative**: $OEE = A \times P \times Q$.
> 5. **Misclassifying Inspection as Testing (1 mark lost):** Inspection = *measures/verifies*; Testing = *evaluates performance/strength*. If the part is *stressed to failure*, it is testing; if the part is *just measured*, it is inspection.
> 6. **Skipping the "Effect on Workpiece" in Part A (0.5–1 mark lost):** Examiners explicitly award marks for stating whether the SPM alters the workpiece geometry. Assembly = yes; Inspection = no; Testing = conditional.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Application-Based SPM Classification:**

- ☐ SPM = **Special Purpose Machine** — designed for **one dedicated operation** on a specific part family.
- ☐ Classification by Application includes: **Assembly, Inspection, Testing, Material Handling, Processing, Packaging**.
- ☐ **Assembly Machines** *JOIN* components → affects workpiece geometry → output is a *sub-assembly*.
- ☐ **Inspection Machines** *MEASURE/VERIFY* → does **NOT** alter workpiece → output is *Pass/Fail* and data.
- ☐ **Testing Machines** *EVALUATE PERFORMANCE* → often *destructive* → output is *life/strength curves*.
- ☐ Multi-station rotary assembly: **N stations → (N−1) index moves per cycle**.
- ☐ Master formula: $P_r = \dfrac{3600 \times \eta}{T_c}$ for parts/hour with $T_c$ in seconds.
- ☐ OEE = $A \times P \times Q$ (multiplicative, not additive); world-class ≈ **85%**.
- ☐ Availability $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ — critical for maintenance decisions.
- ☐ Inspection tolerance ±0.001 mm; Assembly tolerance ±0.05 mm; Testing load accuracy ±0.5%.
- ☐ In KTU answers, always include: **a labelled sketch/diagram**, **unit in every numerical answer**, and **a one-line interpretation** of the final result.
- ☐ Vision resolution check: feature is *resolvable* if covered by **≥ 2 pixels** (Nyquist criterion).
- ☐ Pressure decay inspection monitors $\Delta P$ over a fixed time interval to detect leaks.
- ☐ Fatigue testing follows Basquin's law: $\sigma_a = \sigma'_f (2N_f)^b$.
- ☐ Always distinguish between *theoretical* and *actual* production rate in numerical problems.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Components and Subsystems - Mechanical, Electrical, and Control Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT601 - SPECIAL PURPOSE MACHINES FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Components and Subsystems - Mechanical, Electrical, and Control Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Purpose Machines (SPMs)** are custom-engineered, automated manufacturing systems designed to perform a specific operation (e.g., drilling, tapping, milling, assembly) on a dedicated part, with high repeatability, rigidity, and throughput. The 2024 KTU syllabus treats SPMs as a **cyber-physical system** built from three tightly coupled subsystems: **Mechanical, Electrical, and Control.**

### 1.1 What is an SPM?
An **SPM (Special Purpose Machine)** is a piece of automated equipment engineered for a *single*, *fixed*, and *repetitive* manufacturing task. Unlike a CNC (Computer Numerical Control) machine which is versatile, an SPM is optimized for **one part family** and **one process** — and it does that process thousands of times per shift with near-zero human intervention.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle vs. Food Truck" Intuition:**
Think of a **CNC machine** as a bicycle — flexible, can go anywhere, but the rider (operator) must constantly guide it. Think of an **SPM** as a **food truck permanently parked outside a stadium** — it has no steering wheel, no gears for turning, but everything inside is purpose-built to make *one* menu item (say, only chai) at blinding speed with minimum labor. Replace "chai" with "drill 4 holes in a gearbox cover" — that's an SPM.

### 1.2 The Three Subsystems of an SPM
An SPM is not a single block. It is a **hierarchical integration** of three functional layers:

| Subsystem | Role in SPM | Real-World Analogy (Food Truck) |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Subsystem** | Provides the rigid skeleton and physical motion (structural frame, drives, slides, spindles) | The truck's chassis, wheels, and cooking vessels |
| **Electrical Subsystem** | Provides power and electrical signaling (motors, drives, sensors, wiring) | The LPG line, electric burners, battery, dashboard wiring |
| **Control Subsystem** | Provides intelligence and sequencing (PLC, HMI, sensors feedback, pneumatic valves) | The chef's brain, timers, thermostat, recipe card |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 mandates that students *must* be able to identify, classify, and justify the selection of components across all three subsystems for a given automation task. Questions are typically application-based ("Design an SPM to drill 6 holes in an engine block — justify your drive system, motor type, and control architecture").

### 1.3 Why SPMs Matter in Modern Manufacturing
- **Cycle Time Reduction:** From 60 s (manual) → 8 s (SPM).
- **Consistency:** Identical quality for 1 million parts (Six Sigma compliant).
- **Lights-Out Manufacturing:** Runs unattended during night shifts.
- **Industry 4.0 Compatibility:** Modern SPMs embed **IIoT sensors, OPC-UA, MQTT** for predictive maintenance.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Memorize for KTU)
- Standard supply voltage in India: **415 V, 3-phase, 50 Hz** AC.
- Standard control voltage: **24 V DC** (per IEC 61131-2 for PLCs).
- Standard pneumatic pressure: **6 bar (0.6 MPa)**.
- Standard hydraulic pressure: **70–210 bar**.
- Encoder resolution benchmark: **2048 PPR** to **20-bit (1,048,576 PPR)**.
- Servo loop bandwidth: typically **1 kHz to 2 kHz**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mechanical Subsystem — Anatomy of Motion
The mechanical subsystem is the *body* of the SPM. Without it, the controller has nothing to command and the motors have nothing to move. It is typically divided into **structural elements**, **kinematic elements**, and **actuation elements**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Standard):** A *kinematic pair* is a joint between two links that allows relative motion. In an SPM, every moving element (slide, spindle, tool head) forms a kinematic pair with the frame.

#### 2.1.1 Structural Elements (The Skeleton)
- **Machine Frame / Column / Base:** Made of **Cast Iron (Grade FG 260)** or **Fabricated Mild Steel (IS 2062)** for high damping and rigidity.
- **Why Cast Iron?** It has a **damping coefficient of ~0.06** (vs. 0.001 for steel) — it *kills* vibrations, which is critical for surface finish.
- **Slides and Slideways:** Linear motion guides; modern SPMs use **Linear Recirculating Ball Bearings (LRBB)** or **Roller Guides** (e.g., THK HSR, Hiwin HGH).
- **Fixtures and Tool Holders:** Hydraulic or pneumatic clamping to hold the workpiece and the cutting tool.

#### 2.1.2 Power Transmission / Drive Elements
The choice of drive defines the SPM's accuracy, speed, and cost.

**Type 1 — Ball Screw Drive** (Most common for high-precision SPMs)
- Converts rotary motion → linear motion via a screw shaft and ball nut.
- Efficiency: **η ≈ 0.90–0.95** (vs. 0.30–0.50 for a plain lead screw).
- Pitch accuracy: **C3 (5 µm / 300 mm) to C0 (3 µm / 300 mm)**.
- Used in: Drilling heads, tapping spindles, indexing tables.

**Type 2 — Rack and Pinion Drive**
- For long travel lengths (> 2 m). Common in gantry SPMs.
- Linear speed: up to **5 m/s** with helical rack + ground pinion.

**Type 3 — Timing Belt and Pulley Drive**
- For low-precision, high-speed transfer (e.g., conveyorized SPMs).
- Speed: up to **20 m/s**; backlash ~0.1 mm.

**Type 4 — Linear Motor Drive** (Premium, contact-free)
- Direct linear force, no mechanical transmission.
- Acceleration: up to **5 g**; accuracy: ± 1 µm.
- Used in high-end semiconductor and inspection SPMs.

#### 2.1.3 Actuation Elements (Where Force is Born)
- **Pneumatic Cylinders:** For clamping, indexing, small strokes. Force = P × A.
- **Hydraulic Cylinders:** For heavy-duty clamping (up to 50 tonnes).
- **Servo Motor + Ball Screw:** For *contoured* motion (variable speed/position).

### 2.2 The Electrical Subsystem — The Nervous System
The electrical subsystem *powers* and *informs* the machine.

#### 2.2.1 Power Circuit Components
- **Three-Phase Induction Motor (Squirrel Cage):** For constant-speed spindles (e.g., 1440 rpm @ 50 Hz, 4-pole).
- **Servo Motor (Brushless DC / AC):** For variable speed/position. Has built-in **encoder (rotary)**.
- **Stepper Motor:** For open-loop indexing. Holding torque up to 50 Nm.
- **Servo Drive (Amplifier):** Receives command pulses from PLC, outputs 3-phase PWM to servo motor.
- **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker), MCCB, Contactor, Overload Relay:** For protection and switching.
- **SMPS (Switched Mode Power Supply):** Converts 230 V AC → 24 V DC for the control bus.
- **Transformer:** Steps down 415 V → 110 V / 24 V for auxiliary circuits.

#### 2.2.2 Signal & Feedback Components
- **Limit Switches (Mechanical):** End-of-travel safety. *Must be used* on every slide (per ISO 13849-1, Category 1 minimum).
- **Proximity Sensors:**
  - **Inductive:** Detects metals only; range 2–8 mm.
  - **Capacitive:** Detects any material; range up to 25 mm.
  - **Magnetic (Reed):** Detects magnet on piston (cylinder position feedback).
- **Photoelectric Sensors:** Diffuse, retro-reflective, through-beam. Range up to 60 m.
- **Encoders:**
  - **Incremental:** Outputs A, B, Z pulses. Resolution 1000–5000 PPR.
  - **Absolute (Multi-turn):** Outputs unique code per shaft angle. 17-bit to 25-bit.
- **Load Cell / Pressure Transducer:** Monitors clamping force or hydraulic pressure.

### 2.3 The Control Subsystem — The Brain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (IEC 61131-3 Standard):** A **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)** is a digital electronic device, designed for use in industrial environments, which uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of user-oriented instructions for implementing specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic, to control, through digital or analog inputs/outputs, various types of machines or processes.

#### 2.3.1 Architecture of an SPM Control System
The control system is **hierarchical** — the *higher* the level, the *slower* and *more strategic* it is.

**Level 1 — Sensor / Actuator Layer:** Limit switches, proximity sensors, solenoid valves, contactors.
**Level 2 — Controller Layer:** PLC or dedicated motion controller. Runs scan cycle every **10–20 ms**.
**Level 3 — Supervisory Layer:** HMI (Human Machine Interface) for operator input/display.
**Level 4 — Plant Layer:** SCADA / MES / Cloud (Industry 4.0).

#### 2.3.2 Types of Controllers
- **PLC (e.g., Siemens S7-1200, Allen-Bradley CompactLogix):** Ladder Logic, Function Block, Structured Text. For sequencing and discrete I/O.
- **Motion Controller (e.g., Delta ASDA, Yaskawa MP2300):** Dedicated for multi-axis synchronized motion (electronic cam, gear, linear interpolation).
- **CNC Controller (e.g., Fanuc, Siemens Sinumerik):** Used when the SPM needs G-code programming (rare in pure SPMs, common in hybrid CNC-SPMs).
- **Microcontroller (Arduino, STM32, PLC-on-chip):** For low-cost, single-purpose SPMs.
- **PC-Based Soft-PLC:** Industrial PC with real-time OS (e.g., Beckhoff TwinCAT, Codesys).

#### 2.3.3 Control Modes (Memorize for KTU)
- **Open-Loop Control:** No feedback. Example: Stepper motor driven by a fixed pulse count. *Cheap, error accumulates.*
- **Closed-Loop Control:** Feedback present. Example: Servo motor with encoder. *Accurate, self-correcting.*
- **Semi-Closed Loop:** Feedback on motor shaft, not on load. Common in CNC feed drives.

### 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the *core engineering mathematics* required for the SPM design questions in KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination). Master these.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Utility in SPMs |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = P \times A$ | $F$ = Cylinder force (N), $P$ = Pressure (Pa), $A$ = Piston area (m²) | Clamping force calculation |
| 2 | $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ (full bore) | $D$ = Piston diameter (m) | Pneumatic/hydraulic sizing |
| 3 | $T = F \times \frac{L}{2\pi \eta}$ | $T$ = Drive torque (N·m), $F$ = Thrust (N), $L$ = Lead (m), $\eta$ = Efficiency | Ball screw torque sizing |
| 4 | $v = N \times L$ | $v$ = Linear speed (m/s), $N$ = Rotational speed (rev/s) | Linear speed of a slide |
| 5 | $P_{motor} = \frac{F \times v}{\eta_{total}}$ | $P_{motor}$ = Required motor power (W) | Motor power selection |
| 6 | $N_{max} = \frac{v_{max}}{L} \times 60$ | $N_{max}$ = Motor rpm, $v_{max}$ = Required linear speed (m/s) | Motor speed selection |
| 7 | $J_{eq} = J_{m} + J_{L} \times \left(\frac{L}{2\pi}\right)^2$ | $J_{eq}$ = Equivalent inertia at motor shaft, $J_m$ = Motor inertia, $J_L$ = Load inertia (kg·m²) | Servo motor inertia matching |
| 8 | $a_{max} = \frac{T_{max} - T_{f}}{J_{eq} \times \omega}$ | $a_{max}$ = Max acceleration (rad/s²), $T_{max}$ = Peak torque, $T_f$ = Friction torque | Acceleration verification |
| 9 | $f_{cycle} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}}$ | $f_{cycle}$ = Cycle frequency (Hz), $T_{cycle}$ = Cycle time (s) | Throughput calculation |
| 10 | $Resolution = \frac{L}{4 \times E_{ppr}}$ | $E_{ppr}$ = Encoder pulses per revolution | Positioning accuracy check |
| 11 | $T_{scan} = n_{I/O} \times t_{per}$ | $T_{scan}$ = PLC scan time (ms), $n_{I/O}$ = Number of I/O, $t_{per}$ = Time per I/O (µs) | PLC scan budget |
| 12 | $\eta_{total} = \eta_{screw} \times \eta_{gear} \times \eta_{bearing}$ | Product of subsystem efficiencies | Overall drive efficiency |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In Formula 3, students *forget* to convert lead $L$ from mm to m, leading to torque values off by **a factor of 1000**. Always write units at every step.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility — Why This Matters in the Field
- **Automotive Assembly (Tata, Maruti):** SPMs drill and tap engine blocks at 8 s/cycle. Each SPM has 12–20 servo axes, 200+ sensors, and 1 PLC.
- **Pharma Packaging (Cipla, Sun Pharma):** SPMs fill, cap, and label bottles at 600 bottles/minute.
- **Electronics (Foxconn):** Linear-motor-driven SPMs place chips on PCBs with ± 1 µm accuracy.
- **Defense (DRDL, HAL):** Custom SPMs for missile body assembly and radar component testing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section provides the *exhaustive, KTU-board-style* derivations for the most frequently asked numerical problems on SPM components. Every algebraic step is shown. No shortcuts.

### 3.1 Derivation: Ball Screw Drive Torque Required to Lift a Load

**Problem Statement:** A vertical slide of an SPM is driven by a **ball screw of lead $L = 10$ mm/rev**. It must lift a **load of mass $m = 500$ kg** against gravity, with a **friction force of $F_f = 200$ N** acting downward. The ball screw has an **efficiency $\eta = 0.92$**. Calculate the torque required at the motor end, in N·m. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

#### Step 1 — Identify the Forces
The total force the screw must overcome is the sum of the **weight of the load** and the **friction force** (both act downward, so the screw must pull up against both).

$$
F_{total} = F_{weight} + F_{friction}
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Weight
The weight is the product of mass and gravitational acceleration.

$$
F_{weight} = m \times g
$$
$$
F_{weight} = 500 \text{ kg} \times 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2
$$
$$
F_{weight} = 4905 \text{ N}
$$

#### Step 3 — Add the Friction Force
$$
F_{total} = 4905 \text{ N} + 200 \text{ N}
$$
$$
F_{total} = 5105 \text{ N}
$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the Work–Power Equivalence for a Screw
For a single revolution of the screw, the **work done by the torque** equals the **work done in lifting the load** (with the efficiency factor $\eta$).

Work done by torque in one revolution:
$$
W_{input} = T \times 2\pi
$$
Work done against load in one revolution (screw advances by $L$):
$$
W_{output} = F_{total} \times L
$$

With efficiency:
$$
W_{output} = \eta \times W_{input}
$$
$$
F_{total} \times L = \eta \times T \times 2\pi
$$

#### Step 5 — Solve for the Torque $T$
$$
T = \frac{F_{total} \times L}{\eta \times 2\pi}
$$

#### Step 6 — Substitute Values (with Unit Consistency)
**Critical Unit Conversion:** Lead $L$ is given in **mm**, but SI requires **m**.
$$
L = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.010 \text{ m}
$$

Now substitute:
$$
T = \frac{5105 \text{ N} \times 0.010 \text{ m}}{0.92 \times 2\pi \text{ rad}}
$$

#### Step 7 — Evaluate the Numerator
$$
5105 \times 0.010 = 51.05 \text{ N·m}
$$

#### Step 8 — Evaluate the Denominator
$$
0.92 \times 2\pi = 0.92 \times 6.2832 = 5.7805
$$

#### Step 9 — Final Division
$$
T = \frac{51.05}{5.7805}
$$
$$
T \approx 8.83 \text{ N·m}
$$

#### Step 10 — Verification by Dimensional Analysis
$$
[T] = \frac{\text{N} \times \text{m}}{\text{rad (dimensionless)}} = \text{N·m} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $T \approx 8.83$ N·m. A standard **servo motor with rated torque of 10 N·m** (e.g., Delta ECMA-E21310RS) is therefore sufficient, with a safety margin of ~12%.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Motor Power Required for a Horizontal Slide

**Problem Statement:** An SPM has a horizontal slide driven by a **rack and pinion**. The slide must accelerate a **load of 800 kg** to a **linear velocity of 1.5 m/s** in **0.3 s**, against a **friction coefficient of 0.05** (between the slide and guide). The total mechanical efficiency from motor to slide is **0.85**. Calculate the **required motor power** in kW. Take $g = 9.81$ m/s².

#### Step 1 — Determine the Required Acceleration
The slide must go from 0 to 1.5 m/s in 0.3 s. Assuming constant acceleration:

$$
a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}
$$
$$
a = \frac{1.5 - 0}{0.3} = 5.0 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Force Needed to Accelerate the Mass
By Newton's second law:

$$
F_{accel} = m \times a
$$
$$
F_{accel} = 800 \text{ kg} \times 5.0 \text{ m/s}^2
$$
$$
F_{accel} = 4000 \text{ N}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Friction Force
The normal force on a horizontal slide is the weight of the load (no vertical acceleration):

$$
F_{normal} = m \times g = 800 \times 9.81 = 7848 \text{ N}
$$
$$
F_{friction} = \mu \times F_{normal}
$$
$$
F_{friction} = 0.05 \times 7848 = 392.4 \text{ N}
$$

#### Step 4 — Total Force the Drive Must Deliver
$$
F_{total} = F_{accel} + F_{friction}
$$
$$
F_{total} = 4000 + 392.4 = 4392.4 \text{ N}
$$

#### Step 5 — Mechanical Power at the Slide
Power is force times velocity. We use the **maximum velocity** since acceleration has just finished:

$$
P_{slide} = F_{total} \times v_{max}
$$
$$
P_{slide} = 4392.4 \text{ N} \times 1.5 \text{ m/s} = 6588.6 \text{ W}
$$

#### Step 6 — Account for Drive Efficiency
The motor must deliver more power than the slide receives, because the drive train has losses:

$$
P_{motor} = \frac{P_{slide}}{\eta_{total}}
$$
$$
P_{motor} = \frac{6588.6}{0.85}
$$
$$
P_{motor} = 7751.3 \text{ W} \approx 7.75 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Step 7 — Add a Safety Factor (Industry Practice)
Standard practice is to multiply by a safety factor $S_f = 1.25$ for servo motors (to handle peak loads and thermal margins):

$$
P_{selected} = 7.75 \times 1.25 = 9.69 \text{ kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Selection:** Choose a **10 kW (13.4 HP) servo motor** (e.g., Siemens 1FK7100-2AF71). Round up to nearest standard size. The KTU examiner expects you to mention the safety factor and the rounding-up rule.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is *exactly* the kind of comparative table KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark design question. Memorize the trade-offs.

| Selection Criterion | Ball Screw | Rack & Pinion | Timing Belt | Linear Motor |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Max Linear Speed** | 1.0 m/s | 5.0 m/s | 10.0 m/s | 5.0 m/s |
| **Positioning Accuracy** | ± 2–5 µm | ± 50 µm | ± 100 µm | ± 1 µm |
| **Max Travel Length** | 6 m | 30+ m | 10 m | 4 m |
| **Efficiency** | 0.90–0.95 | 0.85–0.90 | 0.95–0.98 | N/A (direct) |
| **Maintenance** | Lubrication every 6 months | Low | Very low | Very low (no contact) |
| **Cost (per metre)** | High | Medium | Low | Very High |
| **Typical Application** | Drilling/tapping head | Gantry loader | Conveyor transfer | Semiconductor pick-place |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: PLC Scan Time Verification

**Problem Statement:** A Siemens S7-1200 PLC has **24 digital inputs, 16 digital outputs, and 2 analog inputs**. The PLC processor takes **0.5 µs per binary instruction** and **2.5 µs per analog conversion**. The user program has **1000 binary instructions and 50 analog conversions**. The I/O update takes **0.3 ms**. Is the PLC fast enough for a **50 ms cycle time** requirement?

#### Step 1 — Binary Logic Scan Time
$$
T_{bin} = n_{bin} \times t_{per,bin}
$$
$$
T_{bin} = 1000 \times 0.5 \text{ µs} = 500 \text{ µs} = 0.5 \text{ ms}
$$

#### Step 2 — Analog Conversion Scan Time
$$
T_{ana} = n_{ana} \times t_{per,ana}
$$
$$
T_{ana} = 50 \times 2.5 \text{ µs} = 125 \text{ µs} = 0.125 \text{ ms}
$$

#### Step 3 — Total Scan Time
$$
T_{scan} = T_{bin} + T_{ana} + T_{IO}
$$
$$
T_{scan} = 0.5 + 0.125 + 0.3 = 0.925 \text{ ms}
$$

#### Step 4 — Compare with the Required Cycle Time
$$
T_{scan} = 0.925 \text{ ms} \ll T_{required} = 50 \text{ ms}
$$
$$
\text{Margin} = 50 - 0.925 = 49.075 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The PLC has **~98% spare time** — the design is *overspec* and could use a smaller, cheaper PLC (e.g., Siemens LOGO! 8) for cost optimization. KTU examiners love this kind of trade-off reasoning in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (per the V10 safeguard protocol) and **raw uppercase text** inside labels. No markdown formatting inside node labels.

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of an SPM (Three-Subsystem View)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[OPERATOR INTERFACE HMI PANEL] --> nodeB[SUPERVISORY CONTROL LAYER LEVEL 3]
    nodeB --> nodeC[CONTROL SUBSYSTEM PLC AND MOTION CONTROLLER LEVEL 2]
    nodeC --> nodeD[ELECTRICAL SUBSYSTEM POWER AND SIGNAL LEVEL 1.5]
    nodeD --> nodeE[MECHANICAL SUBSYSTEM RIGID FRAME AND DRIVES LEVEL 1]
    nodeE --> nodeF[WORKPIECE OUTPUT]
    nodeF -. FEEDBACK .-> nodeD
    nodeD -. SENSOR DATA .-> nodeC
    nodeC -. STATUS DISPLAY .-> nodeB
    nodeB -. VISUALIZATION .-> nodeA
    nodeG[EXTERNAL UTILITIES COMPRESSED AIR AND POWER SUPPLY] --> nodeD
    nodeG --> nodeE
```

### 4.2 Detailed Component Architecture — Subsystem View

```mermaid
graph TB

        nodeM2[LINEAR GUIDES LRBB TYPE]

        nodeM4[SPINDLE MOTOR MOUNT]

    subgraph nodeSUB2[ELECTRICAL SUBSYSTEM]

    end

    subgraph nodeSUB3[CONTROL SUBSYSTEM]

    nodeC2 --> nodeE3
    nodeE2 --> nodeM3

    nodeC4 -. E STOP SIGNAL .-> nodeE3
```

### 4.3 Functional Signal Flow Diagram — Closed-Loop Servo Axis

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeP1[COMMAND PULSE FROM PLC POSITION SETPOINT] --> nodeP2[SERVO DRIVE PID CONTROLLER]
    nodeP2 --> nodeP3[PWM POWER AMPLIFIER]
```
