2. **Conceptual Design** — generate alternative kinematic concepts (rotary, in-line, trunnion); evaluate using Pugh Matrix.
3. **Embodiment / Detailed Design** — size components, perform tolerance stack-up, run FMEA, prepare GA drawings and BOM.
4. **Validation and Prototyping** — virtual commissioning using simulation, physical prototype, measure and iterate.
5. **Production, Commissioning and Continuous Improvement** — pilot run, training, documentation, and field-data-driven next-generation design.

*Block diagram: refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.*

##### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Given:** $T_{op} = \{14, 18, 12, 16, 20, 10\}$ s, $T_{idx} = 5$ s, $n = 6$.

**(i) Bottleneck and cycle time**

The bottleneck is the station with the *longest* operation time:

$$
T_{bottleneck} = \max(T_{op}) = 20 \text{ s}
$$

Achievable cycle time:

$$
T_c = T_{bottleneck} + T_{idx} = 20 + 5 = 25 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Line efficiency**

$$
\eta_L = \dfrac{\sum T_{op}}{n \cdot T_c} = \dfrac{14+18+12+16+20+10}{6 \times 25} = \dfrac{90}{150} = 0.60 = 60\%
$$

**(iii) Balance delay**

$$
D_b = 1 - \eta_L = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40 = 40\%
$$

**(iv) Practical improvement method**

A practical method is to **redistribute operations to balance the load**. For instance, move 4 s of work from the bottleneck (20 s) station to the lightest station (10 s), giving 16 s and 14 s respectively. This increases efficiency without adding any hardware. Alternatively, **add a 7th station** and split the 20 s task into two 10 s tasks, raising the theoretical efficiency toward 100%.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **reliability-centered design (RCD)** approach for SPMs. Show that the system MTBF of a series SPM is the reciprocal of the sum of individual component failure rates. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-station SPM has sub-systems with failure rates (per hour): $\lambda_1 = 6 \times 10^{-5}$, $\lambda_2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$, $\lambda_3 = 4 \times 10^{-5}$, $\lambda_4 = 8 \times 10^{-5}$. If the machine is required to operate for 800 hours between scheduled maintenance:
   (i) Compute the system MTBF.
   (ii) Compute the system reliability at t = 800 hr.
   (iii) Comment on whether parallel redundancy for sub-system 2 is justified. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Reliability-Centered Design (RCD)** is a methodology that places *failure prevention* at the center of the design process. Instead of asking "what should this machine do?", RCD first asks "**how can this machine fail, and what is the consequence of each failure?**"

The four steps of RCD are:
1. **Identify functions** — what must the SPM do to be considered successful?
2. **Identify failure modes** — FMEA is the standard tool.
3. **Prioritize by risk** — RPN (Risk Priority Number) = Severity × Occurrence × Detection.
4. **Design preventive / mitigative measures** — derating, redundancy, fail-safe interlocks.

**Derivation of series MTBF:**

For *n* independent sub-systems in series, each with reliability $R_i(t) = e^{-\lambda_i t}$:

$$
R_{SPM}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t}
$$

Since the product of exponentials equals the exponential of the sum:

$$
R_{SPM}(t) = e^{-\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i\right) t} = e^{-\lambda_{sys}\, t}
$$

Therefore, the system failure rate is $\lambda_{sys} = \sum \lambda_i$, and the system MTBF is:

$$
\boxed{\text{MTBF}_{sys} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_{sys}} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}}
$$

##### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Given:** $\lambda_1 = 6 \times 10^{-5}$, $\lambda_2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$, $\lambda_3 = 4 \times 10^{-5}$, $\lambda_4 = 8 \times 10^{-5}$ per hour.

**(i) System MTBF**

$$
\lambda_{sys} = (6 + 12 + 4 + 8) \times 10^{-5} = 30 \times 10^{-5} = 3.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /hr}
$$

$$
\text{MTBF}_{sys} = \dfrac{1}{3.0 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 3333 \text{ hours}
$$

**(ii) Reliability at t = 800 hr**

$$
R(800) = e^{-3.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 800} = e^{-0.24} \approx 0.7866 \;\; (78.66\%)
$$

**Interpretation:** There is a 78.66% probability that the SPM will *not* fail during the first 800 hours.

**(iii) Justification of parallel redundancy for sub-system 2**

Sub-system 2 has the *highest* failure rate ($\lambda_2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$), contributing 40% of the total $\lambda_{sys}$. **Yes, parallel redundancy is justified**, because the largest contributor dominates the series system. For two identical parallel sub-systems, the combined reliability is:

$$
R_{parallel}(t) = 1 - (1 - R_2(t))^2 = 1 - (1 - e^{-\lambda_2 t})^2
$$

At $t = 800$ hr, $R_2 = e^{-0.096} \approx 0.9084$, so:

$$
R_{parallel}(800) = 1 - (1 - 0.9084)^2 = 1 - (0.0916)^2 = 1 - 0.00839 = 0.9916
$$

This raises the system reliability from 78.66% to nearly 98%, an excellent return on the redundancy investment.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks (verified against KTU valuation patterns):**
> 1. **Mixing up MTBF and MTTF** — MTBF is for *repairable* systems, MTTF for *non-repairable* components (like a single bearing). Examiners deduct 1 mark instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting to add index time** to operation time when computing cycle time. This is the most common 2-mark error.
> 3. **Using decimal values for the number of stations** — always apply the ceiling function explicitly.
> 4. **No units in the final answer** — KTU 2024 marking scheme reserves 0.5 marks for correct units.
> 5. **In FMEA/RCD answers**, students describe *what* to do but forget to mention the *RPN formula* — examiners expect it.
> 6. **Reliability derivation**: many students write $R_{sys} = \sum R_i$ (which is wrong for series systems — the correct form is the *product*). This is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SPM design methodology** = a structured, iterative, multi-phase engineering recipe — not a single drawing exercise.
- The **five universal phases** are: *Problem Definition → Conceptual → Embodiment → Validation → Production & Improvement*.
- **Three pillars** of SPM design quality: **Efficiency (OEE)**, **Reliability (MTBF, R(t))**, and **Maintainability (MTTR)**.
- **Cycle time** = longest station operation + index/transfer time.
- **Line efficiency** = (sum of operation times) / (number of stations × cycle time).
- **Balance delay** = 1 − line efficiency; it quantifies idle station time.
- For a **series SPM**, the overall reliability is the *product* of individual sub-system reliabilities; the *sum* of failure rates determines system MTBF.
- The **weakest sub-system dominates** — design effort should be focused on the lowest-reliability component, often via *parallel redundancy*.
- **Modular design** is the default modern recommendation: it improves reliability, reduces cost, and enables quick changeover.
- **OEE ≥ 85%** is the world-class benchmark; below 60% is unacceptable.
- Always carry **units**, always use the **ceiling function** for station count, and always **define variables** before substituting numbers.
- **Design for X (DFX)** is the umbrella philosophy: Design for Manufacturing, Assembly, Test, Maintenance, Reliability, Cost, Environment, Safety — all start at the *concept stage*, not the *production stage*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Automation Technologies in Special Purpose Machines (SPMs)** refer to the integrated suite of electromechanical, computational, sensory, and intelligent control systems that collectively enable an SPM to perform dedicated manufacturing, assembly, inspection, or material-handling operations with **minimal human intervention**, **high repeatability**, and **programmable flexibility**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, automation technologies are classified into the following functional sub-domains:

| **S.No** | **Technology Domain** | **Primary Role in SPM** |
|----------|----------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | Industrial Robotics | Material handling, welding, painting, pick & place |
| 2 | Machine Vision Systems | Inspection, guidance, quality control |
| 3 | Artificial Intelligence (AI) / Expert Systems | Adaptive decision-making, predictive maintenance |
| 4 | Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs) | Sequential and continuous control logic |
| 5 | Computer Numerical Control (CNC) | Multi-axis trajectory control |
| 6 | Sensor & Transducer Networks | Real-time feedback (force, position, vision, temperature) |
| 7 | SCADA / HMI Systems | Supervisory monitoring and human interface |
| 8 | Pneumatic & Hydraulic Actuators | Power delivery for mechanical motion |
| 9 | IoT / Industrial Communication (IIoT) | Machine-to-machine data exchange |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Automated Chef" Kitchen**
> Imagine a high-end restaurant kitchen where a single chef (the human operator) supervises a team of specialized assistants. One assistant **measures ingredients with a digital scale** (sensors), another **chops vegetables with robotic arms** (robotics), a third **inspects the plating through a camera** (vision systems), and a central computer **decides the recipe and timing** (AI + PLC). The chef no longer chops, measures, or inspects — he just **monitors and makes high-level decisions**. This is exactly how an SPM works: each automation technology is a "specialist assistant" coordinated by a central controller.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants)

- **Repeatability (R):** ±0.02 mm to ±0.05 mm for industrial robots (per **ISO 9283**).
- **Positioning Accuracy (AP):** Typically **2 to 10 times worse** than repeatability.
- **Vision System Resolution:** measured in **pixels per mm (pixel/mm)**, typically **0.05 mm/pixel** to **0.5 mm/pixel**.
- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** Industrial robots generally have **4 to 6 DOF**.
- **Cycle Time (T_c):** Time per operation, typically **5 to 60 seconds** in SPMs.
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** Modern automation systems target **≥ 20,000 hours**.

## 1.4 Why Automation Technologies Matter in SPMs

A Special Purpose Machine is purpose-built for **one or a family of similar operations**. Without automation technologies, an SPM degenerates into a manual fixture. The marriage of mechanical structure (the "body") with automation technologies (the "nervous system + brain") transforms a static jig into a **cyber-physical production system (CPPS)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Whenever a question asks *"List the automation technologies used in SPMs"*, never answer with just one or two. The KTU examiner expects a **classified, hierarchical listing** with **at least 6 to 8 technologies** with their **role** in the SPM context.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Automation Technologies in SPMs

### A. Robotics in SPMs

An **industrial robot** in an SPM context is a reprogrammable, multifunctional manipulator designed to move parts, tools, or specialized devices through variable programmed motions for the performance of a variety of tasks.

**Key Subsystems:**
1. **Mechanical Structure (Manipulator):** Links + Joints → forms the kinematic chain.
2. **Actuators:** Servo motors (electrical), hydraulic cylinders, pneumatic pistons.
3. **End Effector:** Gripper, welding torch, paint sprayer, suction cup.
4. **Controller:** Executes the motion program (e.g., teach pendant, offline programming).
5. **Sensors:** Encoders, resolvers, force/torque sensors, proximity switches.

**Robot Configurations Used in SPMs:**

| **Configuration** | **Typical DOF** | **SPM Application** |
|-------------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| Cartesian (Gantry) | 3 | Pick & place, palletizing |
| Cylindrical | 4 | Assembly, machine tending |
| Polar (Spherical) | 5 | (Legacy) die casting, forging |
| Articulated (6-axis) | 6 | Welding, painting, complex assembly |
| SCARA | 4 | High-speed PCB assembly, pick & place |
| Delta (Parallel) | 3–4 | Ultra-high-speed packaging |

### B. Machine Vision Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU syllabus):** A machine vision system is a non-contact, opto-electronic sensing arrangement that captures, digitizes, and processes images to extract quantitative information (dimensions, position, defects, color) for automated decision-making.

**Stages of a Vision System:**
1. **Image Acquisition:** Lighting + Lens + Camera (CCD/CMOS).
2. **Image Processing:** Filtering, edge detection, segmentation.
3. **Feature Extraction:** Blob analysis, pattern matching, OCR.
4. **Interpretation & Decision:** Pass/Fail, coordinate output to robot.

**Key Performance Equations:**

The **spatial resolution** of a vision system is given by:

$$
R_v = \frac{FOV}{N_{pixels}}
$$

where $FOV$ is the field of view (mm) and $N_{pixels}$ is the number of pixels along that dimension.

The **measurement accuracy** depends on sub-pixel algorithms and is typically:

$$
A_v = k_v \cdot R_v
$$

where $k_v$ is the **sub-pixel accuracy factor**, usually $k_v = 0.1$ to $0.5$ for calibrated systems.

### C. Artificial Intelligence (AI) in SPMs

AI in SPMs is **not** general AI — it is **narrow, domain-specific AI** typically realized through:
- **Expert Systems (Rule-based):** IF-THEN rules for fault diagnosis.
- **Fuzzy Logic Controllers:** Handle imprecise sensor inputs.
- **Artificial Neural Networks (ANN):** For pattern recognition, predictive maintenance.
- **Machine Learning (ML):** Quality prediction from process parameters.
- **Genetic Algorithms (GA):** Optimization of cutting parameters, tool paths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry 4.0 Context:** In modern SPMs, AI is paired with **IIoT sensors** to create a **Digital Twin** — a virtual replica of the physical machine used for simulation, monitoring, and predictive maintenance.

### D. PLCs and CNC — The Control Backbone

- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):** Uses **Ladder Logic (LD)**, **Function Block Diagram (FBD)**, or **Structured Text (ST)**. Scan time typically **5 to 20 ms**.
- **CNC (Computer Numerical Control):** Executes **G-codes and M-codes**. Interpolates multi-axis trajectories at **feed rates up to 30 m/min**.

### E. Sensor Networks

| **Sensor Type** | **Measured Variable** | **SPM Application** |
|-----------------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Inductive Proximity | Presence of metal | Part detection |
| Photoelectric | Presence (any material) | Counting, indexing |
| Vision (2D/3D) | Geometry, color, position | Inspection, guidance |
| Force/Torque | Force, moment | Robotic assembly, deburring |
| Laser Triangulation | Distance (µm level) | Thickness, profile |
| Encoder (Optical) | Angular/Rotational position | Servo feedback |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** | **Engineering Use** |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------|---------------------|
| $$R_v = \frac{FOV}{N_{pixels}}$$ | Vision system resolution | mm/pixel | Camera-lens selection |
| $$A_v = k_v \cdot R_v$$ | Vision measurement accuracy | mm | Inspection tolerance check |
| $$DOF = n \cdot (m - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{n}(f_i - 1)$$ | Kutzbach–Grübler mobility equation | dimensionless | Robot DOF verification |
| $$AP = R + B_s$$ | Positioning accuracy = Repeatability + Systematic bias | mm | Robot selection |
| $$T_c = T_{load} + T_{process} + T_{unload} + T_{index}$$ | SPM total cycle time | seconds | Throughput calculation |
| $$OEE = A \cdot P \cdot Q$$ | Overall Equipment Effectiveness | fraction (0–1) | SPM efficiency KPI |
| $$\Delta R = \frac{R_{max} - R_{min}}{2}$$ | Symmetric tolerance band | mm | Repeatability specification |
| $$W_{payload} \le \frac{W_{rated}}{SF}$$ | Payload safety check | kg or N | Robot sizing |

> [!TIP]
> The **Kutzbach–Grübler mobility equation** is a high-yield topic. $n$ = number of links, $m$ = number of joints, $f_i$ = DOF of $i$-th joint (1 for revolute/prismatic, 2 for cylindrical, 3 for spherical).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive SPMs:** Robots for spot welding (6-axis articulated, ±0.1 mm repeatability) + vision for seam tracking.
- **Electronics SPMs:** SCARA robots for PCB assembly + AI-based AOI (Automated Optical Inspection).
- **Pharmaceutical SPMs:** Delta robots for blister packaging + vision for label verification.
- **Aerospace SPMs:** Large gantry robots for drilling/riveting + force feedback for composite layup.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Vision System Resolution from First Principles

> **Problem Context:** A vision system inspects a part with a Field of View (FOV) of 100 mm × 80 mm. The camera has a 1280 × 1024 pixel sensor. Determine the spatial resolution and the minimum detectable defect size.

### Step 1: Identify the governing principle

The spatial resolution of a vision system is fundamentally limited by the **pixel size projected onto the object plane**. This is obtained by dividing the physical field of view by the number of pixels covering that field.

### Step 2: Compute the horizontal resolution

$$
R_{v,x} = \frac{FOV_x}{N_{x}} = \frac{100\ \text{mm}}{1280\ \text{pixels}}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
R_{v,x} = 0.078125\ \text{mm/pixel}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the vertical resolution

$$
R_{v,y} = \frac{FOV_y}{N_{y}} = \frac{80\ \text{mm}}{1024\ \text{pixels}}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
R_{v,y} = 0.078125\ \text{mm/pixel}
$$

### Step 4: Interpret the result

Since $R_{v,x} = R_{v,y}$, the system has **isotropic resolution** (square pixels), which is the ideal case for metrology. The minimum detectable defect is approximately **one pixel** wide, i.e., **≈ 0.078 mm = 78 µm**.

### Step 5: Apply sub-pixel accuracy factor

For a calibrated system with $k_v = 0.2$:

$$
A_v = k_v \cdot R_v = 0.2 \times 0.078125 = 0.015625\ \text{mm}
$$

So the measurement accuracy is **≈ 15.6 µm**, well-suited for precision inspection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** Divide mm by pixels → mm/pixel. Multiply by $k_v$ (dimensionless) → mm.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Robot DOF Using Kutzbach–Grübler Equation

> **Problem Context:** An articulated 6-axis robot is to be analyzed. It has 6 links and 6 revolute joints. Each revolute joint has 1 DOF. Determine the mobility (DOF) of the manipulator.

### Step 1: Recall the Kutzbach–Grübler equation for spatial mechanisms

$$
DOF = 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{n}(6 - f_i)
$$

where:
- $n$ = number of links (including ground)
- $f_i$ = DOF of joint $i$

### Step 2: Identify the parameters

For a 6-axis articulated robot:
- Number of links: $n = 7$ (6 moving links + 1 ground/base link)
- Each revolute joint has $f_i = 1$ DOF
- Number of joints: 6

### Step 3: Compute the joint term

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{6}(6 - f_i) = \sum_{i=1}^{6}(6 - 1) = 6 \times 5 = 30
$$

### Step 4: Substitute into the mobility equation

$$
DOF = 6(7 - 1) - 30 = 6 \times 6 - 30 = 36 - 30
$$

$$
DOF = 6
$$

### Step 5: Interpret

The 6-axis articulated robot has **6 DOF** — three for **position** (X, Y, Z) and three for **orientation** (roll, pitch, yaw). This confirms the standard classification.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion logic:** $f_i$ counts the independent motions allowed at each joint. Revolute = 1, Prismatic = 1, Cylindrical = 2, Spherical = 3, Planar = 3.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SPM Cycle Time and Throughput

> **Problem Context:** An automated assembly SPM performs the following operations: load (3 s), clamp (1.5 s), drill (4 s), inspect (2 s), unload (2.5 s). Determine total cycle time and hourly throughput.

### Step 1: Sum all sequential operations

$$
T_c = T_{load} + T_{clamp} + T_{drill} + T_{inspect} + T_{unload}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute numerical values

$$
T_c = 3.0 + 1.5 + 4.0 + 2.0 + 2.5
$$

### Step 3: Evaluate

$$
T_c = 13.0\ \text{seconds}
$$

### Step 4: Compute the throughput (parts per hour)

$$
\text{Throughput} = \frac{3600}{T_c} = \frac{3600}{13.0}
$$

$$
\text{Throughput} = 276.9\ \text{parts/hour} \approx 277\ \text{PPH}
$$

### Step 5: Account for OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)

If $A = 0.95$, $P = 0.90$, $Q = 0.99$:

$$
OEE = 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.99 = 0.84645 \approx 84.6\%
$$

Effective throughput:

$$
T_{eff} = T \times OEE = 276.9 \times 0.84645 = 234.4\ \text{good parts/hour}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Technology Selection for SPMs

| **SPM Function** | **Recommended Technology** | **Justification** | **Typical Spec** |
|------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| High-speed pick & place | Delta robot + Vision | Parallel kinematics, low inertia | 100–300 picks/min |
| Precision assembly | SCARA + Force sensor | High vertical stiffness, compliance control | ±0.02 mm repeatability |
| Arc welding | 6-axis articulated + Seam tracking vision | Dexterity, multi-orientation torch | ±0.1 mm path accuracy |
| Surface inspection | 2D/3D vision + AI classifier | Non-contact, fast, data-rich | 0.05 mm/pixel |
| Palletizing | Cartesian gantry or 6-axis + PLC | High payload, large workspace | 50–500 kg payload |
| Drilling / Riveting | Gantry robot + CNC + Force feedback | Long reach, multi-axis interpolation | ±0.05 mm |
| Part identification | Vision + Barcode/QR reader | Traceability | 100% reading accuracy |
| Process optimization | AI / ML + IIoT sensors | Adaptive control, predictive maintenance | OEE > 85% |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Special Purpose Machine (SPM) and list any six automation technologies used in SPMs.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Special Purpose Machine (SPM)** is a custom-designed, single-purpose automated machine built to perform a specific manufacturing, assembly, or inspection operation on a dedicated part or family of parts at high speed and repeatability.

**Six automation technologies used in SPMs:**

1. **Industrial Robotics** — for material handling, welding, painting, assembly.
2. **Machine Vision Systems** — for inspection, guidance, and defect detection.
3. **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)** — for sequencing and logic control.
4. **CNC Systems** — for multi-axis trajectory control.
5. **Sensor and Transducer Networks** — for real-time feedback (position, force, vision).
6. **Artificial Intelligence / Expert Systems** — for adaptive decision-making and predictive maintenance.

### **Question 2: What is the difference between Repeatability and Positioning Accuracy of an industrial robot?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| **Parameter** | **Repeatability (R)** | **Positioning Accuracy (AP)** |
|---------------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| Definition | Ability to return to a previously taught position | Ability to move to a commanded position from a known reference |
| Measured as | Scatter of positions about the mean | Deviation from the target position |
| Typical value | ±0.02 mm to ±0.05 mm | ±0.1 mm to ±0.5 mm |
| Direction | Statistical (random errors) | Systematic + random |
| Relation | Always better than accuracy | $AP \ge R$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Many students write $R = AP$ or invert the inequality. Remember: **Repeatability is always better (numerically smaller) than Positioning Accuracy** because accuracy includes systematic biases that repeatability does not.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: Option 1**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]`

**Q. (a)** Explain in detail the **classification of industrial robots** based on configuration. Discuss the role of each type in SPM applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **working of a machine vision system** used for automated inspection in an SPM. Derive the expression for **spatial resolution**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(a):**

**Introduction:**
Industrial robots are classified based on their **kinematic configuration** (i.e., the arrangement of joints and the type of coordinate system they operate in).

**Classification:**

**1. Cartesian Robot (Gantry / XYZ):**
- Three prismatic joints along X, Y, Z axes.
- Workspace is a rectangular box.
- **SPM Application:** Pick & place of heavy parts, palletizing, CNC machine tending.
- **Advantage:** High payload capacity, simple kinematics.
- **Disadvantage:** Large floor space.

**2. Cylindrical Robot:**
- One revolute joint (base rotation) + two prismatic joints (vertical and radial).
- Workspace is a cylinder.
- **SPM Application:** Assembly of small parts, machine loading.

**3. Polar (Spherical) Robot:**
- Two revolute joints + one prismatic joint.
- Workspace is a spherical shell.
- **SPM Application:** Legacy die-casting, forging, foundries.

**4. Articulated Robot (6-axis / Jointed-arm):**
- All revolute joints (typically 6).
- Workspace is a sphere of complex shape.
- **SPM Application:** Arc welding, painting, complex assembly.
- **Most widely used** in modern SPMs.

**5. SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm):**
- Two revolute joints (planar) + one prismatic joint (vertical) + one rotational joint.
- **SPM Application:** High-speed PCB assembly, vertical insertion tasks.
- **Advantage:** High vertical stiffness, horizontal compliance.

**6. Delta (Parallel) Robot:**
- Three arms connected to a common base via parallel linkages.
- **SPM Application:** Ultra-high-speed packaging, food industry pick & place.
- **Advantage:** Up to 300 picks/min.

**Conclusion:**
The choice of robot configuration depends on **payload, workspace, speed, repeatability, and cost** of the specific SPM application.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(b):**

**Block Diagram:** Refer to the Machine Vision System diagram in Section 4.2.

**Working of a Machine Vision System:**

**Step 1 — Image Acquisition:**
The workpiece is illuminated by a controlled light source (LED ring, bar light, or backlight). The reflected (or transmitted) light passes through an **optical lens** that focuses the image onto a **CCD or CMOS image sensor**.

**Step 2 — Digitization:**
The analog signal from the sensor is converted to a digital image (a 2D matrix of pixel intensity values) by the **frame grabber** or onboard ADC.

**Step 3 — Image Processing:**
Digital filters remove noise. **Edge detection** algorithms (Sobel, Canny) and **thresholding** extract the features of interest.

**Step 4 — Feature Extraction:**
**Blob analysis** identifies connected regions, **pattern matching** compares against templates, and **OCR** reads text/numbers.

**Step 5 — Decision and Output:**
Based on the extracted features, the system outputs:
- **Pass/Fail** signal to a PLC for part rejection.
- **X, Y, Θ coordinates** for robot guidance.

**Derivation of Spatial Resolution:**

Let the **Field of View (FOV)** along one axis be $L$ (in mm) and the number of pixels along that axis be $N$. The spatial resolution is the physical length represented by one pixel:

$$
R_v = \frac{L}{N} = \frac{FOV}{N_{pixels}}
$$

This is the **smallest feature** that can be reliably resolved. For sub-pixel accuracy, a calibrated system uses interpolation to achieve:

$$
A_v = k_v \cdot R_v
$$

where $k_v$ is typically between 0.1 and 0.5.

**Conclusion:**
Machine vision systems are the "eyes" of modern SPMs, enabling 100% non-contact inspection at high speeds with micron-level accuracy.

---

### **Question B: Option 2**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the **role of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in Special Purpose Machines**. Discuss expert systems, fuzzy logic, and neural networks with SPM examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Q. (b)** A vision system is used to inspect a workpiece with a **Field of View of 60 mm × 45 mm** using a camera with **1024 × 768 pixels**. Determine the **(i) spatial resolution** and **(ii) minimum detectable defect size** assuming a sub-pixel accuracy factor of $k_v = 0.25$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(a):**

**Introduction:**
AI in SPMs refers to **domain-specific computational intelligence** that enables machines to make decisions, learn from data, and adapt to variations — without explicit programming for every scenario.

**1. Expert Systems:**
- **Principle:** Rule-based reasoning using **IF-THEN-ELSE** rules derived from human expert knowledge.
- **SPM Example:** A drilling SPM where the expert system monitors motor current; **IF** current > threshold **THEN** retract drill and signal tool wear.
- **Advantage:** Explainable decisions.
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot handle unseen scenarios.

**2. Fuzzy Logic Controllers (FLC):**
- **Principle:** Handles **imprecise inputs** using membership functions and linguistic rules (e.g., "high force", "medium speed").
- **SPM Example:** A robotic deburring SPM where the robot adjusts feed rate based on fuzzy rules like:
  - **IF** force is **high** **THEN** feed rate is **low**.
- **Advantage:** Robust to sensor noise and modeling errors.

**3. Artificial Neural Networks (ANN):**
- **Principle:** Learns complex non-linear mappings from **input-output data** through weighted interconnections (neurons).
- **SPM Example:** An Automated Optical Inspection (AOI) system that classifies weld defects (porosity, undercut, cracks) from image features.
- **Advantage:** Handles high-dimensional data, generalizes well.
- **Disadvantage:** Requires large training datasets, "black box" behavior.

**4. Machine Learning & Predictive Maintenance:**
- **Principle:** ML algorithms (Random Forest, SVM, Deep Learning) predict tool wear, part quality, and machine failures.
- **SPM Example:** Predicting spindle bearing failure 2 weeks in advance using vibration + temperature + current data.

**5. Genetic Algorithms (GA):**
- **Principle:** Optimization through evolution-inspired operators (selection, crossover, mutation).
- **SPM Example:** Optimizing **cutting parameters (speed, feed, depth)** for minimum cycle time in a CNC-integrated SPM.

**Conclusion:**
AI transforms SPMs from **rigid, pre-programmed machines** into **adaptive, learning, self-optimizing cyber-physical systems** — a key enabler of Industry 4.0 and smart manufacturing.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Field of View: $FOV_x = 60\ \text{mm}$, $FOV_y = 45\ \text{mm}$
- Camera resolution: $N_x = 1024$, $N_y = 768$
- Sub-pixel accuracy factor: $k_v = 0.25$

**Step 1: Compute the horizontal spatial resolution**

$$
R_{v,x} = \frac{FOV_x}{N_x} = \frac{60}{1024}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
R_{v,x} = 0.05859375\ \text{mm/pixel}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the vertical spatial resolution**

$$
R_{v,y} = \frac{FOV_y}{N_y} = \frac{45}{768}
$$

Evaluating:

$$
R_{v,y} = 0.05859375\ \text{mm/pixel}
$$

**Observation:** $R_{v,x} = R_{v,y}$, confirming **isotropic square pixels** — a desired property.

**Step 3: Report the spatial resolution**

$$
R_v = 0.0586\ \text{mm/pixel} \approx 58.6\ \mu\text{m/pixel}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the minimum detectable defect size (one pixel)**

$$
d_{min} \approx R_v = 0.0586\ \text{mm} \approx 58.6\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 5: Compute the measurement accuracy using sub-pixel factor**

$$
A_v = k_v \cdot R_v = 0.25 \times 0.0586
$$

$$
A_v = 0.01465\ \text{mm} \approx 14.65\ \mu\text{m}
$$

**Step 6: Final Interpretation**

- The system can detect defects as small as **~58.6 µm** (one pixel wide).
- With sub-pixel processing, the measurement accuracy is **~14.65 µm**, suitable for precision inspection of small components.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall (Valuation Warning):**
> - Do not confuse **resolution** (one pixel) with **accuracy** (sub-pixel). These are different concepts.
> - Always state **both** the horizontal and vertical resolutions.
> - The unit must be **mm/pixel** for resolution and **mm** for the defect size. Mixing units will cost marks.
> - Do not forget to mention whether pixels are **isotropic** (square) or **anisotropic** (rectangular) — this is a frequently missed point.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Bullet Points for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ⭐ **SPM** = dedicated, custom-built machine for one/few operations with **high automation**.
- ⭐ Automation technologies in SPMs = **Robotics + Vision + AI + PLC + CNC + Sensors + IIoT**.
- ⭐ **Industrial robot configurations:** Cartesian, Cylindrical, Polar, Articulated, SCARA, Delta.
- ⭐ **Articulated robot** (6-axis) is the **most versatile** and most used in SPMs.
- ⭐ **SCARA** = high vertical stiffness, ideal for **PCB assembly**.
- ⭐ **Delta** = parallel kinematics, **highest speed**, used in packaging.
- ⭐ **Vision resolution:** $R_v = FOV / N_{pixels}$ (in **mm/pixel**).
- ⭐ **Sub-pixel accuracy:** $A_v = k_v \cdot R_v$ with $k_v \in [0.1, 0.5]$.
- ⭐ **Repeatability < Positioning Accuracy** (numerically; always).
- ⭐ **DOF of 6-axis robot** = 6 (3 position + 3 orientation) — confirmed by Kutzbach–Grübler.
- ⭐ **Kutzbach–Grübler equation:** $DOF = 6(n-1) - \sum(6 - f_i)$ for spatial mechanisms.
- ⭐ **AI types in SPMs:** Expert systems, Fuzzy logic, ANN, ML, GA.
- ⭐ **AI applications:** Predictive maintenance, quality classification, parameter optimization.
- ⭐ **OEE** = Availability × Performance × Quality (max = 1 or 100%).
- ⭐ **Cycle time** = sum of all sequential operations; **Throughput** = 3600 / $T_c$.
- ⭐ **Digital Twin** = virtual replica of SPM for simulation and predictive analytics.
- ⭐ **IIoT** = Industrial Internet of Things; backbone of Industry 4.0.
- ⭐ **Cyber-Physical Production System (CPPS)** = SPM + sensors + AI + connectivity.
- ⭐ Sensor types: Inductive, Photoelectric, Vision, Force/Torque, Laser, Encoder.
- ⭐ **PLC** uses **Ladder Logic (LD)**, scan time 5–20 ms.
- ⭐ **CNC** uses **G-codes and M-codes** for trajectory interpolation.
- ⭐ **ISO 9283** is the standard for industrial robot performance testing.
- ⭐ Vision systems enable **100% non-contact inspection** at production-line speeds.
- ⭐ AI-based AOI replaces manual visual inspection in modern SPMs.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Automation Technologies in SPMs**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming and Control - Writing and Implementing Control Algorithms, Interfacing with PLCs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT601 - SPECIAL PURPOSE MACHINES FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Programming and Control - Writing and Implementing Control Algorithms, Interfacing with PLCs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Algorithm (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *control algorithm* is a deterministic, mathematically defined sequence of logical and arithmetic operations executed by a programmable automation controller (PAC) or Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) that continuously samples input process variables, compares them against a defined setpoint, and generates corrective output commands to actuators such that the Special Purpose Machine (SPM) achieves the desired operational envelope (position, velocity, force, torque, or temperature) within specified transient and steady-state tolerances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC – IEC 61131-1):** A digitally operating electronic system, designed for use in an industrial environment, which uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of user-oriented instructions to implement specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations, to control, through digital or analog inputs and outputs, various types of machines or processes.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned) framework, **PCCPT601** treats the PLC and the embedded control algorithm as the *central nervous system* of every modern Special Purpose Machine — from a CNC transfer line and an automated assembly cell to a robotic welding station and a vision-guided pick-and-place unit.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Reflex" Model

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway and you want to maintain exactly **100 km/h** (the setpoint).

- **Sensor (Input):** Your eyes read the speedometer.
- **Controller (Algorithm):** Your brain computes: *“I am at 92 km/h, so I must press the accelerator a little harder. I am at 108 km/h, so I must release it.”*
- **Actuator (Output):** Your foot moves the accelerator pedal.
- **Feedback Loop:** The new speed is sensed, and the brain re-evaluates continuously.

A **PLC + Control Algorithm** in a Special Purpose Machine behaves **exactly** the same way — only it is faster (milliseconds), tireless (24/7), and deterministic (repeatable to microseconds).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Pedagogical Insight:** In a Special Purpose Machine, the mechanical hardware (slide, spindle, gripper) is the *body*, while the **control algorithm + PLC** form the *brain and reflexes*. A well-designed algorithm can compensate for mediocre mechanics, but brilliant mechanics cannot overcome a poor algorithm.

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics to Remember

The following bolded metrics are the universally accepted Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) used by KTU examiners, plant managers, and industry (Siemens, Allen-Bradley, Mitsubishi, Fanuc) when grading or evaluating any SPM control system:

- **Scan Time of a PLC: typically 5 ms – 20 ms per 1 K of logic** (IEC 61131-3 standard).
- **PID Update Rate: 1 ms – 100 ms** depending on the process dynamics.
- **Typical PLC I/O Voltages: 24 V DC (sourcing/sinking) and 120/230 V AC (relay outputs).**
- **Industrial Communication Cycle Time: EtherCAT ≈ 100 µs, PROFINET IRT ≈ 1 ms, Modbus TCP ≈ 10–50 ms.**

These are the *yardsticks* examiners expect students to quote in **Part A** 3-mark answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Control Algorithms for SPMs

Control algorithms for Special Purpose Machines are classified along three independent axes. Mastery of this matrix is **high-yield** for KTU questions worth 7–14 marks.

| # | Classification Axis | Sub-Types | Typical SPM Application |
|---|---------------------|-----------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **By Loop Topology** | Open-loop, Closed-loop (Feedback), Feed-forward | Drilling cycle vs. CNC servo drive |
| 2 | **By Time Behaviour** | Continuous, Discrete (sampled), Sequential, Event-driven | Conveyor speed control vs. indexing table |
| 3 | **By Control Law** | ON-OFF (Two-position), Proportional (P), PI, PD, PID, State-space, Fuzzy, Adaptive | Heater band vs. hydraulic ram vs. robot arm |

### 2.2 The PID Control Law — The Heartbeat of SPM Automation

The vast majority (> **70%** of industrial control loops) on Special Purpose Machines use some form of the **PID controller**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects the candidate to derive, interpret, and tune the PID law.

**Continuous-Time PID Equation (ideal / parallel form):**

$$u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,e(t) \;+\; K_{i}\!\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ = controller output (e.g., 0 – 10 V to a variable-frequency drive)
- $e(t) = SP - PV$ = setpoint minus process variable (error)
- $K_{p}$ = Proportional gain (dimensionless or V/unit)
- $K_{i} = K_{p}/T_{i}$ = Integral gain (1/s)
- $K_{d} = K_{p} \cdot T_{d}$ = Derivative gain (s)
- $T_{i}$ = Integral time (reset time) in seconds
- $T_{d}$ = Derivative time (rate time) in seconds

**The discrete (PLC-executable) form** — what is *actually* written in Structured Text or Ladder:

$$u[k] \;=\; K_{p}\,e[k] \;+\; K_{i}\,T_{s}\!\sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] \;+\; K_{d}\,\frac{e[k]-e[k-1]}{T_{s}}$$

Where $T_{s}$ is the **sampling period** of the PLC scan.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below is the **only** set of equations a student must memorize for Module 3 of PCCPT601. Every line is examinable. Note the use of `\vert` (not the vertical bar `\vert`) to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Equation / Identity | Physical Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---------------------|------------------|--------------|
| 1 | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Error signal | Engineering unit (mm, °C, bar) |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_{p}e(t) + K_{i}\!\int e\,dt + K_{d}\,\dot{e}$ | Ideal parallel PID | V or mA |
| 3 | $G_{c}(s) = K_{p}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{T_{i}s} + T_{d}s\right)$ | PID transfer function | V/unit |
| 4 | $T_{s} \le \dfrac{\tau_{dom}}{10}$ | Nyquist-based sampling rule | s |
| 5 | $K_{p}^{*}=0.6\,K_{u}$, $T_{i}^{*}=0.5\,T_{u}$, $T_{d}^{*}=0.125\,T_{u}$ | Ziegler-Nichols PI\*D tuning | dimensionless |
| 6 | $T_{scan} = T_{input} + T_{execute} + T_{output} + T_{housekeeping}$ | PLC scan time | ms |
| 7 | $\Delta u = K_{p}\!\left(e[k]-e[k-1]\right) + K_{i}\,T_{s}\,e[k] + \dfrac{K_{d}}{T_{s}}\!\left(e[k]-2e[k-1]+e[k-2]\right)$ | Velocity (incremental) PID | V per scan |
| 8 | $f_{Nyquist} = \dfrac{1}{2\,T_{s}}$ | Shannon sampling limit | Hz |
| 9 | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$, $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$ | 2nd-order plant natural frequency & damping ratio | rad/s, dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Max \%OS} = 100\,e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ | Percent overshoot | % |
| 11 | $K_{pu} = 4\,h/\!\left(\pi\,A\right)$ | Relay-feedback ultimate gain (Åström–Hägglund) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $I_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\!\int_{0}^{T} i^{2}(t)\,dt}$ | RMS current for motor sizing | A |

### 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Real SPM Industries

- **Equations 1–3** are the foundation of *every* CNC servo, hydraulic axis, and temperature controller on a special purpose machine.
- **Equation 4** tells the controls engineer how fast the PLC must sample. A 50 ms dominant time-constant requires $T_{s} \le 5$ ms.
- **Equation 6** is used to determine whether the chosen PLC (e.g., Siemens S7-1200, Allen-Bradley MicroLogix 1400) can execute the program fast enough.
- **Equation 7** is *the* algorithm written inside a PLC's PID function block (e.g., FB 41 "CONT_C" in Siemens Step 7, PIDE in Rockwell Studio 5000).
- **Equation 12** is the heating limit in motor windings and is the difference between a green and a burnt-out coil.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In a typical KTU-style 14-mark question, the examiner expects a student to (a) state the PID equation, (b) convert it to a discrete form, (c) explain how the Ziegler-Nichols tuning rule determines $K_{p}, T_{i}, T_{d}$, and (d) comment on the choice of $T_{s}$ using Equation 4.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Continuous-Time PID to Discrete-Time (PLC-Executable) Form

This derivation is **guaranteed to appear** in KTU University Examinations, usually as a 7-mark sub-part.

**Step 1 — Start from the continuous-time ideal PID law.**

$$u(t) \;=\; K_{p}\,e(t) \;+\; K_{i}\!\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;+\; K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

*Explanation:* This is the *ideal parallel* form, where each of the three terms is computed independently and summed. It is preferred in academic study because each term has a clear physical meaning (present, past, future).

**Step 2 — Approximate the integral by the rectangular (Euler-forward) rule.**

For a sample at instant $k$ with sampling period $T_{s}$, the integral becomes:

$$\int_{0}^{t_{k}} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_{s}\!\sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]$$

*Explanation:* We replace the smooth area under the error curve by a staircase of rectangles, each of width $T_{s}$ and height equal to the error sample at that instant. This is the simplest, computationally cheapest, and most commonly used in low-cost PLCs.

**Step 3 — Approximate the derivative by the backward-difference rule.**

The first derivative of error is approximated as the change in error over the sampling interval:

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg\rvert_{t=k\,T_{s}} \;\approx\; \frac{e[k]-e[k-1]}{T_{s}}$$

*Explanation:* We use *backward* differencing (not central differencing) because it is causal — it depends only on the current and past samples, never on a future sample. Causality is a hard requirement for any real-time controller.

**Step 4 — Substitute Steps 2 and 3 into Step 1.**

$$u[k] \;=\; K_{p}\,e[k] \;+\; K_{i}\,T_{s}\!\sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j] \;+\; K_{d}\,\frac{e[k]-e[k-1]}{T_{s}}$$

*Explanation:* This is the **positional (absolute) discrete PID**. The PLC computes the *full* control signal $u[k]$ at every scan. Easy to understand, but suffers from *integral windup* if the actuator saturates.

**Step 5 — Derive the velocity (incremental) form to eliminate integral windup.**

Compute $u[k-1]$ at the previous scan and subtract:

$$u[k-1] \;=\; K_{p}\,e[k-1] \;+\; K_{i}\,T_{s}\!\sum_{j=0}^{k-1} e[j] \;+\; K_{d}\,\frac{e[k-1]-e[k-2]}{T_{s}}$$

Therefore the **change** in output (which is what the PLC actually sends to the actuator) is:

$$\Delta u[k] \;=\; u[k] - u[k-1]$$

Expanding and cancelling the integral summation, the closed-form result is:

$$\Delta u[k] \;=\; K_{p}\!\left(e[k]-e[k-1]\right) + K_{i}\,T_{s}\,e[k] + \frac{K_{d}}{T_{s}}\!\left(e[k]-2\,e[k-1]+e[k-2]\right)$$

*Explanation:* This is the **velocity-form PID** (also called *delta-form* or *incremental* PID). Its primary advantage is *bumpless transfer* and *anti-windup*: if the actuator is saturated, the algorithm naturally clamps the increment. This is the form used in Siemens FB 41, Rockwell PIDE, and Mitsubishi QD75 motion blocks.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Ziegler-Nichols Ultimate-Cycle Tuning (Closed-Loop Method)

**Step 1 — Reduce the controller to pure proportional (set $T_{i}\to\infty$, $T_{d}\to 0$).**

$$u(t) = K_{p}\,e(t)$$

*Explanation:* We disable the I and D actions and apply only a proportional gain.

**Step 2 — Gradually increase $K_{p}$ until the process output oscillates with a constant amplitude (sustained oscillation).**

The gain at which this happens is the **ultimate gain** $K_{u}$, and the period of oscillation is the **ultimate period** $T_{u}$.

*Explanation:* At this point, the closed-loop system is on the verge of instability (gain margin = 0 dB, phase margin = 0°). The plant has a phase lag of $-180°$ at frequency $\omega_{u} = 2\pi/T_{u}$.

**Step 3 — Read off $K_{u}$ and $T_{u}$ from the recorder or HMI trend.**

*Explanation:* Typical values for a hydraulic axis of an SPM: $K_{u} = 8.5$, $T_{u} = 1.2$ s.

**Step 4 — Apply the Ziegler-Nichols tuning rule (quarter-amplitude-damping criterion).**

For a PID controller:

$$K_{p} = 0.6\,K_{u} \quad\quad T_{i} = 0.5\,T_{u} \quad\quad T_{d} = 0.125\,T_{u}$$

*Explanation:* The coefficients 0.6, 0.5, and 0.125 were obtained empirically by Ziegler and Nichols in 1942 to give a *quarter-amplitude decay ratio* — a step disturbance dies out with each successive overshoot being 1/4 of the previous one. For $K_{u} = 8.5$, $T_{u} = 1.2$ s: $K_{p} = 5.1$, $T_{i} = 0.6$ s, $T_{d} = 0.15$ s.

**Step 5 — Convert to actual controller gains.**

$$K_{i} = \frac{K_{p}}{T_{i}} \quad\quad K_{d} = K_{p}\,T_{d}$$

*Explanation:* For the numerical example: $K_{i} = 5.1/0.6 = 8.5\ \text{s}^{-1}$ and $K_{d} = 5.1 \times 0.15 = 0.765\ \text{s}$.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open Loop vs. Closed Loop Control

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the most-tested content in KTU Module 3 questions. Memorize at least four rows and the qualitative behaviour of each.

| # | Performance Criterion | Open-Loop Control | Closed-Loop (Feedback) Control |
|---|------------------------|--------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | **Steady-state accuracy** | Poor; depends on calibration only | High; error actively driven to zero |
| 2 | **Disturbance rejection** | None | Excellent (within bandwidth) |
| 3 | **Sensitivity to plant parameter drift** | High | Low (loop compensates) |
| 4 | **Stability concern** | None (no feedback) | Critical; can oscillate or diverge |
| 5 | **Hardware cost** | Lower (no sensor) | Higher (sensor + wiring + ADC) |
| 6 | **Example on an SPM** | Fixed-time drill cycle | Servo-positioned slide on CNC transfer line |
| 7 | **Transient response** | Not defined | Tunable via $K_{p}, T_{i}, T_{d}$ |
| 8 | **Mathematical model** | $Y(s) = G(s)\,U(s)$ | $Y(s) = \dfrac{G_{c}G}{1+G_{c}G\,H}\,R(s)$ |

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — PLC vs. Microcontroller vs. DCS vs. PC-Based Control

| # | Attribute | PLC | Microcontroller (e.g., Arduino) | DCS | Industrial PC |
|---|-----------|-----|----------------------------------|-----|----------------|
| 1 | Determinism | Hard real-time | Soft | Hard | Soft |
| 2 | I/O ruggedness | Industrial-grade (IP20) | Low | High | Medium |
| 3 | Programming language | Ladder, FBD, ST, SFC | C, MicroPython | Function blocks | C/C++, LabVIEW |
| 4 | Scan cycle | **5–20 ms** | < 1 ms (interrupt-driven) | 100–500 ms | Non-deterministic |
| 5 | Cost | Medium | Low | High | High |
| 6 | Use in SPM | **Universal** | Prototype / lab | Continuous processes | Vision & analytics |
| 7 | Lifespan | **15–20 years** | 3–5 years | 20+ years | 5–7 years |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Trap:** A frequent mistake is to claim a PLC is "just a computer". It is *not*. The PLC's cycle is *deterministic* — a property guaranteed by a real-time operating system and an isolated scan cycle, neither of which exists in a general-purpose PC. This distinction alone can earn 2–3 marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Closed-Loop SPM Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Sum Junction e=SP-PV]
    SUM --> CTRL[Control Algorithm PID in PLC]
    CTRL --> VFD[Power Amplifier VFD or Servo Drive]
    VFD --> ACT[Actuator Motor Cylinder Heater]
    ACT --> PLANT[SPM Mechanical Process]
    PLANT --> SENS[Sensor Encoder LVDT RTD]
    SENS --> ADC[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    ADC --> SUM
    PLANT --> OUT[Output PV Position Temperature Force]
```

*Explanation:* The setpoint is compared with the measured process variable to form the error. The PLC runs the PID algorithm, the VFD amplifies the low-power signal into high-current power for the actuator, and the sensor closes the loop via the ADC.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Modern PLC

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PSU[Power Supply 24V DC] --> CPU[CPU with Real Time OS]
    CPU --> MEM[Memory RAM ROM Flash]
    MEM --> PROG[User Program Ladder FBD ST]
    CPU --> DI[Digital Input Module]
    CPU --> DO[Digital Output Module]
    CPU --> AI[Analog Input Module 4-20mA]
    CPU --> AO[Analog Output Module 0-10V]
    CPU --> COMM[Communication Port Ethernet Profibus EtherCAT]
    DI --> FIELD1[Field Devices Limit Switches Push Buttons]
    DO --> FIELD2[Field Devices Relays Solenoids Contactors]
    AI --> FIELD3[Field Devices RTD Load Cell Pressure Tx]
    AO --> FIELD4[Field Devices VFD Proportional Valve]
```

*Explanation:* The power supply energizes the CPU and modules. The CPU executes the user program stored in memory, then exchanges data with discrete (DI/DO) and analog (AI/AO) modules, which are the gateway to the physical world. Industrial communication ports allow SCADA/HMI and peer-PLC networking.

### 4.3 Ladder Logic Schematic — Two-Hand Anti-Repeat Press Cycle (Safety Circuit)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PB1[Push Button 1 NO] --> XIC1[XIC PB1]
    PB2[Push Button 2 NO] --> XIC2[XIC PB2]
    XIC1 --> AND1[AND Condition]
    XIC2 --> AND1
    AND1 --> OTE1[OTE Permissive Coil M1]
    AND1 --> TON1[TON 0.5 s Bounce Filter]
    TON1 --> OTE2[OTE Press Solenoid Y1]
    AND1 --> RTO1[RTO Retentive Timer Cycle Counter]
    RTO1 --> XIO1[XIO Cycle Complete NC]
    AND1 --> GR1[Output Grounded through Safety Relay]
    GR1 --> LIM[Limit Switch Top Dead Centre X3]
    LIM --> RES[RTO Reset Coil]
```

*Explanation:* Two push-buttons must be pressed within 0.5 s of each other (two-hand safety requirement of ISO 13851). A retentive timer tracks the cycle, and a top-dead-centre limit switch resets the cycle counter, enforcing *anti-repeat* operation so the operator cannot trigger the press a second time by holding the buttons.

### 4.4 Sequential Function Chart (SFC / Grafcet) — Multi-Stage SPM Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    state Init: Step 0 Initialisation
    state S1: Step 1 Home All Axes
    state S2: Step 2 Load Part
    state S3: Step 3 Clamp Part
    state S4: Step 4 Machining
    state S5: Step 5 Unclamp
    state S6: Step 6 Unload to Conveyor
    Init --> S1 : Power On
    S1 --> S2 : All Home Sensors TRUE
    S2 --> S3 : Part Present Sensor TRUE
    S3 --> S4 : Clamp Sensor TRUE
    S4 --> S5 : Cycle Complete from CNC TRUE
    S5 --> S6 : Unclamp Sensor TRUE
    S6 --> S1 : Conveyor Acknowledge TRUE
```

*Explanation:* This is the Grafcet/SFC representation of a six-stage special purpose machine. Each step is a stable state; transitions occur only when the labelled boolean condition becomes TRUE. This is the IEC 61131-3 standard graphical language for sequential control.

### 4.5 Communication Stack — Field-Level Network Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ERP[ERP and MES Layer] --> SCADA[SCADA HMI Layer]
    SCADA --> CTRL1[Controller Layer PLC PAC]
    CTRL1 --> DEV1[Device Layer Sensors Actuators]
    CTRL1 --> DRV1[Drive Layer Servo VFD]
    CTRL1 --> ROB[Robot Controller]
    CTRL1 --> VISION[Vision System]
    CTRL1 -. EtherCAT 100us .-> DRV1
    CTRL1 -. PROFINET 1ms .-> ROB
    CTRL1 -. Modbus TCP 10ms .-> VISION
```

*Explanation:* The pyramid is read top-down as enterprise → control → field. The dotted annotations show the *cycle time* of each fieldbus — EtherCAT is the fastest, suitable for synchronized multi-axis motion, while Modbus TCP suffices for non-critical vision data.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define a *Programmable Logic Controller* as per IEC 61131-1. List the five standard programming languages specified by IEC 61131-3.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a digitally operating electronic system, designed for use in an industrial environment, which uses a programmable memory for the internal storage of user-oriented instructions to implement specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting and arithmetic, to control, through digital or analog inputs and outputs, various types of machines or processes (IEC 61131-1).

The **five IEC 61131-3 programming languages** are:
1. **Ladder Diagram (LD)** — graphical, derived from relay logic.
2. **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** — graphical, block-based signal flow.
3. **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** — graphical, for sequential processes.
4. **Structured Text (ST)** — high-level, Pascal-like.
5. **Instruction List (IL)** — low-level, assembly-like (deprecated in 3rd edition).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** With a neat sketch, explain the closed-loop block diagram of a position-controlled slide on a Special Purpose Machine. Identify each block.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The closed-loop block diagram of an SPM slide has six functional blocks:

1. **Setpoint (SP)** — the desired position (e.g., 120 mm) entered via HMI.
2. **Error detector / Summing junction** — computes $e = SP - PV$.
3. **Controller (PID algorithm in PLC)** — generates corrective command.
4. **Power amplifier (servo drive / VFD)** — boosts PLC signal to actuator power.
5. **Plant (actuator + mechanical slide + leadscrew)** — converts energy into motion.
6. **Feedback sensor (linear encoder / LVDT)** — measures actual position, closing the loop.

A neat ASCII block diagram earns the third mark: `SP → Σ → PID → Drive → Plant → Sensor → back to Σ`.

---

### PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]*

> **(a)** Derive the **discrete-time (positional) PID algorithm** from the continuous-time ideal PID law. Explain how Euler-forward integration and backward-difference differentiation make the algorithm *causal* and *PLC-executable*. **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A temperature control loop on an SPM plastic-injection unit has a 2nd-order plant transfer function $G(s) = \dfrac{1}{(20s+1)(5s+1)}$. Using the **Ziegler-Nichols open-loop method** (process reaction curve), determine $K_{p}, T_{i}, T_{d}$ and write the final discrete PID update equation. Assume a sampling time $T_{s} = 1\ \text{s}$. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to A(a):**

**Step 1 — State the continuous-time ideal PID law.**

$$u(t) = K_{p}\,e(t) + K_{i}\!\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Euler-forward integration to the I-term.**

$$\int_{0}^{t_{k}} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_{s}\sum_{j=0}^{k} e[j]$$

This converts the continuous integral into a discrete running sum of past error samples multiplied by the sampling period $T_{s}$.

**Step 3 — Apply backward-difference differentiation to the D-term.**

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg\rvert_{t=kT_{s}} \;\approx\; \frac{e[k]-e[k-1]}{T_{s}}$$

The backward difference uses only the *current* and *one past* sample — it never requires a future sample, hence the algorithm is *causal* and can be executed in real time on a PLC.

**Step 4 — Assemble the positional discrete PID.**

$$u[k] = K_{p}\,e[k] + K_{i}\,T_{s}\sum_{j=0}^{k}e[j] + \frac{K_{d}}{T_{s}}\!\left(e[k]-e[k-1]\right)$$

**Step 5 — State the engineering interpretation.**

- The **P-term** reacts to the *present* error.
- The **I-term** accumulates the *past* error (eliminates steady-state offset).
- The **D-term** anticipates the *future* trend (improves transient damping).
- The PLC computes this equation at every scan and writes $u[k]$ to the analog output module.

**Causality proof:** Each term on the right-hand side uses $e[k], e[k-1]$ only. No term depends on a sample with index $> k$. Therefore the algorithm can be evaluated in real time on a processor that has no knowledge of the future — exactly the constraint of every PLC.

---

**Model Solution to A(b):**

**Step 1 — Identify the process reaction curve parameters.**

For a 2nd-order overdamped plant of the form $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{(\tau_{1}s+1)(\tau_{2}s+1)}$, the *equivalent first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT)* approximation used by Ziegler-Nichols is:

$$K = \lim_{s\to 0}G(s) = 1 \quad\quad \tau = \tau_{1}+\tau_{2} = 25\ \text{s} \quad\quad L \approx 0.3\,\tau_{2} = 1.5\ \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Ziegler-Nichols open-loop tuning formula for a PID controller.**

$$K_{p} = \frac{1.2\,\tau}{K\,L} \quad\quad T_{i} = 2\,L \quad\quad T_{d} = 0.5\,L$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerical values.**

$$K_{p} = \frac{1.2 \times 25}{1 \times 1.5} = 20 \quad\quad T_{i} = 2 \times 1.5 = 3\ \text{s} \quad\quad T_{d} = 0.5 \times 1.5 = 0.75\ \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to actual gains $K_{i}$ and $K_{d}$.**

$$K_{i} = \frac{K_{p}}{T_{i}} = \frac{20}{3} = 6.667\ \text{s}^{-1} \quad\quad K_{d} = K_{p}\,T_{d} = 20 \times 0.75 = 15\ \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the discrete PID positional equation with $T_{s} = 1$ s.**

$$u[k] = 20\,e[k] + 6.667\,(1)\sum_{j=0}^{k}e[j] + \frac{15}{1}\!\left(e[k]-e[k-1]\right)$$

Simplifying:

$$u[k] = 20\,e[k] + 6.667\sum_{j=0}^{k}e[j] + 15\!\left(e[k]-e[k-1]\right)$$

**Step 6 — Engineering comment.**

This controller will give a *quarter-amplitude decay* response for setpoint changes. To avoid integral windup, the production PLC program must include a *clamp-and-anti-windup* subroutine that freezes the summation when $u[k]$ saturates at the actuator limits (0–10 V or 4–20 mA).

---

#### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]*

> **(a)** Draw the **internal architecture of a modular PLC** and explain the function of *each module*. Why is the *scan cycle* deterministic in a PLC but not in a general-purpose PC? **[7 marks]**
>
> **(b)** A bottling-line Special Purpose Machine uses a PLC to control three conveyors, a filling station, and a capping station. Design a **Ladder Logic program** (with at least two rungs) and an **SFC (Grafcet)** sequence for the following cycle: (i) conveyor-1 starts when start push-button is pressed and proximity sensor detects a bottle, (ii) bottle is held for 2 s at the filling station by a solenoid, (iii) capping station activates for 1 s, (iv) conveyor-3 ejects the bottle; emergency stop must halt everything instantly. **[7 marks]**

---

**Model Solution to B(a):**

**Step 1 — Block diagram (refer to SECTION 4, Figure 4.2).**

The modular PLC has the following functional blocks:
- **Power Supply (PSU)** — converts 230 V AC to 24 V DC for internal electronics; often supplies sensor power too.
- **CPU module** — houses the microprocessor, real-time OS, and the scan engine.
- **Memory** — non-volatile flash stores the user program; RAM holds runtime data, I/O image table, and retentive flags.
- **Digital Input (DI) module** — interfaces to limit switches, push-buttons, proximity sensors; provides optical isolation.
- **Digital Output (DO) module** — drives relays, contactors, solenoid valves, indicator lamps.
- **Analog Input (AI) module** — reads 4–20 mA or 0–10 V signals from temperature, pressure, flow transmitters.
- **Analog Output (AO) module** — sends 0–10 V or 4–20 mA to VFDs, proportional valves, and thyristor power controllers.
- **Communication module(s)** — Ethernet/PROFINET/EtherCAT for SCADA, peer-PLC, and remote I/O.

**Step 2 — Determinism explanation.**

A PLC executes a strict, **cyclic scan**:

1. *Read all inputs* into the process image (PII).
2. *Execute the user program* rung-by-rung, top to bottom.
3. *Write all outputs* from the process image (PIO).
4. *Perform housekeeping* (communication, diagnostics, self-test).

The **worst-case scan time** is *bounded* by the program length, so for any given program the maximum latency between an input change and the corresponding output change is **known and guaranteed** (typically 5–20 ms). This property is called *hard real-time determinism*.

A general-purpose PC, in contrast, runs a multi-tasking OS (Windows, Linux). The OS may preempt the control task for a virus scan, a USB enumeration, or a user opening a browser. The latency is *statistical* and can spike to hundreds of milliseconds or more — unacceptable for an SPM where a 50 ms delay can crash a hydraulic slide. Hence, safety-critical SPMs (presses, CNC, packaging) must use a PLC or a certified PAC, not a PC.

---

**Model Solution to B(b):**

**Step 1 — I/O assignment table.**

| Symbol | Address | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| Start_PB | I0.0 | NO push-button (green) |
| E_Stop | I0.1 | NC push-button (red mushroom) |
| Prox_Bottle | I0.2 | Inductive proximity sensor |
| Bott_Present | I0.3 | Bottle-at-fill sensor |
| Capping_Done | I0.4 | Capping torque sensor |
| Conv1_M | Q0.0 | Conveyor-1 motor contactor |
| Fill_Sol | Q0.1 | Filling-station solenoid |
| Cap_Sol | Q0.2 | Capping-station solenoid |
| Conv3_M | Q0.3 | Conveyor-3 motor contactor |
| Run_Flag | M0.0 | Internal memory (seal-in) |

**Step 2 — Ladder rungs (textual representation).**

Rung 1 — Master seal-in / E-Stop permissive:

| E_Stop (NC) | Start_PB (NO) | Prox_Bottle | Run_Flag | → | Run_Flag |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| /I0.1 | I0.0 | I0.2 | M0.0 |   | (M0.0) |

Logic: Run_Flag is latched (seal-in) only when E-Stop is *not* pressed, Start is pressed, and a bottle is sensed. Pressing E-Stop drops the seal-in instantly.

Rung 2 — Filling station:

| Run_Flag | Bott_Present | Timer_T1.DN | → | Fill_Sol |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| M0.0 | I0.3 | T1 |   | Q0.1 |

with a parallel branch: `I0.3 → TON T1, Preset = 2 s`. When Bott_Present goes TRUE, the 2 s timer starts, and the fill solenoid is energized. After 2 s, T1.DN falls and the solenoid de-energizes.

Rung 3 — Capping station:

| Fill_Done (T1.DN) | Capping_Timer.DN | → | Cap_Sol |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | T2 |   | Q0.2 |

with `Capping_Timer = TON T2, Preset = 1 s`. Capping is enabled only after filling is complete; it runs for 1 s, then releases.

Rung 4 — Conveyor-1 and Conveyor-3:

| Run_Flag | → | Conv1_M |
|---|---|---|
| M0.0 |   | Q0.0 |

| Cap_Done (T2.DN) | → | Conv3_M (with 0.5 s ON-delay) |
|---|---|---|
| T2 |   | Q0.3 via TON T3 = 0.5 s |

**Step 3 — SFC / Grafcet equivalent.**

| Step | Action | Transition Condition |
|------|--------|-----------------------|
| S0 | Idle (all off) | Start_PB AND Prox_Bottle |
| S1 | Conveyor-1 ON | Bott_Present |
| S2 | Filling solenoid ON, 2 s timer | T1.DN |
| S3 | Capping solenoid ON, 1 s timer | T2.DN |
| S4 | Conveyor-3 ON, 0.5 s | T3.DN |
| → back to S1 for next bottle; S0 if E_Stop |

**Step 4 — E-Stop behaviour (forced transitions).**

The E-Stop push-button (NC) is wired *both* in software (as the first contact on Rung 1) and in hardware (a hardware safety relay in series with all contactor coils). On E-Stop, the hardware relay drops the motor contactors in ≤ 25 ms (Category 1 stop per ISO 13850) — the PLC's software response (~10 ms) is parallel and redundant, achieving **Category 3 / PL d** safety integrity.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing "PID" with "P, I, D in series" vs "in parallel"** — the *ideal parallel* form is the one in the syllabus; the *ideal series* form has $K_{p}(1 + 1/T_{i}s)(1 + T_{d}s)$ and gives different results. Marks are deducted for silent mixing.
> 2. **Forgetting units in tuning calculations** — $K_{p}$ is dimensionless only if input and output share the same unit; otherwise it has V/°C or V/mm. State it explicitly.
> 3. **Ignoring the sampling theorem** — if $T_{s}$ is too large, the discrete PID is *unstable* even if the continuous one is stable. Quote Equation 4 from the Formula Sheet.
> 4. **Treating PLC scan as instantaneous** — for a 14-mark question on Ladder logic, you *must* show the seal-in (latching) contact; examiners look for it explicitly.
> 5. **Omitting anti-windup** — any PID answer without a clamp/anti-windup statement is graded down by at least 1 mark in 14-mark questions.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Control algorithm** = mathematical recipe executed by a PLC/PAC to drive an SPM to a desired state.
- ✅ **PLC** = IEC 61131-1 definition; **five languages** under IEC 61131-3: **LD, FBD, SFC, ST, IL**.
- ✅ **Closed-loop** requires SP → Σ → Controller → Actuator → Plant → Sensor → back to Σ.
- ✅ **Ideal parallel PID** (must memorize): $u(t) = K_{p}e + K_{i}\!\int e\,dt + K_{d}\dot{e}$.
- ✅ **Discrete PID** (PLC-executable): substitute Euler integral + backward derivative.
- ✅ **Velocity-form PID** eliminates integral windup — preferred in production.
- ✅ **Ziegler-Nichols** ultimate cycle: $K_{p}=0.6K_{u}$, $T_{i}=0.5T_{u}$, $T_{d}=0.125T_{u}$.
- ✅ **Sampling rule**: $T_{s} \le \tau_{dom}/10$; Nyquist: $f_{s} \ge 2f_{max}$.
- ✅ **PLC scan cycle** = Input read → Program execute → Output write → Housekeeping. Determinism is the killer advantage over a PC.
- ✅ **Ladder logic essentials**: XIC (NO), XIO (NC), OTE (output), OTL/OTU (latch/unlatch), TON/TOF/RTO (timers), CTU/CTD (counters).
- ✅ **SFC / Grafcet** = Steps + Transitions + Actions — the *only* standard language explicitly designed for sequential control of SPMs.
- ✅ **Anti-windup, bumpless transfer, and rate limiting** = the three engineering refinements expected in any 14-mark answer.
- ✅ **Safety standards** to mention: ISO 13849 (PL a–e), ISO 13850 (E-Stop), ISO 13851 (two-hand control).
- ✅ **Fieldbus speed ranking (fastest to slowest)**: EtherCAT (≈100 µs) → PROFINET IRT (≈1 ms) → Modbus TCP (≈10 ms) → PROFIBUS-DP (≈10 ms).
- ✅ A **well-tuned PID** typically yields $\zeta \approx 0.7$ and $\%OS \approx 5\%$ — quote these in numerical questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety and Standards - Safety Considerations and Industry Standards for Automation Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT601 - SPECIAL PURPOSE MACHINES FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Safety and Standards - Safety Considerations and Industry Standards for Automation Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Functional Safety** is the part of the overall safety of a machine or automation system that depends on the correct functioning of the Safety-related Electrical Control Systems (SRECS), Programmable Electronic Systems (PES), and other safety-critical subsystems in response to hazardous input conditions, without giving rise to new hazards. As defined in the seminal benchmark standard **IEC 61508**, functional safety ensures that risk is reduced to a tolerable level by ensuring that any failure leading to a hazardous situation is detected and the system is brought to, or maintained in, a safe state.

In the context of KTU's PCCPT601 syllabus for Special Purpose Machines, safety considerations encompass:

- **Risk Assessment** — the systematic evaluation of hazards, severity, and probability of occurrence.
- **Safety Integrity** — quantified by **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL)** in process automation (IEC 61508/61511) and **Performance Levels (PL)** in machinery safety (ISO 13849-1).
- **Safeguarding** — engineered physical or logical measures (guards, interlocks, light curtains, two-hand controls) that protect personnel from machine hazards.
- **Compliance with Harmonized Standards** — adherence to standards such as **ISO 12100**, **ISO 13849-1**, **IEC 62061**, **ISO 10218-1/-2**, and **ISO/TS 15066**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For automation and robotics coursework, examiners frequently frame questions around the **hierarchy of risk reduction** (inherently safe design → safeguarding → complementary protective measures → information for use). Always cite this hierarchy explicitly in design-based questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** *Safety* ≠ *Security*. **Safety** protects humans from physical harm caused by the machine; **Security** protects data and intellectual property from unauthorized access. KTU questions often test this distinction.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine an **automatic garage door**. The door has a powerful motor capable of crushing a person or pet. Now, the door has:

- An **infrared photocell** at the floor that reverses the door if something breaks the beam (a *protective device*).
- A **mechanical edge sensor** on the door's leading edge that reverses on contact.
- A **logic controller** (often a Safety PLC) that processes these signals and decides motor action.

The door's design follows the **"three-step safety philosophy"**:
1. **Inherent design**: Use a soft rubber bottom edge (passively safer).
2. **Safeguarding**: Install the photocell and edge sensor.
3. **Information for use**: Affix a warning sticker.

This is exactly the philosophy applied in industrial automation: **a robot cell is engineered so that even if a single component fails, no human is harmed.** Standards like ISO 12100 and ISO 13849 codify this philosophy in measurable engineering terms.

## 1.3 Standardized Risk Metrics (in Bold)

The most commonly invoked numerical metrics in KTU questions are:

- **SIL (Safety Integrity Level):** Discrete levels **1, 2, 3, 4** per IEC 61508.
- **PL (Performance Level):** Discrete levels **a, b, c, d, e** per ISO 13849-1.
- **PFHd (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour):** Target values from **≥ 10⁻⁵ to < 10⁻⁷** for PL d/e.
- **MTTFd (Mean Time To dangerous Failure):** Categories: **Low (< 3 years), Medium (3–10 years), High (> 10 years)**.
- **DC (Diagnostic Coverage):** Categories: **None, Low (≥ 60 %), Medium (≥ 90 %), High (≥ 99 %)**.
- **Category (B, 1, 2, 3, 4):** Architectural requirements for safety-related parts of control systems (SRP/CS).
- **Tolerable Risk:** The risk that society or stakeholders are prepared to accept in a given context.

> [!NOTE]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **SIL** (probability of failure on demand) with **PL** (probability of dangerous failure per hour). They are *related but not identical*. SIL is the process-control paradigm; PL is the machinery paradigm. KTU accepts both, but the question framing determines which is correct.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Risk Reduction Triad

Safety engineering in automation is fundamentally about **risk reduction**. The mathematical model is:

$$ R = f(S, F)$$

Where **R** is *Risk*, **S** is *Severity of harm*, and **F** is *Frequency (and possibility) of occurrence of that harm*. More elaborately, ISO 12100 decomposes frequency into:

$$ F = f(O, P)$$

Where **O** is *Exposure to the hazard* and **P** is the *Possibility of avoiding harm or limiting harm*.

The goal of safety engineering is to drive the residual risk below a tolerable threshold through a structured **3-step methodology**:

1. **Inherently Safe Design Measures** — eliminate the hazard or reduce risk by changing the machine itself (e.g., reducing motor torque, using softer materials, reducing operating speeds, ergonomic redesign).
2. **Safeguarding and Complementary Protective Measures** — add guards, interlocks, light curtains, two-hand controls, or hold-to-run controls.
3. **Information for Use** — provide warnings, training, and procedural instructions (this is the *weakest* layer, often the only acceptable one for low-severity risks).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 examiners reward answers that explicitly follow the **"hierarchy of risk reduction"** above. Always list the steps in this order; never invert them.

## 2.2 Determining Required Performance Level (PLr) — The Risk Graph Method

The **Risk Graph** (per **ISO 13849-1:2015**, Annex A) is the standardized KTU-relevant method for determining the required Performance Level (PLr) for a Safety Function. The graph uses four risk parameters:

- **S**: Severity of injury
  - **S1** = slight (normally reversible injury)
  - **S2** = serious (normally irreversible injury or death)
- **F**: Frequency and/or exposure to hazard
  - **F1** = seldom-to-less-often
  - **F2** = frequent-to-continuous
- **P**: Possibility of avoidance
  - **P1** = possible under specific conditions
  - **P2** = scarcely possible
- **W**: Probability of occurrence of a **W**ell-known **W**ell-experienced hazard pattern (optional, used for fine-tuning)
  - **W1** = low-to-medium
  - **W2** = high (rarely used; pushes to higher PL)

Starting from a base PL (typically **PL a**), each successive parameter that "skews toward danger" increments the required PL.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Quantity | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Typical Range / Unit | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Risk | $R$ | $R = f(S, F, P)$ | Qualitative / numeric | ISO 12100 |
| Frequency of Occurrence | $F$ | $F = f(O, P)$ | Qualitative | ISO 12100 |
| Probability of Dangerous Failure per Hour | $PFH_d$ | $PFH_d = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \lambda_{Di}$ | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-8}$ per hour | ISO 13849-1 |
| MTTFd (per channel) | $MTTF_{d,\text{chan}}$ | $MTTF_{d,\text{chan}} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_d}$ | Low / Medium / High | ISO 13849-1 |
| Diagnostic Coverage | $DC$ | $DC = \dfrac{\lambda_{DD}}{\lambda_{D,\text{total}}}$ | 0 to 1 (or %) | ISO 13849-1 |
| Average Diagnostic Coverage | $DC_{avg}$ | $DC_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum DC_i / MTTF_{d,i}}{\sum 1 / MTTF_{d,i}}$ | 60 % / 90 % / 99 % | ISO 13849-1 |
| Common Cause Failure (CCF) Score | $CCF$ | Sum of point values from Annex F | 0 to 100 (≥ 65 required) | ISO 13849-1 |
| Safety Integrity Level (SIL) Target | $PFD_{avg}$ | $PFD_{avg} = \dfrac{\lambda_{D} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ for a 1oo1 architecture | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$ | IEC 61508 |
| Availability (channel) | $A$ | $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$ | 0 to 1 | General |
| Safe Failure Fraction | $SFF$ | $SFF = 1 - \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{\lambda_{total}}$ | 60 % to 99.9 % | IEC 61508 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Table Construction Note:** Per the engine's strict formatting rules, the vertical bar `$\vert$` notation has been used inside the table cells to prevent the markdown pipe delimiter from breaking the table structure.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

Every automated production line in an automotive plant, every collaborative robot (cobot) on a shop floor, and every SCADA-controlled chemical process must demonstrate — via a **Safety Integrity Assessment** — that the residual risk lies below tolerable limits. The metrics above are not academic; they are legal requirements under the **EU Machinery Directive (2006/42/EC)** and analogous Indian regulations such as the **Factories Act, 1948** and the **Indian Boiler Regulations**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: MTTFd for a Single-Channel Subsystem

The **Mean Time To dangerous Failure** is derived from the *failure rate* $\lambda_d$ of a component. For a non-repaired system with constant failure rate (exponential distribution):

**Step 1 — Start with the reliability function.**

$$ R(t) = e^{-\lambda_d t} $$

This gives the probability that the system has *not* failed dangerously at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Express the probability density of failure.**

The probability density function (PDF) of failure time is the derivative of the unreliability $Q(t) = 1 - R(t)$:

$$ f(t) = \dfrac{dQ(t)}{dt} = \lambda_d e^{-\lambda_d t} $$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean of this distribution.**

The expected (mean) failure time is:

$$ MTTF_d = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \lambda_d e^{-\lambda_d t} \, dt $$

**Step 4 — Evaluate using integration by parts.**

Let $u = t$ and $dv = \lambda_d e^{-\lambda_d t} dt$. Then $du = dt$ and $v = -e^{-\lambda_d t}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
MTTF_d &= \Big[-t \, e^{-\lambda_d t}\Big]_{0}^{\infty} + \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda_d t} \, dt \\
&= 0 + \Big[-\dfrac{e^{-\lambda_d t}}{\lambda_d}\Big]_{0}^{\infty} \\
&= 0 - \left( -\dfrac{1}{\lambda_d} \right) = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_d}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Final compact form.**

$$ \boxed{MTTF_d = \dfrac{1}{\lambda_d}} $$

**Engineering interpretation:** If a safety relay has $\lambda_d = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}$ failures per hour, then:

$$ MTTF_d = \dfrac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 6.67 \times 10^{5} \text{ hours} \approx 76 \text{ years} $$

This is well into the **"High"** category (> 30 years per ISO 13849-1:2015 Table 4). High MTTFd is necessary — but not sufficient — to achieve PL d/e.

## 3.2 Derivation: PFHd for a 1oo2 (One-out-of-Two) Redundant Architecture

In a **1oo2 architecture**, two independent channels must both fail dangerously for a system failure. Each channel has a dangerous failure rate $\lambda_d$.

**Step 1 — Define the channel-level PFHd.**

For a single non-redundant channel operating continuously, the probability of dangerous failure per hour is:

$$ PFH_{d,1oo1} = \lambda_d $$

**Step 2 — Model the 1oo2 failure path.**

For a 1oo2 system, both channels must fail in the *same hour* (approximation, valid for $\lambda_d \cdot T \ll 1$):

$$ PFH_{d,1oo2} = (\lambda_d)^2 \cdot T_{cycle} $$

**Step 3 — Standard approximation per IEC 61508.**

For a 1oo2 system with diagnostic testing interval $T_{proof}$ (assumed small relative to component life):
