$$ \boxed{PFH_{d,1oo2} = \dfrac{\lambda_{d1} \cdot \lambda_{d2} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}} $$

If $\lambda_{d1} = \lambda_{d2} = \lambda_d$:

$$ PFH_{d,1oo2} = \dfrac{\lambda_d^2 \cdot T_{proof}}{2} $$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**

Let $\lambda_d = 10^{-6}$ /hr per channel, $T_{proof} = 8$ hours (single shift).

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFH_{d,1oo2} &= \dfrac{(10^{-6})^2 \times 8}{2} \\
&= \dfrac{8 \times 10^{-12}}{2} \\
&= 4 \times 10^{-12} \text{ /hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **six orders of magnitude safer** than the 1oo1 architecture, and would qualify for **PL e** (since $PFH_d < 10^{-7}$).

## 3.3 Derivation: Average Diagnostic Coverage (DCavg) for a Multi-Component Subsystem

When a safety function involves multiple components, the diagnostic coverage must be weighted by each component's contribution to the failure rate.

**Step 1 — Per-component failure-rate weight.**

$$ w_i = \dfrac{1}{MTTF_{d,i}} = \lambda_{d,i} $$

**Step 2 — Normalized weights.**

$$ W_i = \dfrac{w_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} w_j} = \dfrac{\lambda_{d,i}}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} \lambda_{d,j}} $$

**Step 3 — Compute DCavg.**

$$ \boxed{DC_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} W_i \cdot DC_i} $$

**Step 4 — Worked example.**

A safety function has two components:
- Component A: $MTTF_{d,A} = 50$ years, $DC_A = 99\,\%$
- Component B: $MTTF_{d,B} = 100$ years, $DC_B = 60\,\%$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{d,A} &= \dfrac{1}{50} = 0.020 \text{ /year} \\
\lambda_{d,B} &= \dfrac{1}{100} = 0.010 \text{ /year} \\
W_A &= \dfrac{0.020}{0.030} = 0.667 \\
W_B &= \dfrac{0.010}{0.030} = 0.333 \\
DC_{avg} &= 0.667 \times 0.99 + 0.333 \times 0.60 \\
&= 0.660 + 0.200 = 0.860 \;(\text{or } 86\,\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $DC_{avg} \geq 90\,\%$ is required for **PL d** with Category 3, this $DC_{avg} = 86\,\%$ would only qualify for **Medium DC** — meaning the system can only achieve **PL c** at best, regardless of MTTFd.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: PL Determination (ISO 13849-1 Simplified Bar Chart)

| Category | MTTFd (per channel) | DCavg | CCF | Resulting PL (max) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| B | Low | None | n/a | **a** |
| B | Medium | None | n/a | **b** |
| 1 | Medium | None | n/a | **c** |
| 1 | High | None | n/a | **c** |
| 2 | Low | Low | ≥ 65 | **a** |
| 2 | Medium | Low | ≥ 65 | **b** |
| 2 | High | Low | ≥ 65 | **c** |
| 2 | High | Medium | ≥ 65 | **d** |
| 3 | Low | Low | ≥ 65 | **b** |
| 3 | Medium | Low | ≥ 65 | **c** |
| 3 | High | Low | ≥ 65 | **d** |
| 3 | High | Medium | ≥ 65 | **d** |
| 4 | High | High | ≥ 65 | **e** |

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is the **single most important analytical tool** for KTU machine-safety problems. Memorize the qualitative relationships: **higher category + higher MTTFd + higher DC + good CCF → higher PL**.

## 3.5 Determining Required PL (PLr) Using the Risk Graph — Worked Example

**Scenario:** A CNC milling machine has a spindle that can amputate a hand. Estimate the required PLr.

- **Severity** = S2 (irreversible injury, possible death).
- **Frequency of exposure** = F2 (operator interacts 8 hours/day).
- **Possibility of avoidance** = P2 (sliding/clamping motion; cannot withdraw in time).

**Step 1 — Start at the base: PL a**.

**Step 2 — Apply S2** → upgrade to **PL c** (serious harm requires at least PL c as a baseline).

**Step 3 — Apply F2** → upgrade to **PL d** (frequent exposure).

**Step 4 — Apply P2** → upgrade to **PL e** (cannot avoid).

**Final answer: PLr = e.**

This is the highest performance level — meaning the design needs Category 4 architecture, High MTTFd, High DC, and excellent CCF.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Safety Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Operator Presence] --> C1[Safety Logic Controller]
    A2[Light Curtain Beam] --> C1
    A3[E-Stop Button] --> C1
    A4[Guard Interlock Switch] --> C1
    C1 --> O1[Contactor 1 Coil]
    C1 --> O2[Contactor 2 Coil]
    O1 --> M1[Three Phase Motor Drive]
    O2 --> M1
    M1 --> S1[Machine Output Action]
    C1 -. Diagnostic Feedback .-> D1[Diagnostic Display]
    O1 -. Aux Contact .-> C1
    O2 -. Aux Contact .-> C1
    style C1 fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M1 fill:#99ccff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading guide:**
- The four **sensor inputs** (left) feed a **Safety Logic Controller** (orange).
- The controller drives **two redundant contactors** that, in turn, energize a **3-phase motor**.
- The dotted lines show **diagnostic feedback** (used to calculate DC).
- Any single failure in a contactor is detected by the auxiliary contact loop, and the redundant contactor takes over.

## 4.2 Risk Reduction Hierarchy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identified Hazard] --> B{Step 1: Inherently Safe Design}
    B -- Residual Risk Still High --> C{Step 2: Safeguarding}
    C -- Residual Risk Still High --> D{Step 3: Information for Use}
    D --> E[Tolerable Risk Achieved]
    B -- Risk Reduced --> E
    C -- Risk Reduced --> E
    style A fill:#ff6666,stroke:#333,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333
    style E fill:#93c47d,stroke:#333,color:#fff
```

**Reading guide:** Starting from a **hazard** (red), the engineer applies the **3-step methodology** in order. Each step either reduces risk to tolerable levels (green terminal node) or passes residual risk to the next step.

## 4.3 Standard Mapping for Automation Systems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Industry Sector] --> S1[IEC 61508 / IEC 61511]
    S1 --> L1[SIL 1 to 4]
    M[Machinery Sector] --> S2[ISO 13849-1]
    S2 --> L2[PL a to e]
    M --> S3[IEC 62061]
    S3 --> L2
    R[Industrial Robots] --> S4[ISO 10218-1 and 10218-2]
    S4 --> L2
    C[Collaborative Robots] --> S5[ISO TS 15066]
    S5 --> L2
    G[General Machinery] --> S6[ISO 12100]
    S6 --> L0[Risk Assessment Foundation]
    S1 -. Foundation .-> S6
    S2 -. Foundation .-> S6
    style L0 fill:#ffe599,stroke:#333
    style L1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333
    style L2 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333
```

**Reading guide:** All machinery standards trace back to **ISO 12100** (yellow). The process industry uses **SIL**, while machinery uses **PL** — both are derived from the same risk-reduction philosophy.

## 4.4 Emergency Stop Circuit (Category 1)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PB1[Normally Closed E-Stop PB1] --> R1[Safety Relay K1]
    PB2[Normally Closed E-Stop PB2] --> R1
    LC[Light Curtain Output] --> R1
    R1 --> K1C[Contactor K1 Coil]
    R1 --> K2C[Contactor K2 Coil]
    K1C --> K1A[K1 Aux NC Contact]
    K2C --> K2A[K2 Aux NC Contact]
    K1A --> R1
    K2A --> R1
    K1C --> MOT[3-Phase Motor]
    K2C --> MOT
    style R1 fill:#f6b26b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style MOT fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#333
```

**Reading guide:** This is a **Category 3 / PL d** architecture: two E-stop pushbuttons, two contactors in series, each contactor's NC auxiliary contact feeds back to the safety relay. Loss of *any one* contactor is detected and the motor is stopped.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define *Functional Safety*. State **two** key differences between Functional Safety and conventional safety measures in an automated plant.

**Model Answer:**

*Functional Safety* is the part of overall safety that depends on the correct operation of safety-related control systems (electrical, electronic, programmable electronic) in response to hazardous inputs, ensuring the system transitions to — or remains in — a safe state. It is defined and quantified by standards such as **IEC 61508** (process) and **ISO 13849-1** (machinery).

**Two key differences:**

| Aspect | Functional Safety | Conventional Safety |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Active — relies on a control system detecting a fault and triggering a safe state | Passive — relies on physical separation, mechanical guards, or fixed barriers |
| Failure mode | Must detect *systematic* and *random* failures (diagnosed by DC) | Generally designed to be inherently safe; failure modes are predominantly physical |
| Quantification | Measured in SIL or PL with numerical targets ($PFH_d$) | Typically qualitative; specified as physical strength, gap, reach distance |

*(Full 3-mark answer: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for differences with examples.)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Expected Pattern]**

List the **three steps** of risk reduction as per **ISO 12100** in their correct order of preference. Give a one-line example of each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Inherently safe design measures** — modify the machine itself to eliminate or reduce the hazard. *Example:* Reduce the maximum spindle speed of a CNC machine to below a level capable of causing bone fracture.
2. **Safeguarding and complementary protective measures** — add engineered protective devices between the hazard and the person. *Example:* Install a light curtain at the operator station that halts the robot when breached.
3. **Information for use** — provide warnings, signs, training, and procedures. *Example:* Affix pictogram-based warning signs and provide a written operating manual.

*(Full 3-mark answer: 1 mark each for the three steps with valid examples.)*

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Performance Level (PL)** as defined in **ISO 13849-1:2015**. Describe the five PL grades (a, b, c, d, e) and the corresponding $PFH_d$ ranges. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A safety-related stop function on a packaging machine has the following parameters:

- **Required PLr = d** (from risk graph analysis)
- Two channels, each with $MTTF_d = 40$ years
- Diagnostic coverage $DC = 95\,\%$
- Common Cause Failure score $CCF = 70$

Determine the **Category** of the safety-related parts of the control system, the **PL achieved**, and whether the design meets PLr. Justify with a calculation of $PFH_d$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Performance Level — 7 marks**

Performance Level (PL) is a discrete level used to specify the ability of safety-related parts of a control system to perform a safety function under foreseeable conditions. The five levels correspond to a range of probability of dangerous failure per hour ($PFH_d$):

| Performance Level | $PFH_d$ (per hour) | Qualitative |
|---|---|---|
| **a** | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ | Low |
| **b** | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ | Low to Medium |
| **c** | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ | Medium |
| **d** | $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ | High |
| **e** | $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ | Very High |

PL is a function of three parameters: **Category** (architectural), **MTTFd** (component reliability), **DCavg** (diagnostic capability), and **CCF** (common cause failure avoidance). The PL is determined from the bar chart in **ISO 13849-1, Figure 5**.

*(4 marks for definition + 3 marks for the table with $PFH_d$ ranges.)*

**(b) Design Verification — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Classify MTTFd.**

$$ MTTF_{d,\text{chan}} = 40 \text{ years} $$

Since $MTTF_{d,\text{chan}} > 30$ years, the per-channel MTTFd is classified as **High**.

**Step 2 — Classify DC.**

$$ DC = 95\,\% $$

Since $90\,\% \le DC < 99\,\%$, the diagnostic coverage is classified as **Medium**.

**Step 3 — Convert MTTF to failure rate.**

Using the relationship $MTTF_d = 1 / \lambda_d$ and converting years to hours ($1$ year $\approx 8760$ hours):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_d &= \dfrac{1}{40 \times 8760} \\
&= \dfrac{1}{350{,}400} \\
&\approx 2.85 \times 10^{-6} \;/\text{hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the total $PFH_d$.**

For a redundant (Category 3) architecture, the dominant failure path is a single-channel dangerous failure *combined with* a failure of the diagnostic to detect it. Standard simplification (per ISO 13849-1):

$$ PFH_d = 2 \cdot \lambda_d \cdot (1 - DC) \cdot T_{cycle} + \lambda_d^2 \cdot T_{proof} $$

For typical $T_{cycle} = 24$ hours and $T_{proof} = 8760$ hours (one year):

$$
\begin{aligned}
PFH_{d,1} &= 2 \times 2.85 \times 10^{-6} \times (1 - 0.95) \times 24 \\
&= 2 \times 2.85 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.05 \times 24 \\
&= 6.84 \times 10^{-6} \;/\text{hour} \quad \text{(per channel)} \\
\\
PFH_{d,2} &= (2.85 \times 10^{-6})^2 \times 8760 \\
&= 8.12 \times 10^{-12} \times 8760 \\
&\approx 7.11 \times 10^{-8} \;/\text{hour} \quad \text{(negligible)} \\
\\
PFH_{d,\text{total}} &\approx 6.84 \times 10^{-6} \;/\text{hour}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Classify the achieved PL.**

Since $PFH_d \approx 6.84 \times 10^{-6}$ falls in the range $\geq 10^{-6}$ and $< 10^{-5}$, the achieved PL is **PL c**.

**Step 6 — Compare with PLr.**

- **PLr (required) = PL d**
- **PL (achieved) = PL c**

**Conclusion:** The design does **NOT meet** the required PL d. To upgrade, the engineer should either:
- Increase DC to High (≥ 99 %), OR
- Add a second diagnostic channel with cross-monitoring (Category 4), OR
- Replace components with higher MTTFd.

*(2 marks for Step 1, 2 marks for Steps 2–4, 2 marks for Steps 5–6, 1 mark for the upgrade recommendation.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – Expected Pattern, Module 3]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** approach of **IEC 61508** with the **Performance Level (PL)** approach of **ISO 13849-1**. Your answer must include at least **four** comparative points and an explicit mapping between the two systems. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A collaborative robot (cobot) cell performs a pick-and-place task near a human operator. Using the **risk graph** of **ISO 13849-1**, determine the required Performance Level (PLr) under the following assumptions:

- The cobot can apply contact forces of up to **150 N** at its end-effector.
- The operator works in the cell **continuously** during a 4-hour shift.
- The contact is a **quasi-static** clamping contact on the operator's hand.

Justify each parameter choice (S, F, P). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SIL vs. PL — 7 marks**

| Aspect | SIL (IEC 61508) | PL (ISO 13849-1) |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Process control / petrochem | Machinery / discrete manufacturing |
| Levels | 1, 2, 3, 4 | a, b, c, d, e |
| Probability basis | $PFD_{avg}$ (Probability of Failure on Demand) for low-demand; $PFH_d$ for high-demand | $PFH_d$ (Probability of dangerous Failure per Hour) |
| Risk model | Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) + risk graph | Risk graph (S, F, P) |
| Architectural basis | Routes 1H, 2H, 3H; 1oo1, 1oo2, 2oo2, etc. | Categories B, 1, 2, 3, 4 |
| Failure metric | Safe Failure Fraction (SFF) | Diagnostic Coverage (DC) |
| Proof test | Explicit ($T_{proof}$) | Implied via CCF and DC |

**Approximate PL ↔ SIL Mapping (per IEC 62061 / ISO 13849-1 Annex):**

| PL | SIL (high demand / continuous) | $PFH_d$ (per hour) |
|---|---|---|
| a | — | $\geq 10^{-5}$ to $< 10^{-4}$ |
| b | 1 | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ |
| c | 1 | $\geq 10^{-6}$ to $< 10^{-5}$ |
| d | 2 | $\geq 10^{-7}$ to $< 10^{-6}$ |
| e | 3 | $\geq 10^{-8}$ to $< 10^{-7}$ |

**Note:** SIL 4 has no PL equivalent and applies only to process-control / nuclear scenarios.

*(4 marks for comparison table, 2 marks for mapping table, 1 mark for concluding remark.)*

**(b) Risk Graph Application — 7 marks**

**Step 1 — Severity (S).**

Quasi-static clamping on a hand at 150 N exceeds the **150 N biomechanical limit** stipulated in **ISO/TS 15066** for collaborative operation. This is classified as **S2** (serious, irreversible injury, possible bone fracture).

**Step 2 — Frequency / Exposure (F).**

The operator works continuously for 4 hours per shift in the cobot workspace. This is **F2** (frequent-to-continuous exposure).

**Step 3 — Possibility of Avoidance (P).**

A quasi-static clamping contact means the operator's hand is *trapped* between the robot and a fixture — the operator cannot withdraw in time. This is **P2** (scarcely possible to avoid).

**Step 4 — Well-known hazard pattern (W).**

Not specifically required; default to **W1** (low-to-medium).

**Step 5 — Derive PLr by iterating through the risk graph.**

- Base: **PL a**
- Apply S2: → **PL c**
- Apply F2: → **PL d**
- Apply P2: → **PL e**

**Final Result: PLr = e**

This means the safety-related parts of the control system (force-sensing skin, speed-and-separation monitoring, contact-detection, etc.) must achieve Category 4 architecture with High MTTFd, High DC, and CCF ≥ 65.

*(2 marks for parameter classification with justification, 3 marks for the iterative PLr derivation, 2 marks for the final design implication.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong direction in the risk graph:** Many students start at PL e and walk *down*. The correct methodology is to **start at PL a and step up** based on worsening parameters.
> 2. **Confusing SIL and PL:** Examiners award zero credit if a student calculates $PFD_{avg}$ when the question asks for PL, or vice versa.
> 3. **Ignoring the hierarchy of risk reduction:** Any safety question that describes a "design" must first discuss *inherent* design changes *before* proposing safeguards. Inverted answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Skipping CCF:** In PL questions, students often forget the **CCF ≥ 65** requirement for Category 3 and above. This is a frequent deduction in KTU valuation keys.
> 5. **Mixing $MTTF_d$ (per channel) and $MTTF_{d,\text{chan}}$:** They are identical, but the bar chart of ISO 13849-1 uses *per-channel* MTTFd. State explicitly whether you are using the per-channel or combined value.
> 6. **Forgetting units:** Always state $PFH_d$ in **per-hour** units and convert years to hours using $8760$ hours/year.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Functional Safety** = ability of a control system to bring a process to a safe state upon detection of a hazardous condition. Defined by **IEC 61508**.
- **Three-step Risk Reduction Hierarchy** (ISO 12100): **(1) Inherently safe design → (2) Safeguarding → (3) Information for use.** Apply in this order — never invert.
- **SIL** (IEC 61508) — Levels **1, 2, 3, 4** — for **process automation** — uses $PFD_{avg}$ for low-demand and $PFH_d$ for high-demand.
- **PL** (ISO 13849-1) — Levels **a, b, c, d, e** — for **machinery automation** — uses $PFH_d$ exclusively.
- **Risk Graph** uses parameters **S (severity), F (frequency), P (avoidability)** to determine PLr; start at **PL a**, increment based on worsening parameters.
- **PL determination** depends on four factors: **Category** (B/1/2/3/4), **MTTFd per channel** (Low/Medium/High), **DCavg** (None/Low/Medium/High), **CCF** (≥ 65 points for PL c and above).
- **$MTTF_d = 1 / \lambda_d$** — convert years to hours using $1$ year $\approx 8760$ hours.
- **$DC_{avg} = \sum W_i \cdot DC_i$** with weights proportional to component failure rates.
- **For PL d/e**, the safety system needs **High MTTFd**, **Medium or High DC**, and **CCF ≥ 65**.
- **Key Standards** to remember for KTU PCCPT601:
  - **ISO 12100** — general risk assessment
  - **ISO 13849-1** — PL for machinery
  - **IEC 62061** — SIL for machinery sector
  - **IEC 61508** — functional safety (process sector)
  - **ISO 10218-1/-2** — industrial robots
  - **ISO/TS 15066** — collaborative robots (150 N force limit)
  - **EN 60204-1** — electrical equipment of machines
- **Emergency Stop Circuit (Cat 1)** — uses two contactors in series, each monitored by NC auxiliaries feeding back to a safety relay.
- **Light Curtains / Safety Mats / Two-Hand Controls** are all *complementary protective measures*, *not* inherently safe design.
- **Cobots** must satisfy **ISO/TS 15066** biomechanical limits — quasi-static contact < **150 N**, transient contact < **120 N**.
- **LOTO (Lockout/Tagout)** is a procedural safeguard, classified under "Information for Use".
- Always state **units** in PFHd calculations; KTU valuation keys penalize missing units.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Troubleshooting Techniques - Common Issues and Solutions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT601 - SPECIAL PURPOSE MACHINES FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Troubleshooting Techniques - Common Issues and Solutions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Troubleshooting** in the context of **Special Purpose Machines (SPMs)** is defined as the systematic, logical, and methodical process of identifying the root cause of a malfunction, fault, or performance degradation in a machine's mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, electrical, or electronic subsystems, followed by the implementation of corrective actions to restore the machine to its specified operational state.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** parlance, troubleshooting of SPMs is broadly classified under the umbrella of **Reliability Engineering and Maintenance Management**, where the prime directive is to minimize **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** while maximizing **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology (As per KTU 2024 Module 3 Syllabus):**
> - **SPM (Special Purpose Machine):** A custom-designed machine for a specific manufacturing operation, often fully or semi-automatic.
> - **Fault:** Any deviation of a machine parameter from its nominal/tolerated range.
> - **Failure:** The inability of a machine or subsystem to perform its required function.
> - **Root Cause Analysis (RCA):** The structured investigation to identify the fundamental reason for a fault.
> - **Downtime:** The period during which the SPM is unavailable for production.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor in an emergency room**. A patient walks in complaining of chest pain. You don't just randomly prescribe medicine. Instead, you:
1. **Ask questions** (Symptom identification)
2. **Measure vital signs** like BP, pulse, ECG (Diagnostics)
3. **Compare against normal ranges** (Specification check)
4. **Identify the actual disease** (Root Cause Analysis)
5. **Prescribe a treatment** (Corrective Action)
6. **Monitor recovery** (Verification & Validation)

Troubleshooting an **SPM** works **exactly the same way**! The machine is the "patient", the symptoms are alarms, abnormal noises, or poor product quality, and you, the engineer, are the doctor. You start with the obvious symptoms and systematically drill down to the root cause.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy for KTU Viva:** When asked "What is systematic troubleshooting?", simply state: *"It is the process of finding the 'why' behind the 'what' — i.e., finding the root cause of a fault rather than just treating the symptom."*

### 1.3 Why is Troubleshooting Critical in SPMs?

| Aspect | Impact on Industry |
|---|---|
| **Cost of Downtime** | In the automotive industry, **1 hour of SPM downtime** can result in losses of **₹50,000 to ₹2,00,000** (depending on production scale). |
| **Product Quality** | Unresolved faults lead to rejection rates of **2-5%** in precision SPMs. |
| **Safety** | Hydraulic or pneumatic faults can lead to **catastrophic accidents** if not diagnosed early. |
| **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)** | Goal in modern SPMs: **MTBF ≥ 2000 hours** for high-reliability systems. |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 7-Step Systematic Troubleshooting Framework (KTU 2024 Recommended Methodology)

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes a **structured, not ad-hoc**, approach. The recommended 7-step framework is:

1. **Symptom Recognition (Observe):** Identify the abnormal condition — unusual noise, vibration, heat, leakage, error code on HMI, or degraded output quality.
2. **Symptom Verification (Confirm):** Verify that the symptom is not a one-time anomaly. Reset the machine and re-test. Check the part produced in the last cycle.
3. **Fault Isolation (Localize):** Narrow down the fault to a specific subsystem: Mechanical, Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electrical, Electronic, or Software/PLC.
4. **Root Cause Analysis (Diagnose):** Apply diagnostic tools (multimeter, oscilloscope, pressure gauge, vibration analyzer) to pinpoint the failed component.
5. **Corrective Action (Fix):** Replace, repair, recalibrate, or re-program the faulty component.
6. **Verification (Test):** Run the machine through a full production cycle to ensure the fault is eliminated and no new faults are introduced.
7. **Documentation (Log):** Record the fault, root cause, corrective action, and time-to-resolve in a maintenance logbook for future reference.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** Out of these 7 steps, **Steps 3 and 4 (Fault Isolation and Root Cause Analysis)** carry the maximum weightage in both theory and practical examinations.

### 2.2 Common Subsystem-Wise Issues in SPMs (Module 3 Focus Area)

#### 2.2.1 Hydraulic System Issues
- **Oil leakage from seals and hoses:** Caused by worn O-rings, loose fittings, or degraded seals.
- **Pressure drop:** Due to pump wear, filter clogging, or internal leakage in valves.
- **Overheating of hydraulic oil:** Caused by cooler failure, low oil level, or excessive back-pressure.
- **Cylinder drift / slow movement:** Due to internal leakage in cylinder seals or worn directional control valves.
- **Cavitation in pump:** Caused by restricted suction line or low oil level.

#### 2.2.2 Pneumatic System Issues
- **Air leakage:** The most common fault; causes excessive compressor cycling and pressure drop.
- **Moisture in air lines:** Causes water hammer and corrosion in valves/cylinders.
- **FRL (Filter-Regulator-Lubricator) malfunction:** Blocked filter element or improper lubrication.
- **Solenoid valve coil failure:** Due to voltage fluctuation or coil burn-out.
- **Slow cylinder actuation:** Caused by leakage or undersized valves.

#### 2.2.3 Electrical & Electronic Issues
- **PLC I/O module failure:** Either input sensor failure or output relay contact welding.
- **Servo drive faults:** Overcurrent, encoder feedback errors, or position deviation errors.
- **Sensor malfunction:** Proximity sensor, photoelectric sensor, or limit switch failures.
- **Wiring issues:** Loose terminals, broken wires in flexible cable carriers (drag chains), or EMI-induced noise.
- **HMI/Display errors:** Touch screen non-responsiveness or communication protocol errors.

#### 2.2.4 Mechanical Issues
- **Bearing failure:** Due to inadequate lubrication, contamination, or overload.
- **Belt/chain slippage:** Due to wear, improper tensioning, or misalignment.
- **Spindle vibration:** Caused by tool unbalance, bearing wear, or tool holder runout.
- **Lead screw / ball screw wear:** Caused by inadequate lubrication or contamination.
- **Guide way scoring:** Caused by chip ingress or insufficient lubrication.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Reliability & Maintenance Metrics)

| Metric | Formula | Standard Notation | Unit | KTU 2024 Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** | $\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$ | $T_{up}$ / N | Hours | Measures reliability of SPM |
| **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)** | $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$ | $T_{down}$ / N | Hours | Measures maintainability |
| **Availability (A)** | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%$ | $A$ | Percentage | KTU target for SPMs: **A ≥ 95%** |
| **Failure Rate (λ)** | $\lambda = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | $\lambda$ | Failures/hour | Used in exponential reliability model |
| **Reliability Function R(t)** | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $R(t)$ | Dimensionless | Probability of survival till time t |
| **OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness)** | $\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}$ | $\text{OEE}$ | Percentage | World-class target: **OEE ≥ 85%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse **MTBF with MTTR**. Remember: **MTBF = Up Time metric**, **MTTR = Down Time metric**. In KTU numerical problems, the question will usually provide operating hours, number of failures, and downtime — read carefully!

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern **Industry 4.0-enabled SPMs**, troubleshooting is increasingly done using:
- **Predictive Maintenance (PdM):** Vibration analysis, thermography, oil analysis, and acoustic emission monitoring.
- **IIoT (Industrial IoT) Sensors:** Real-time cloud-based monitoring of machine health.
- **Digital Twins:** Virtual replicas of the SPM used to simulate faults before they occur.
- **AI/ML-based fault diagnosis:** Machine learning models trained on historical fault data to predict failures.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Fault-Tree Logic for Common SPM Failures

The **Analytical Matrix** (also known as the **Fault Tree** in reliability engineering) maps a top-level failure to its sub-causes. This is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU examinations.

#### Worked Example 1: Deriving Availability from MTBF and MTTR

**Problem Statement:** A CNC-based Special Purpose Machine operates for a total of **1800 hours** in a month. During this period, it experienced **3 failures**, with individual downtimes of **4 hours, 6 hours, and 2 hours** respectively. Calculate the **(a) MTBF, (b) MTTR, and (c) Availability** of the SPM.

**Given Data (extracted from problem):**
- Total Operating Time (T_up) = **1800 hours**
- Number of Failures (N) = **3**
- Individual Downtimes: t₁ = 4 h, t₂ = 6 h, t₃ = 2 h

**Step 1: Calculate Total Downtime**

$$
T_{down} = t_1 + t_2 + t_3
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
T_{down} = 4 + 6 + 2 = 12 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate MTBF**

By definition:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}} = \frac{T_{up}}{N}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{1800}{3} = 600 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate MTTR**

By definition:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}} = \frac{T_{down}}{N}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{12}{3} = 4 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Availability**

Using the standard availability formula:

$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100\%
$$

Substituting the computed values:

$$
A = \frac{600}{600 + 4} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A = \frac{600}{604} \times 100\%
$$

$$
A = 99.34\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation for KTU Viva:** An availability of **99.34%** is **excellent** for an SPM. It means the machine is available for production **99.34% of the time** and is under repair only **0.66% of the time**. World-class manufacturing systems target **A ≥ 99%**.

#### Worked Example 2: Reliability Function for an SPM Component

**Problem Statement:** A servo motor used in an SPM has a failure rate of **λ = 0.0005 failures/hour**. Calculate the probability that the servo motor will operate **without failure for 1000 hours**.

**Given Data:**
- Failure rate, λ = 0.0005 failures/hour
- Operating time, t = 1000 hours

**Step 1: State the Reliability Function**

For a constant failure rate (exponential distribution):

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Values**

$$
R(t) = e^{-(0.0005)(1000)}
$$

$$
R(t) = e^{-0.5}
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the Exponential**

Using the standard value:

$$
e^{-0.5} \approx 0.6065
$$

$$
R(t) \approx 60.65\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** This result means there is a **60.65% probability** that the servo motor will survive 1000 hours without failure. The probability of failure is **1 - 0.6065 = 0.3935** or **39.35%**. This is why **preventive replacement** of such critical components is recommended before they reach their expected life.

#### Worked Example 3: OEE Calculation for an SPM

**Problem Statement:** A flexible manufacturing SPM has the following data for one shift (8 hours):
- Planned Production Time: **480 minutes**
- Downtime (setup + breakdowns): **60 minutes**
- Ideal Cycle Time: **1.2 minutes/part**
- Total Parts Produced: **320 parts**
- Defective Parts: **8 parts**

Calculate the **OEE**.

**Step 1: Calculate Availability**

$$
\text{Availability} = \frac{\text{Operating Time}}{\text{Planned Production Time}} = \frac{480 - 60}{480}
$$

$$
\text{Availability} = \frac{420}{480} = 0.875 = 87.5\%
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Performance**

$$
\text{Performance} = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Parts Produced}}{\text{Operating Time}}
$$

$$
\text{Performance} = \frac{1.2 \times 320}{420} = \frac{384}{420} = 0.9143 = 91.43\%
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Quality**

$$
\text{Quality} = \frac{\text{Good Parts}}{\text{Total Parts Produced}} = \frac{320 - 8}{320}
$$

$$
\text{Quality} = \frac{312}{320} = 0.975 = 97.5\%
$$

**Step 4: Calculate OEE**

$$
\text{OEE} = \text{Availability} \times \text{Performance} \times \text{Quality}
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.875 \times 0.9143 \times 0.975
$$

$$
\text{OEE} = 0.780 = 78.0\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industry Benchmark:** An OEE of **78%** indicates the SPM has scope for improvement. World-class OEE is **≥ 85%**. The biggest loss here is the **Availability loss** (12.5%), indicating a need to **reduce breakdowns through better troubleshooting and preventive maintenance**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Systematic Troubleshooting Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete troubleshooting workflow as recommended by KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Fault Observed on SPM] --> stepB{Step 1: Symptom Recognition}
    stepB --> stepC{Step 2: Verify Symptom - Is it Persistent?}
    stepC -->|No| stepD[False Alarm - Reset and Monitor]
    stepC -->|Yes| stepE{Step 3: Identify Subsystem}
    stepE --> stepF[Mechanical Subsystem]
    stepE --> stepG[Hydraulic Subsystem]
    stepE --> stepH[Pneumatic Subsystem]
    stepE --> stepI[Electrical PLC Subsystem]
    stepE --> stepJ[Electronic Sensor Subsystem]
    stepF --> stepK{Step 4: Apply Diagnostic Tools}
    stepG --> stepK
    stepH --> stepK
    stepI --> stepK
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL{Step 5: Root Cause Identified?}
    stepL -->|No| stepM[Escalate to Senior Engineer]
    stepL -->|Yes| stepN[Step 6: Apply Corrective Action]
    stepN --> stepO[Step 7: Verify on Test Cycle]
    stepO --> stepP{Fault Eliminated?}
    stepP -->|No| stepK
    stepP -->|Yes| stepQ[Step 8: Document in Maintenance Log]
    stepQ --> endR[End: SPM Restored to Production]
    stepM --> endR
```

### 4.2 Fault-Tree Analysis Diagram for SPM Downtime

The following diagram shows a top-level fault (SPM Stops) and its possible root causes across different subsystems — a typical KTU Module 3 examination expectation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    topA[TOP EVENT: SPM Stops Unexpectedly] --> midB[Mechanical Fault]
    topA --> midC[Hydraulic Fault]
    topA --> midD[Pneumatic Fault]
    topA --> midE[Electrical PLC Fault]
    topA --> midF[Sensor Fault]
    midB --> leafG[Bearing Failure]
    midB --> leafH[Ball Screw Wear]
    midB --> leafI[Belt Chain Breakage]
    midC --> leafJ[Pump Failure]
    midC --> leafK[Valve Coil Burnt]
    midC --> leafL[Oil Leakage Seal]
    midD --> leafM[Compressor Failure]
    midD --> leafN[Air Leakage in Line]
    midD --> leafO[FRL Blocked]
    midE --> leafP[PLC I O Module Failure]
    midE --> leafQ[Servo Drive Overcurrent]
    midE --> leafR[Power Supply Failure]
    midF --> leafS[Proximity Sensor Dirty]
    midF --> leafT[Encoder Feedback Loss]
    midF --> leafU[Limit Switch Worn]
```

### 4.3 5-Why Analysis Example (Block Diagram)

A popular KTU Module 3 question asks students to perform a **5-Why Analysis** on an SPM fault. The following diagram shows a worked example.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    whyA[Why 1: Why did the hydraulic cylinder stop moving?]
    whyA --> whyB[Why 2: Why is there no pressure at the cylinder port?]
    whyB --> whyC[Why 3: Why is the directional control valve not shifting?]
    whyC --> whyD[Why 4: Why is the solenoid coil not energizing?]
    whyD --> whyE[Why 5: Why is the PLC output relay contact welded?]
    whyE --> rootCause[ROOT CAUSE: PLC output relay is at end of life due to frequent switching - REPLACE RELAY]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Diagram Tip:** In your answer sheet, always draw the **fault tree from top to bottom**, with the **top event as a rectangle** and the **basic events as circles**. Label every connecting line clearly. This is the ISQC standard for FTA (Fault Tree Analysis) as per IEC 61025.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define MTBF and MTTR. Why are these metrics important in SPM maintenance?

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** It is the average time elapsed between two consecutive failures of a repairable system. $\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$.
- **MTTR (Mean Time To Repair):** It is the average time taken to repair a failed system and bring it back to operational state. $\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime}}{\text{Number of Repairs}}$.
- **Importance:** These metrics help in **quantifying the reliability and maintainability** of an SPM. A high MTBF and low MTTR indicate a well-maintained and reliable machine, which directly translates to higher **availability** and **OEE** in production environments. **(1 mark for definitions, 2 marks for importance).**

#### Question 2: List any four common causes of hydraulic system failure in SPMs.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Four common causes of hydraulic system failure in SPMs are:
1. **Oil leakage** from worn seals, O-rings, and hose connections.
2. **Pump failure** due to cavitation, wear, or contamination of hydraulic oil.
3. **Valve malfunction** caused by solenoid coil burn-out or spool wear.
4. **Overheating of hydraulic oil** due to cooler failure, low oil level, or excessive back-pressure.

> *(Any four valid causes — 0.75 marks each)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the systematic 7-step methodology for troubleshooting Special Purpose Machines with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A transfer line SPM operates for **2400 hours** in 6 months. During this period, it experienced **4 failures** with total downtime of **20 hours**. Calculate the **MTBF, MTTR, and Availability**. If the management wants to improve availability to **99.5%**, what should be the target MTTR? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7-Step Troubleshooting Methodology (7 Marks):**

The systematic troubleshooting of an SPM follows a structured 7-step process:

1. **Symptom Recognition (1 mark):** The engineer observes an abnormal condition — e.g., the SPM's hydraulic clamping cylinder is not applying full clamping force, leading to part slippage during machining.

2. **Symptom Verification (1 mark):** The engineer confirms the issue by repeating the cycle. The fault is persistent — clamping force is consistently low.

3. **Fault Isolation (1 mark):** The engineer localizes the issue to the **hydraulic subsystem** (the mechanical and electrical systems are ruled out by checking other functions).

4. **Root Cause Diagnosis (1 mark):** Using a **pressure gauge** at the cylinder port, the engineer measures actual pressure. The pressure is only **40 bar** instead of the specified **80 bar**. A further check at the pump output shows the pump is delivering **80 bar**. This indicates the **pressure drop is between the pump and the cylinder**, pointing to either a **leaking directional control valve** or a **leaking cylinder seal**.

5. **Corrective Action (1 mark):** The directional control valve is inspected and found to have **internal leakage due to worn spool**. The valve is replaced and the system is re-pressurized.

6. **Verification (1 mark):** The SPM is run through **10 dry cycles** to verify clamping force. Pressure is now stable at **80 bar**, and no leakage is observed.

7. **Documentation (1 mark):** The entire fault, root cause, and corrective action is recorded in the **maintenance logbook** for future reference and trend analysis.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Total Operating Time (T_up) = 2400 hours
- Number of Failures (N) = 4
- Total Downtime (T_down) = 20 hours

**Step 1: Calculate MTBF (2 marks)**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{T_{up}}{N} = \frac{2400}{4} = 600 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate MTTR (2 marks)**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{T_{down}}{N} = \frac{20}{4} = 5 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Current Availability (1 mark)**

$$
A_{current} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{600}{600 + 5} = \frac{600}{605}
$$

$$
A_{current} = 0.9917 = 99.17\%
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Target MTTR for 99.5% Availability (2 marks)**

$$
A_{target} = 0.995 = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}_{new}}
$$

Substituting MTBF = 600:

$$
0.995 = \frac{600}{600 + \text{MTTR}_{new}}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
0.995 \times (600 + \text{MTTR}_{new}) = 600
$$

$$
597 + 0.995 \cdot \text{MTTR}_{new} = 600
$$

$$
0.995 \cdot \text{MTTR}_{new} = 3
$$

$$
\text{MTTR}_{new} = \frac{3}{0.995} = 3.015 \text{ hours} \approx 3.02 \text{ hours}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The current MTTR of **5 hours** must be reduced to approximately **3 hours** to achieve the target availability of **99.5%**. This can be achieved by **stocking critical spares, training maintenance staff, and using predictive maintenance techniques**.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** Describe any **four common issues** observed in **pneumatic systems** of SPMs and their **troubleshooting solutions**. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **5-Why Analysis** methodology for root cause identification. Apply it to a scenario where an **SPM's CNC spindle motor overheats frequently**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Four Pneumatic Issues and Solutions (7 Marks):**

| Sl. No. | Common Pneumatic Issue | Root Cause | Troubleshooting Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Excessive air leakage in pipelines** | Worn or damaged O-rings, loose fittings, cracked hoses. | Conduct a **soap-water test** on all joints. Tighten fittings. Replace damaged hoses and O-rings. Use **leak detection spray**. |
| **2** | **Slow cylinder actuation / insufficient force** | Pressure drop due to leakage or undersized valves, clogged silencers. | Measure pressure at the cylinder port. Clean or replace **FRL filter element**. Replace undersized valves with correctly rated ones. |
| **3** | **Moisture in air lines (water hammer)** | Inadequate **air dryer** capacity; drainage of receiver tank not done. | Install a **refrigerated air dryer** with adequate capacity. Drain receiver tank **daily**. Add **water traps** at low points. |
| **4** | **Solenoid valve coil burn-out** | Voltage fluctuation, coil overheat, water ingress. | Measure supply voltage with a **multimeter**. Install a **voltage stabilizer**. Replace the coil. Seal the valve enclosure to IP65 standard. |

*(1.5 marks per issue + 1 mark for the table presentation = 7 marks)*

---

**Part (b) — 5-Why Analysis for CNC Spindle Motor Overheating (7 Marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** The **5-Why Analysis** is a root cause analysis technique developed by **Sakichi Toyoda** of Toyota. It works on the principle that asking "Why" **five times** (or as many times as needed) progressively moves from the **symptom** to the **root cause**.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    why1[WHY 1: Why is the CNC spindle motor overheating?]
    why1 --> why2[WHY 2: Why is the motor drawing excessive current?]
    why2 --> why3[WHY 3: Why is the spindle bearing seizing?]
    why3 --> why4[WHY 4: Why is the bearing not receiving adequate lubrication?]
    why4 --> why5[WHY 5: Why is the auto-lubrication system not delivering grease?]
    why5 --> rcROOT[ROOT CAUSE: The lubrication pump's solenoid valve is stuck open, causing the grease reservoir to empty without delivering grease. REPLACE SOLENOID VALVE AND REFILL RESERVOIR.]
```

**Detailed Explanation (2 marks):**
- **Why 1:** The **temperature sensor** on the spindle housing shows a reading of **95°C** (normal is **≤ 70°C**).
- **Why 2:** The **servo drive** indicates an **overcurrent alarm** — current draw is **40A** instead of the normal **25A**.
- **Why 3:** A **manual rotation check** of the spindle reveals **stiffness and grinding noise**, confirming **bearing seizure** due to overheating.
- **Why 4:** The **bearings** show signs of **dry running and discoloration**, indicating the **auto-lubrication system has been failing silently**.
- **Why 5:** The **lubrication pump** is not delivering grease. Further inspection shows the **solenoid valve** controlling grease flow is **mechanically stuck**, causing the reservoir to empty without dispensing grease at the bearings.
- **Root Cause & Corrective Action:** The **solenoid valve** is replaced, the **grease reservoir is refilled**, the **bearings are replaced**, and the **lubrication system is scheduled for monthly preventive checks**. A **flow sensor** is added to the lubrication line for future early warning.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often **write only 2-3 "Whys"** and stop. The 5-Why method requires you to **persist until you find an actionable root cause**, not just a contributing factor.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In the **MTBF/MTTR numerical**, students forget to convert the **final availability into percentage** (multiply by 100). They also confuse **MTTR calculation denominator** (it is the number of *repairs/failures*, not the number of parts or operators).
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In the **7-step methodology**, students often **miss Step 7 (Documentation)** and lose 1 mark. Always include documentation as it is critical for **Kaizen and continuous improvement**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** For **OEE calculations**, students often forget the **ideal cycle time** concept and incorrectly use actual cycle time. **Ideal cycle time is the theoretical minimum** — never use measured cycle time for the performance calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- [x] **Troubleshooting** is a **systematic, logical, and methodical** process — never ad-hoc.
- [x] The **7-Step Methodology**: Observe → Verify → Isolate → Diagnose → Fix → Test → Document.
- [x] **MTBF = Operating Time / Number of Failures** (unit: hours). Higher is better.
- [x] **MTTR = Downtime / Number of Repairs** (unit: hours). Lower is better.
- [x] **Availability = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR) × 100%**. World-class target: **≥ 99%**.
- [x] **Failure Rate λ = 1 / MTBF**. **Reliability R(t) = e^(-λt)**.
- [x] **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality**. World-class target: **≥ 85%**.
- [x] The **5-Why Analysis** is a root cause tool — keep asking "why" until an actionable cause is found.
- [x] **Common Hydraulic Issues:** Oil leakage, pressure drop, overheating, cavitation, cylinder drift.
- [x] **Common Pneumatic Issues:** Air leakage, moisture, FRL block, coil burn-out, slow actuation.
- [x] **Common Electrical Issues:** PLC I/O failure, servo drive fault, sensor malfunction, wiring breakage.
- [x] **Common Mechanical Issues:** Bearing failure, belt slip, spindle vibration, ball screw wear.
- [x] **Always document** the fault, root cause, and corrective action in a **maintenance logbook**.
- [x] **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** uses vibration analysis, thermography, oil analysis — it is the modern best practice for SPM troubleshooting.
- [x] **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** is drawn top-down with rectangles for top events and circles for basic events.
- [x] For 14-mark numericals, **always show units** (hours, %) and **state the formula before substitution**.

---

> **End of Notes — Module 3: Troubleshooting Techniques — Common Issues and Solutions**
> **Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme | PCCPT601 — Special Purpose Machines for Automation**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theoretical Troubleshooting Techniques - Concepts and Strategies for Diagnosing and Resolving Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCPT601 - SPECIAL PURPOSE MACHINES FOR AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Theoretical Troubleshooting Techniques - Concepts and Strategies for Diagnosing and Resolving Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Troubleshooting** in the context of Special Purpose Machines (SPM) for automation is defined as a **systematic, logical, and analytical process of identifying, isolating, and rectifying faults** — electrical, mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, electronic, or software-based (PLC/HMI) — that cause deviation from the designed performance, repeatability, or safety specification of an automated manufacturing system.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus framework for PCCPT601, troubleshooting is treated as a **core engineering competency** that integrates knowledge of mechatronics, control engineering, sensor-actuator interfacing, and process diagnostics. The theoretical foundation rests on three pillars:

1. **Fault Recognition** — knowing what "normal" looks like.
2. **Fault Isolation** — localizing the defective component, subsystem, or signal path.
3. **Fault Rectification & Verification** — restoring the system and confirming the fix does not introduce secondary faults.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor-Detective" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine an automated CNC transfer line as a human body.** The PLC is the **brain**, hydraulic actuators are the **muscles**, sensors are the **senses (eyes/skin)**, and the power supply is the **heart**. When a machine "feels sick" (a fault occurs), a troubleshooter acts like a **doctor-detective**: first takes the patient's history (symptom log), checks vital signs (sensor readings), runs targeted tests (isolated actuation), prescribes treatment (replace/repair), and finally re-examines to confirm the cure worked. Skipping steps — like jumping straight to "replace the servo" without diagnosis — is like prescribing antibiotics without examining the patient. It may "work" sometimes, but it is unscientific, expensive, and unsustainable in industry.

## 1.3 Classification of Faults in SPMs

A foundational understanding requires classifying the *type* of fault being diagnosed. Each category demands a different troubleshooting strategy.

| Fault Category | Definition | Typical Symptom | Strategy Hint |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Systematic (Hard) Fault** | Permanent, repeatable deviation; component is broken. | Machine fails every cycle in the same way. | Replace after isolation. |
| **Random (Soft) Fault** | Non-deterministic; depends on load, temperature, or timing. | Occasional rejects, sporadic alarms. | Statistical / vibration / thermal analysis. |
| **Intermittent Fault** | Appears, disappears, reappears; sensitive to vibration, humidity, or contact wear. | Fault returns every few hours/days. | Thermal cycling, tap testing, soak test. |
| **Incipient Fault** | Slowly developing; not yet an outright failure. | Gradual drift in accuracy, creep in pressure. | Trend analysis, predictive maintenance. |
| **Catastrophic Fault** | Sudden, severe, often damages adjacent parts. | Sheared shaft, burst hose, short circuit. | Emergency stop, root-cause analysis post-repair. |

## 1.4 The "Five Golden Rules" of Theoretical Troubleshooting

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 examiner rewards answers that demonstrate **methodical discipline**, not guesswork. Memorize these five rules:
> 1. **Rule of Confirmation:** Verify the fault *exists* before chasing it.
> 2. **Rule of Single-Variable Change:** Change only one thing at a time.
> 3. **Rule of Half-Splitting:** Bisect the system to find the midpoint of the fault.
> 4. **Rule of Evidence:** A measurement is stronger than a hypothesis.
> 5. **Rule of Documentation:** If it is not written down, it never happened.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Strategy Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven-Step Generic Troubleshooting Framework

The KTU 2024 syllabus treats troubleshooting as a **closed-loop engineering process**, not a one-shot activity. The universally accepted seven-step model is:

1. **Symptom Recognition** — Operator/operator-HMI records deviation.
2. **Symptom Verification** — Reproduce the fault under controlled conditions.
3. **Fault Localization** — Narrow down to subsystem (electrical / hydraulic / mechanical / control).
4. **Cause Identification** — Determine *why* the localized component failed.
5. **Cause Remedy** — Repair, replace, recalibrate, or redesign.
6. **System Verification** — Run multiple cycles to confirm restoration.
7. **Root-Cause Closure** — Update SOPs, FMEA sheets, and preventive schedules.

> [!NOTE]
> Steps 1–3 are **diagnostic**, Step 4 is **analytical**, and Steps 5–7 are **corrective + preventive**. KTU exam questions frequently test the student's ability to *sequence* these correctly.

## 2.2 The Six Classical Troubleshooting Strategies

These are the **theoretical strategies** examined in Module 4. Each is suited to a different fault profile.

### Strategy A — Top-Down Approach
- Start from the system-level output (e.g., finished part quality) and progress inward toward components.
- **Best for:** Quality-related faults where the symptom is a defective workpiece.
- **Logic flow:** Finished part → machine station → station sub-assembly → component.

### Strategy B — Bottom-Up Approach
- Start from the power/sensor source and work outward toward the output.
- **Best for:** Power-supply, air-supply, or sensor-loss faults.
- **Logic flow:** Power/utility → driver → controller → actuator → output.

### Strategy C — Divide and Conquer (Half-Splitting)
- Bisect the signal/power path. Test the midpoint. If good, fault is downstream; if bad, fault is upstream.
- **Best for:** Long serial chains (cable harnesses, hydraulic lines, ladder logic rungs).
- **Mathematical basis:** Reduces $N$ possible failure points to $\log_2 N$ tests.

### Strategy D — Follow the Path (Signal-Flow Tracing)
- Trace the energy/signal from input to output using a schematic.
- **Best for:** PLC ladder logic, hydraulic circuit diagrams, and pneumatic schematics.

### Strategy E — Comparison / Swapping Method
- Compare a faulty station with an identical, known-good station; swap modules to confirm.
- **Best for:** Modular SPMs with duplicate stations (e.g., multi-spindle indexing tables).

### Strategy F — Substitution by Specification
- Replace the suspect component with a known-good spare; observe the change.
- **Best for:** When no diagnostics are available (e.g., black-box PCBAs).

## 2.3 Root-Cause Analysis (RCA) — The Analytical Core

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symptom fix ≠ Root-cause fix.** KTU examiners explicitly test the difference. Replacing a tripped MCB without investigating why it tripped scores **low marks**; the same action accompanied by a 5-Why or Ishikawa analysis scores **high marks**.

### 2.3.1 The 5-Why Analysis (Sakichi Toyoda Method)
- Ask "Why?" iteratively, typically **five times**, until the systemic cause is reached.
- *Example:* Reject → Why? → Dimension out of tolerance → Why? → Slide worn → Why? → Lubrication missed → Why? → PM schedule ignored → Why? → No digital log system. **Root cause: process, not part.**

### 2.3.2 Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram
- Categorizes potential causes into the classical **6 Ms**:
  - **Man (Personnel)** — skill, training, fatigue
  - **Machine (Equipment)** — wear, calibration, design
  - **Material** — batch variation, storage
  - **Method (Process)** — sequence, parameters
  - **Measurement** — gauge, sensor drift
  - **Mother Nature (Environment)** — temperature, humidity, dust
- Used when the cause is **multi-factorial** (very common in SPMs).

### 2.3.3 Pareto Analysis (80/20 Rule)
- $80\%$ of faults originate from $20\%$ of causes.
- Plot frequency of fault types; address the tallest bar first.
- **Mathematical form:** $C_{\text{vital-few}} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i \geq 0.80 \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{N} f_j$ where $k \ll N$.

### 2.3.4 Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) — Boolean Decomposition
- Top-down deductive analysis using **AND/OR gates**.
- The top event (system failure) is decomposed into basic events.
- Uses Boolean algebra: $T = A \cup B$ (OR gate) and $T = A \cap B$ (AND gate).
- Cut-set: minimal combination of basic events that triggers the top event.

### 2.3.5 FMEA — Failure Mode and Effects Analysis
- **RPN (Risk Priority Number):**

$$RPN = S \times O \times D$$

where $S$ = Severity (1–10), $O$ = Occurrence (1–10), $D$ = Detection difficulty (1–10). A high RPN indicates a priority troubleshooting target.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Strategy Cheat Sheet

| Technique | Best Fault Type | Mathematical Basis | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top-Down | Quality / output defects | Hierarchical decomposition | **Very High** |
| Bottom-Up | Power / utility loss | Reverse hierarchical | High |
| Divide & Conquer | Long serial chains | $\log_2 N$ efficiency | **Very High** |
| 5-Why | Single-cause systemic | Iterative questioning | High |
| Ishikawa | Multi-cause | Categorical (6 Ms) | **Very High** |
| Pareto | Resource prioritization | 80/20 cumulative frequency | Medium |
| FTA | Safety-critical systems | Boolean $\cup / \cap$ | Medium |
| FMEA | Proactive engineering | $RPN = S \cdot O \cdot D$ | **Very High** |
| Trend Analysis | Incipient faults | Time-series drift | Medium |
| Swapping | Modular systems | Empirical comparison | High |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

These strategies are directly applied in:
- **Automotive assembly SPMs** (robotic weld cells) — FMEA + FTA dominate.
- **Pharmaceutical packaging lines** — 5-Why + Pareto for GMP compliance.
- **Hydraulic press shops** — Bottom-up + half-splitting for pressure faults.
- **Semiconductor pick-and-place** — Top-down + statistical trend analysis for sub-micron accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Frameworks & Derivations

> Since this topic is **theoretical-procedural** rather than purely computational, this section treats the major analytical frameworks (FMEA, FTA, Half-Splitting decision logic) as step-by-step derivations of *decision pathways* and *quantitative metrics* expected in KTU 14-mark answers.

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: FMEA Risk Priority Number (RPN)

The RPN is the **single most-tested formula** in Module 4. KTU examiners expect the student to compute RPN, interpret it, and propose a corrective action.

### Step 1 — Define the System Boundary
A Special Purpose Machine is broken into functional sub-systems: clamping, indexing, drilling, lubrication, and ejection. Each sub-system is a row in the FMEA worksheet.

### Step 2 — Identify Failure Modes
For each sub-system, list *how* it can fail.
*Example:* For the hydraulic clamping sub-system, failure modes include: "pressure drop," "seal leakage," and "valve coil burnout."

### Step 3 — Score Severity (S)
Severity is rated 1–10, where **10 = catastrophic (safety hazard, total stoppage)** and **1 = negligible (no functional impact)**.

### Step 4 — Score Occurrence (O)
Occurrence is rated 1–10, where **10 = very frequent (multiple times per shift)** and **1 = remote (once per year or less)**.

### Step 5 — Score Detection (D)
Detection difficulty is rated 1–10, where **10 = no detection method exists** and **1 = detected 100% of the time before reaching customer**.

### Step 6 — Compute RPN
Using the master equation:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

### Step 7 — Triage the RPN
A typical industrial threshold is:

$$
RPN_{\text{action}} \geq 100 \quad \text{(action mandatory)}
$$
$$
50 \leq RPN < 100 \quad \text{(review and monitor)}
$$
$$
RPN < 50 \quad \text {(accept with documentation)}
$$

### Step 8 — Re-compute After Action
After corrective action, new $S'$, $O'$, $D'$ are scored and $RPN' = S' \cdot O' \cdot D'$. The percentage reduction is reported:

$$
\Delta RPN\,\% = \frac{RPN - RPN'}{RPN} \times 100
$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)
*Consider an SPM drilling station. A drill breakage failure mode has $S = 8$ (downstream scrap), $O = 5$ (once per week), $D = 7$ (detected only by post-machining inspection).*

$$
RPN = 8 \times 5 \times 7 = 280
$$

This is **far above the 100 threshold** — action mandatory.

*After installing a real-time spindle current monitor, the new scores are $S' = 8$ (severity unchanged, scrap still occurs if missed), $O' = 5$ (occurrence unchanged), $D' = 2$ (current spike detected in 0.1 s).*

$$
RPN' = 8 \times 5 \times 2 = 80
$$

$$
\Delta RPN\,\% = \frac{280 - 80}{280} \times 100 \approx 71.4\,\%
$$

**Conclusion:** The corrective action is justified and effective.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Half-Splitting (Divide and Conquer) Efficiency

This derivation shows *why* half-splitting is mathematically superior to linear search — a favorite KTU theory question.

### Step 1 — Define N
Let $N$ be the total number of test points in a serial chain (e.g., sensors in a line, rungs in a ladder program).

### Step 2 — Linear Search Cost
A linear (left-to-right) search requires in the worst case:

$$
T_{\text{linear}} = N \text{ tests}
$$

### Step 3 — Half-Splitting Search Cost
At each step, the search space is halved. After $k$ steps, the search space is $N / 2^{k}$. We stop when the space is 1:

$$
\frac{N}{2^{k}} = 1
$$

Solving for $k$:

$$
2^{k} = N
$$

$$
k = \log_{2} N
$$

### Step 4 — Worst-Case Test Count

$$
T_{\text{half-split}} = \lceil \log_{2} N \rceil
$$

### Step 5 — Efficiency Comparison
For $N = 1024$ sensors, linear search needs **1024 tests**, half-splitting needs only:

$$
T_{\text{half-split}} = \log_{2} 1024 = 10 \text{ tests}
$$

This is a **99\% reduction in diagnostic time** — a critical metric in production environments where downtime cost is typically ₹5,000–₹50,000 per hour.

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Fault Tree Analysis (Boolean Cut-Set)

### Step 1 — Define the Top Event
*Top Event (T):* "SPM drilling station produces out-of-tolerance holes."

### Step 2 — Decompose with Gates
Suppose $T$ occurs if **either** the drill is dull **OR** the workpiece is misclamped **OR** the spindle has bearing play.

$$
T = A \cup B \cup C
$$

Now further decompose $C$ (bearing play) as an AND of two sub-causes: $C = C_1 \cap C_2$ where $C_1$ = lubrication failure, $C_2$ = bearing wear.

The full Boolean expression becomes:

$$
T = A \cup B \cup (C_1 \cap C_2)
$$

### Step 3 — Identify Minimal Cut Sets
A *minimal cut set* is the smallest combination of basic events whose simultaneous occurrence triggers $T$. Here the minimal cut sets are:

- $\{A\}$ — single point failure
- $\{B\}$ — single point failure
- $\{C_1, C_2\}$ — dual-point failure (requires both lubrication failure **and** bearing wear)

### Step 4 — Compute Top-Event Probability (assuming independence)
Using the inclusion–exclusion principle for independent events:

$$
P(T) = 1 - P(\overline{A}) \cdot P(\overline{B}) \cdot P(\overline{C_1} \cup \overline{C_2})
$$

$$
P(\overline{C_1} \cup \overline{C_2}) = 1 - P(C_1 \cap C_2) = 1 - P(C_1) \cdot P(C_2)
$$

Substituting back:

$$
P(T) = 1 - [1-P(A)][1-P(B)]\,[1-P(C_1)P(C_2)]
$$

**Numerical example:** $P(A) = 0.01$, $P(B) = 0.02$, $P(C_1) = 0.05$, $P(C_2) = 0.05$.

$$
P(C_1 \cap C_2) = 0.05 \times 0.05 = 0.0025
$$

$$
P(\overline{A} \cap \overline{B} \cap \overline{C_1 \cap C_2}) = 0.99 \times 0.98 \times (1 - 0.0025)
$$

$$
= 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.9975 \approx 0.9677
$$

$$
P(T) = 1 - 0.9677 \approx 0.0323 \text{ (i.e., 3.23\%)}
$$

**Interpretation:** Single-point failures ($A$, $B$) dominate the risk; the dual-cause path is negligible. Engineering effort should focus on improving $A$ and $B$ first — a textbook Pareto/FTA outcome.

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: 5-Why Closure Logic

The 5-Why is not random questioning; it has a *depth-of-causation* test.

### Step 1 — Start with the Symptom
"Drill breakage on station 3."

### Step 2 — Ask Why #1
*Why did the drill break?* → Because it experienced excessive cutting force.

### Step 3 — Why #2
*Why was cutting force excessive?* → Because feed rate was set too high for the workpiece material.

### Step 4 — Why #3
*Why was feed rate set incorrectly?* → Because the operator selected the wrong program from the HMI.

### Step 5 — Why #4
*Why did the operator select the wrong program?* → Because two programs had similar names ("DRILL_45A" vs "DRILL_45B") and there was no confirmation prompt.

### Step 6 — Why #5
*Why was the naming convention ambiguous?* → Because no naming standard was enforced during PLC programming.

### Step 7 — Closure Test
The 5-Why chain has reached a *systemic* cause (programming SOP) rather than a *technical* cause (drill quality). At this point, the corrective action targets the **process**, not the **part**. The chain is closed.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Strategy

| Observed Symptom | First-Check Strategy | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| No power to machine | Bottom-up | Start at utility/breaker. |
| Single part dimension wrong | Top-down | Output symptom → station. |
| Multiple random alarms | Divide & conquer | Many serial I/O points. |
| Pressure drop in hydraulics | Follow the path | Trace schematic from pump to actuator. |
| Slow degradation over weeks | Trend analysis | Incipient fault. |
| Identical twin station works | Swapping | Eliminate module-level doubt. |
| Safety-circuit intermittent | FTA + 5-Why | Must find systemic root cause. |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Seven-Step Generic Troubleshooting Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Operator Observes Deviation] --> A1{Is the fault reproducible?}
    A1 -- No --> A2[Run multiple cycles and log sensors]
    A2 --> A1
    A1 -- Yes --> A3[Define the symptom boundary]
    A3 --> A4[Choose strategy: Top-down / Bottom-up / Half-split / Follow-path / Swap]
    A4 --> A5[Isolate suspect subsystem]
    A5 --> A6{Is the fault localized?}
    A6 -- No --> A4
    A6 -- Yes --> A7[Identify root cause via 5-Why or Ishikawa]
    A7 --> A8[Apply corrective action]
    A8 --> A9[Run verification cycles]
    A9 --> A10{Fault cleared and no regression?}
    A10 -- No --> A7
    A10 -- Yes --> A11[Update FMEA, SOP, PM schedule]
    A11 --> A12[Document and close]
```

## 4.2 Top-Down vs Bottom-Up Strategy — Comparative Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TOPD[Top-Down Approach]
        TD1[Finished Part Quality] --> TD2[Station Output]
        TD2 --> TD3[Sub-Assembly]
        TD3 --> TD4[Component Level]
    end
    subgraph BOTU[Bottom-Up Approach]
        BU1[Utility Supply Power] --> BU2[Drive Electronics]
        BU2 --> BU3[Controller PLC]
        BU3 --> BU4[Actuator Output]
    end
    style TOPD fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style BOTU fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

## 4.3 Half-Splitting (Divide and Conquer) Search Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Serial Chain of N Test Points] --> S1[Test midpoint at position N/2]
    S1 --> Q1{Is midpoint OK?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> S2[Fault is downstream — test N/2 + N/4]
    Q1 -- No --> S3[Fault is upstream — test N/4]
    S2 --> S4[Repeat until isolated]
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> S5[Pinpoint single failure point]
    style S0 fill:#e6f3ff
    style S5 fill:#d4f4dd
```

## 4.4 Ishikawa (Fishbone) Cause-Category Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HEAD[SPM Fault Symptom] --> M1[Man: Operator Skill]
    HEAD --> M2[Machine: Wear, Calibration]
    HEAD --> M3[Material: Batch Variation]
    HEAD --> M4[Method: Process Sequence]
    HEAD --> M5[Measurement: Sensor Drift]
    HEAD --> M6[Environment: Temperature, Dust]
    style HEAD fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style M1 fill:#e6e6e6
    style M2 fill:#e6e6e6
    style M3 fill:#e6e6e6
    style M4 fill:#e6e6e6
    style M5 fill:#e6e6e6
    style M6 fill:#e6e6e6
```

## 4.5 FMEA Worksheet Functional Block Layout

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Subsystem: e.g., Hydraulic Clamp] --> B[List Failure Modes]
    B --> C[Score S: 1 to 10]
    B --> D[Score O: 1 to 10]
    B --> E[Score D: 1 to 10]
    C --> F[Compute RPN = S x O x D]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{RPN greater than 100?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Action Mandatory]
    G -- No --> I[Review and Monitor]
    H --> J[Re-score after correction to get new RPN]
    style F fill:#fff2cc
    style H fill:#ffcccc
    style I fill:#d4f4dd
```

## 4.6 Fault Tree Analysis — Boolean Gate Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[Top Event: SPM Drill Out-of-Tolerance] --> OR1
    OR1{{OR Gate}} --> A[A: Drill Dull]
    OR1 --> B[B: Workpiece Misclamped]
    OR1 --> AND1
    AND1{{AND Gate}} --> C1[C1: Lubrication Failed]
    AND1 --> C2[C2: Bearing Worn]
    style TOP fill:#ff9999
    style OR1 fill:#ffe6e6
    style AND1 fill:#e6e6ff
```

## 4.7 Strategy Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Detected Fault] --> Q1{Is the fault reproducible?}
    Q1 -- No --> R1[Use Trend Analysis or Statistical Monitoring]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is there a duplicate station?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> R2[Use Swapping Method]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is the fault symptom at output or input?}
    Q3 -- Output Quality --> R3[Use Top-Down Strategy]
    Q3 -- Input Power/Utility --> R4[Use Bottom-Up Strategy]
    Q3 -- Long Serial Chain --> R5[Use Half-Splitting]
    Q3 -- Schematic Available --> R6[Use Follow-the-Path]
    style START fill:#cce5ff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between systematic and random faults in a Special Purpose Machine. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A *systematic fault* is a permanent, repeatable deviation caused by a hard failure of a component; it appears every cycle in the same way. **Example:** A burned-out solenoid coil in a hydraulic valve — the clamping station will fail to clamp on every cycle.

A *random fault* is non-deterministic, depends on operating conditions (load, temperature, timing, humidity), and does not repeat consistently. **Example:** An intermittent loose connector in a proximity-sensor cable that occasionally loses signal during machine vibration.

Key distinction: systematic faults are **deterministic and repair-by-replacement**; random faults require **statistical monitoring and environmental correlation** to diagnose.

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Risk Priority Number (RPN) used in FMEA. State its mathematical formula and the typical action threshold.**

**Model Answer:**
The RPN is a quantitative metric used in Failure Mode and Effects Analysis to prioritize failure modes for corrective action.

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

where $S$ is Severity (1–10), $O$ is Occurrence (1–10), and $D$ is Detection difficulty (1–10).

**Typical action threshold:** $RPN \geq 100$ mandates immediate corrective action; $50 \leq RPN < 100$ requires review and monitoring; $RPN < 50$ is accepted with documentation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **seven-step generic troubleshooting methodology** applied to Special Purpose Machines. For each step, mention the key deliverable a troubleshooter produces. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain the **5-Why analysis** and the **Ishikawa fishbone diagram**. State one situation where each is most appropriate. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Seven-Step Troubleshooting Methodology**

1. **Symptom Recognition** — Operator/HMI logs the deviation (defect, alarm, abnormal noise). *Deliverable:* Fault log entry with timestamp and operating context.
2. **Symptom Verification** — Reproduce the fault under controlled conditions. *Deliverable:* Confirmed fault signature.
3. **Fault Localization** — Narrow the fault to a sub-system (electrical / hydraulic / mechanical / control). *Deliverable:* Identified suspect zone.
4. **Cause Identification** — Determine the *why* using 5-Why, Ishikawa, or FTA. *Deliverable:* Root-cause statement.
5. **Cause Remedy** — Repair, replace, recalibrate, or redesign. *Deliverable:* Work-order with parts list.
6. **System Verification** — Run multiple cycles to confirm restoration. *Deliverable:* Verification report with pass/fail data.
7. **Root-Cause Closure** — Update SOPs, FMEA, PM schedules, and training. *Deliverable:* Updated documentation.

**(b) 5-Why and Ishikawa Diagram**

**5-Why Analysis** is an iterative questioning technique that drills down from a symptom to its systemic root cause by asking "Why?" typically five times. *Example (drill breakage):* Drill breaks → Why? → Excessive force → Why? → Wrong feed rate → Why? → Wrong program selected → Why? → Ambiguous program name → Why? → No naming SOP. **Most appropriate when:** a single dominant cause is suspected and the chain of causation is linear.

**Ishikawa (Fishbone) Diagram** categorizes potential causes under the 6 Ms (Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature). It is best drawn as a horizontal "spine" with branches for each category. *Example application:* A chronic dimensional drift in a milling station — the fishbone helps explore whether the cause is operator skill, tool wear, material hardness, cutting parameters, gauge calibration, or shop temperature. **Most appropriate when:** the fault is multi-factorial and the team must brainstorm collectively.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** methodology. Using a Boolean expression, construct a simple fault tree for the top event "SPM drilling station fails to produce a hole," and identify the minimal cut sets. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **RPN** for a clamping sub-system failure mode "pressure drop" with the following scores: $S = 9$, $O = 4$, $D = 6$. After installing a real-time pressure transducer with auto-cutoff, the new scores are $S' = 9$, $O' = 2$, $D' = 1$. Compute the percentage reduction in RPN and comment on the effectiveness of the corrective action. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)**

FTA is a top-down deductive analysis that decomposes a system-level failure (top event) into basic events using AND/OR gates.

*Top Event (T):* "SPM drilling station fails to produce a hole."

Decomposition:
- $T$ occurs if **either** the spindle does not rotate **OR** the drill does not feed **OR** the workpiece is not clamped.
- Boolean expression: $T = A \cup B \cup C$
- Further decomposition: $A$ (no rotation) requires $A_1$ (motor failure) **AND** $A_2$ (power supply failure): $A = A_1 \cap A_2$
- $B$ (no feed) requires $B_1$ (servo fault) **AND** $B_2$ (ballscrew wear): $B = B_1 \cap B_2$

Full expression:

$$
T = (A_1 \cap A_2) \cup (B_1 \cap B_2) \cup C
$$

**Minimal cut sets:**
- $\{A_1, A_2\}$ — both motor and power must fail.
- $\{B_1, B_2\}$ — both servo and ballscrew must fail.
- $\{C\}$ — single-point failure of clamping.

The single-point cut set $\{C\}$ is the most critical because it has order 1, meaning a single failure triggers $T$.

**(b) RPN Computation**

Initial RPN:

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D = 9 \times 4 \times 6 = 216
$$

Post-correction RPN:

$$
RPN' = S' \times O' \times D' = 9 \times 2 \times 1 = 18
$$

Percentage reduction:

$$
\Delta RPN\,\% = \frac{216 - 18}{216} \times 100 = \frac{198}{216} \times 100 \approx 91.67\,\%
$$

**Comment:** The corrective action (real-time pressure transducer with auto-cutoff) is **highly effective**, reducing the RPN by approximately **91.7%**, well below the action threshold of 100. The detection difficulty dropped from 6 to 1, and occurrence halved due to auto-cutoff preventing repeated events. The severity remains at 9 because a pressure drop, if undetected, still causes scrap — but the likelihood of *reaching* that state is now negligible.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing strategy with tool.** "Using a multimeter" is a *tool*; "Top-Down strategy" is a *strategy*. Examiners want the **strategy** named and justified.
> 2. **Skipping the verification step.** Students often jump from fault detection straight to repair, omitting the verification cycle — losing 2 marks.
> 3. **RPN without interpretation.** Computing 216 is not enough; you **must** state the threshold (100), classify the risk, and recommend action.
> 4. **5-Why chains that stop at a technical cause.** Stopping at "the bearing failed" is incomplete. A *systemic* cause (e.g., "no PM schedule") is the expected endpoint.
> 5. **Fault tree without minimal cut sets.** Drawing the gates is only half the answer; **identifying the minimal cut sets** is what the question awards marks for.
> 6. **Ignoring documentation.** Every KTU 14-mark answer should end with a note on **documentation update** — this is a recurring examiner pet peeve.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 4 — Theoretical Troubleshooting**

- **Definition:** Troubleshooting = systematic process of fault recognition → isolation → rectification → verification → closure.
- **Fault Types:** Systematic, Random, Intermittent, Incipient, Catastrophic — know the difference.
- **Seven Steps:** Symptom Recognition → Verification → Localization → Cause ID → Remedy → Verification → Closure.
- **Six Strategies:** Top-Down, Bottom-Up, Divide & Conquer, Follow-the-Path, Swapping, Substitution.
- **Half-Splitting Efficiency:** Worst-case tests = $\lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ (vs $N$ for linear search).
- **5-Why:** Iterative questioning to reach *systemic* (not technical) root cause.
- **Ishikawa 6 Ms:** Man, Machine, Material, Method, Measurement, Mother Nature.
- **Pareto:** 80% of faults come from 20% of causes.
- **FTA:** Boolean decomposition with AND/OR gates; minimal cut sets identify critical paths.
- **FMEA Master Formula:**

$$
RPN = S \times O \times D
$$

- **RPN Thresholds:** $\geq 100$ action mandatory; $50$–$100$ review; $< 50$ accept.
- **Single-Point vs Dual-Point Failure:** Single-point cut sets (order 1) are most critical.
- **Golden Rules:** Verify fault exists, change one variable, half-split, measure not guess, document everything.
- **Preventive vs Corrective:** Symptom fix ≠ root-cause fix. KTU always rewards the latter.
